question: A solenoid of length 50 $cm$ having 100 turns carries a current of 2.5 $A$ . The magnetic field at one end of the solenoid is
explanation:
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}nI}}{2} = \left[ \begin{gathered}4\pi \times {10^{ - 7}} \times (100/0.50) \hfill \\ \times 2.5 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]/2$
$ \approx 3.14 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,T$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Solenoid

question: A long cylindrical wire of radius $R$ carries a uniform current $I$ flowing through it. The variation of magnetic field with distance $r$ from the axis of the wire is shown by
explanation:
For a long cylindrical wire of radius $R$ and current $I$.
${B_{{\rm{outside }}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{2\pi }}\frac{I}{r} \Rightarrow \,B \propto \frac{1}{r}\,$
${B_{{\rm{inside }}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{2\pi }}\frac{{Ir}}{{{R^2}}} \Rightarrow \,B \propto r$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to Infinitely Long Hollow and Solid Cylindrical Current Carrying Wires

question: A long straight rod of diameter $4\,mm$ carries a steady current ' $i$ ' . The current is uniformly distributed across its cross section. The ratio of the magnetic fields at distances $1\,mm$ and $4\,mm$ from the axis of the rod is
explanation:
$R = 2\,mm,B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2\pi {R^2}}}.r(r \le R)$
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}(r \geq R)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952786/preptracker/questions/q_111308_explanation_1764952786510.png At ${r_1} = 1\,mm,{B_1} = \left( {\frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2\pi {R^2}}}} \right){r_1}$
$\therefore B_{1} \propto \dfrac{r_{1}}{R^{2}}$
At $r_{2}=4 m m, B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r_{2}} \Rightarrow B_{2} \alpha \dfrac{1}{r_{2}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=\dfrac{r_{1} / R^{2}}{1 / r_{2}}=\dfrac{r_{1} r_{2}}{R^{2}}=\dfrac{1 \times 4}{(2)^{2}}=1: 1$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to Infinitely Long Hollow and Solid Cylindrical Current Carrying Wires

question: Match the items in Column I related to solenoids and amperian loop with their appropriate descriptions in Column II. Choose the correct code from the options given below:
explanation:
A: A long solenoid implies that the solenoid's length is significantly larger than its radius, which leads to the magnetic field outside the solenoid approaching zero due to the cancellation of the fields from adjacent turns (Q).
B: Enameled wires are used in the construction of a solenoid to insulate the turns from each other, ensuring that the electric current flows through the entire length of the wire without short-circuiting between turns (R).
C : An Amperian loop is a hypothetical loop used in Ampere's circuital law to relate the magnetic field inside a solenoid to the current passing through it. In the case of the solenoid, a rectangular loop is considered to determine the magnetic field (S).
D: The magnetic field inside a very long solenoid is uniform and everywhere parallel to the axis of the solenoid, which is a direct consequence of the symmetry and the nature of the winding in a solenoid (P). Correct option is (4)

options.text: A - R,        	B - S, 	C - P,  	D - Q
A - Q, 	B - P,	C - S, 	D - R
A - P, 	B - Q,	C - R, 	D - S

question: A closely wound solenoid of  $80{\text{ }}cm$ long has 5 layers of windings of 400 turns each. The diameter of the solenoid is  $18{\text{ }}cm.$   If it carries a current of $8 A$, then the magnitude of the magnetic field inside the solenoid near its centre is approximately
explanation:
Given Length of solenoid,
$l=80 {~cm}=0.8 {~m}, i=8 {~A}$
Total number of turns,
$N=$ No. of layers $\times$ No. of turns in each layer
$=5 \times 400=2000$
$\therefore$ No. of turns per unit length,
$=\dfrac{N}{l}=\dfrac{2000}{0.8}=2500$ turns $/ {m}$
$B=\mu_{0} n i$
$=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2500 \times 8=2.5 \times 10^{-2} T$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Solenoid

question: A toroid has a core (non-ferro magnetic) of inner radius  $24{\text{ }}cm$  and outer radius  $26{\text{ }}cm$  around which 2000 turns of a wire is wound. If the current in the wire is $12 A$, the magnetic field inside the core of the toroid is
explanation:
Given, inner radius of toroid,
$r_{1}=24 {~cm}=0.24 {~m}$,
Outer radius, $r_{2}=26 {~cm}=0.26 {~m}$
Current, $i=12 A, N=2000$ turns Magnetic field inside the core of toroid,
$B=\left(\dfrac{\mu_{0} N i}{l}\right)$
where, $l=2 \pi\left(\dfrac{r_{1}+r_{2}}{2}\right)$
$=2 \pi\left(\dfrac{0.24+0.26}{2}\right)=2 \pi \times 0.25=0.5 \pi$
$\therefore B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N i}{0.5 \pi}=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2000 \times 12}{0.5 \pi}$
$=1.92 \times 10^{-2} T$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Toroid

question: A long solenoid has  70 turns  $/cm$  and carries current $i$. An electron moves within the solenoid in a circle of radius  $2.5{\text{ }}cm$  perpendicular to the solenoid axis. If the speed of the electron is $4.4 \times 10^{6} {~m} / {s}$, then the current in the solenoid is(take $\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}$ SI unit, mass electron$9 \times 10^{-31} {~kg}$, charge of electron $1.6 \times 10^{-19} {C}$ )
explanation:
Number of turns per unit length of solenoid, $n=70$ turns $/ {cm}=7000$ turns $/ {m}$
Since, electron is moving inside the solenoid in circular path of radius $r$, then $r=\dfrac{m v}{B e}$
Magnetic field inside the solenoid ,
$B = {\mu _0}ni\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
$ \Rightarrow r = \frac{{mv}}{{{\mu _0}nie}} \Rightarrow i = \frac{{mv}}{{{\mu _0}nre}}$ [From Eq. (1)]
Here, $m=9.1 \times 10^{-31} {~kg}, v=4.4 \times 10^{6} {~m} / {s}$
$e=1.6 \times 10^{-19} {C}$
$\Rightarrow \mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}$ SI unit
$r=2.5 {~cm}=2.5 \times 10^{-2} {~m}$
Putting these values in the above equation, we get
$i=\dfrac{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 4.4 \times 10^{6}}{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 7000 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$
$=0.1125 {~A}=112.5 {~mA}$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Solenoid

question: A long solenoid with  10 turn $/cm$  and a radius of  $8{\text{ }}cm$  carries a current of   $7{\text{ }}mA.$   A current carrying straight conductor is located along the central axis of the solenoid. If the direction of resulting magnetic field is $60^{\circ}$ to the axial direction at a point  $5{\text{ }}cm$  from the axis of the solenoid along the radial direction, then the current in the conductor is   [take, $\sqrt{2}=1.4, \sqrt{3}=1.7]$
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952774/preptracker/questions/q_111233_explanation_1764952774358.png We have to find the magnetic field at $P$ at a distance $5\,cm$ from wire.
At $P$, two perpendicular fields exists. $B_{1}$ is magnetic field due to solenoid and $B_{2}$ is magnetic field of wire.
$B_{2}$ (Perpendicular to axis of solenoid) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952775/preptracker/questions/q_111233_explanation_1764952774943.png Now, $B_{1}=\mu_{0} n i_{1}$
$=\mu_{0}(1000) \times 7 \times 10^{-3}=7 \mu_{0} T$
And $B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i_{2}}{2 \pi d}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i_{2}}{2 \pi \times 5 \times 10^{-2}}=\dfrac{10 \mu_{0} i_{2}}{\pi} T$
As, $B_{1}=B \cos \theta$
and $B_{2}=B \sin \theta$
$\Rightarrow \tan \theta=\dfrac{B_{2}}{B_{1}} \Rightarrow B_{2}=B_{1} \tan 60^{\circ}$
(Given, $\theta=60^{\circ}$ )
Substituting values of $B_{2}$ and $B_{1}$, we get
$\dfrac{10 \mu_{0} i_{2}}{\pi}=7 \mu_{0} \times \sqrt{3}$
$\Rightarrow \quad i_{2}=\dfrac{7 \times \pi \times \sqrt{3}}{10} {~A} \Rightarrow I_{2}=3.74 {~A}$
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Solenoid

question: A toroid core has inner radius of $0.24 m$ and outer radius of  $0.26{\text{ }}m.$   A current of  $10{\text{ }}A$  flows through the wire having 2500 turns around it. Find the magnetic field inside the core of the toroid
explanation:
Given, inner radius and outer radius of toroid,
$r_{1}=0.24 m, r_{2}=0.26 m$
Current, $i=10 {~A}$
Number of turns, $N=2500$
Number of turns per unit length of toroid
$n=\dfrac{N}{2 \pi\left(\dfrac{r_{1}+r_{2}}{2}\right)}=\dfrac{2500}{2 \pi\left(\dfrac{0.24+0.26}{2}\right)}$
$=\dfrac{2500}{2 \pi \times 0.25}=\dfrac{1 \times 10^{4}}{2 \pi}$
$\therefore$ Magnetic field inside the core of toroid,
$B=\mu_{0} n i=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{1 \times 10^{4}}{2 \pi} \times 10$
$=2 \times 10^{-2} T$
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Toroid

question: If the number of turns per unit length of a solenoid is doubled, what happens to the magnetic field in the solenoid?
explanation:
Given, initial and final number of turns per unit length be $n_{i}$ and $n_{f}$. i. e.
$n_{i}=2 n_{f}$ and
As we know that, in case of solenoid
$B=n \mu_{0} I$
Here, $B \propto n$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{B_{i}}{B_{f}}=\dfrac{n_{i}}{n_{f}}$
$\left[B_{i}, B_{f} \rightarrow\right. $ initial and final magnetic field]
$\Rightarrow B_{f}=B_{i} \dfrac{n_{f}}{n_{i}}=B_{i} \dfrac{2 n_{i}}{n_{i}}=2 B_{i}$
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Solenoid

question: The magnetic field (in SI units) at point $P$ of given figure due to carrying of current $i$ by a conductor of radius $R$, is
explanation:
Magnetic field due to a current carrying conductor at an outside point at a distance ' $r$ ' from central axis is given by (Ampere's law)
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 \pi r} T$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to Infinitely Long Hollow and Solid Cylindrical Current Carrying Wires

question: A  $50{\text{ }}cm$  long solenoid has winding of 400 turns. What current must pass through it to produce a magnetic field of induction     $4 \pi \times 10^{-3} T$ at the centre?
explanation:
For a solenoid,
$B=\mu_{0} n i \Rightarrow n=\dfrac{N}{i}$
Here, $B=4 \pi \times 10^{-3} T$
$n=\dfrac{400}{50 \times 10^{-2}}=\dfrac{400 \times 100}{50}$
$=800$ turns $/ {m}$
Since, $B=\mu_{0} n i$
$4 \pi \times 10^{-3}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 800 \times i$
$\Rightarrow i=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-3}}{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 800}$
$\Rightarrow \quad i=\dfrac{10000}{800}=12.5 {~A}$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Solenoid

question: The ratio of the magnetic field inside a solenoid at an axial point well inside and at an axial end point is
explanation:
The magnetic field at some internal point on the axis of a solenoid is given by
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} n i}{2}\left(\cos \theta_{1}-\cos \theta_{2}\right)$
Magnetic field at the centre of a long solenoid is given by setting $\theta_{1}=0^{\circ}$ and $\theta_{2}=\pi$.
$\Rightarrow \quad B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} n i}{2}\left(\cos 0^{\circ}-\cos \pi\right)$
$ = \frac{{{\mu _0}ni}}{2}[1 - ( - 1)] = {\mu _0}ni\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
Similarly, the magnetic field at the end of a long solenoid is given by setting $\theta_{1}=\pi / 2$ and $\theta_{2}=\pi$. $\Rightarrow B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} n i}{2}\left(\cos \dfrac{\pi}{2}-\cos \pi\right)$
$ = \frac{{{\mu _0}ni}}{2}[0 - ( - 1)] = \frac{{{\mu _0}ni}}{2}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
From Eq. (1) and Eq. (2), we get
$\dfrac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} n i}{\mu_{0} n i} \times 2=2$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Solenoid

question: A solenoid has a core of a material with relative permeability $\dfrac{800}{\pi}$. The windings of the solenoid are insulated from the core and carry a current of  $2{\text{ }}A.$  If the number of turns is 1000 per metre, find the magnitude of magnetic field $B$.
explanation:
Given, relative permeability, $\mu_{r}=\dfrac{800}{\pi}$
Current, $i=2 A, n=1000 m^{-1}$
As we know that,
$H=n i=1000 \times 2=2 \times 10^{3} {Am}^{-1}$
Since, $B=\mu H=\mu_{0} \mu_{r} H \quad\left(\because \mu=\mu_{0} \mu_{r}\right)$
$=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{800}{\pi} \times 2 \times 10^{3}$
$=0.64 T=640 m T$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Solenoid

question: A solenoid of length $2 m$ carries a current of  $20{\text{ }}A.$  The diameter of the solenoid is  $3{\text{ }}cm.$  If the magnetic field inside the solenoid is  $20{\text{ }}mT,$  then the length of wire forming the solenoid is (assume, $\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} {H} / {m}$)
explanation:
Given,
$i=20 A, l=2 m, B=20 m T=20 \times 10^{-3} T$
Diameter, $d=3 {~cm}=3 \times 10^{-2} {~m}$
$\therefore$ Radius, $r=\dfrac{3}{2} \times 10^{-2} {~m}$
If $n$ be the number of turns per unit length in the solenoid, then magnetic filed through it is given by
$\Rightarrow \quad B=\mu_{0} n i$
$\Rightarrow n=\dfrac{B}{\mu_{0} i}=\dfrac{20 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 20}=\dfrac{10^{4}}{4 \pi}$
$\therefore$ Total number of turns in the solenoid,
$N=n l=\dfrac{10^{4}}{4 \pi} \times 2$
$\Rightarrow N=\dfrac{10^{4}}{2 \pi}$
Length of wire $=N \times$ length of wire in one turn
$=N \times 2 \pi r=\dfrac{10^{4}}{2 \pi} \times 2 \pi \times \dfrac{3}{2} \times 10^{-2}$
$=1.5 \times 10^{2} {~m}=150 {~m}$
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Solenoid

question: A toroid has an iron core with an internal magnetic field of $10 \pi m T$, when the current in the winding of 1500 turns per meter is  $10\,A.$  Determine the field due to magnetisation   $({\mu _0} = 4\pi  \times {10^{ - 7}}H/{m^{ - 1}})$
explanation:
Given, internal magnetic field,
$B_{\text {net }}=10 \pi m T$ current, flowing through
winding, $i=10 {~A}$ and number of turns per unit
length, $n=1500$
Since, magnetic field of toroid,
$B_{\text {toroid }}=\mu_{0} n i$
$=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1500 \times 10=6 \pi m T$
Hence, the field due to magnetisation,
$B_{m}=B_{\text {net }}-B_{\text {toroid }}=10 \pi-6 \pi$
$\Rightarrow B_{m}=4 \pi m T$
Hence, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Toroid

question: A long wire carries a current of $18 A$ kept along the axis of a long solenoid of radius  $1\,cm.$   The magnetic field due to the solenoid is $8.0 \times 10^{-3} T$. The magnitude of the resultant field at a point  $0.6{\text{ }}mm$  from the solenoid axis is (Assume, $\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} {Tm} / {A}$)
explanation:
Given, current in a long wire, $I=18 A$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957911/preptracker/questions/q_111192_explanation_1764957911405.png magnetic field due to a solenoid,
$B_{1}=8 \times 10^{-3} T, r=0.6 {~mm}=0.6 \times 10^{-3} {~m}$ & Radius of solenoid, $R=1 {~cm}=1 \times 10^{-2} {~m}$
Magnetic field due to current carrying wire at a point, $P$
$B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i}{r}$
$=2 \times 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{18}{0.6 \times 10^{-3}}$
$=6 \times 10^{-3} T \quad$ (in upward direction)
Since, current carrying wire is placed along the axis of solenoid, hence magnetic field produced by wire and magnetic field due to solenoid at point $P$, both are perpendicular to each other, Hence, the magnitude of the resultant magnetic field,
$B=\sqrt{B_{1}^{2}+B_{2}^{2}}$
$=\sqrt{\left(8 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}+\left(6 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}}=10 \times 10^{-3} T$
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Solenoid

question: A solenoid is $1\,m$ long and $4\,cm$ in diameter. It has five layers of windings of 1000 turns each and carries a current of $7\,A$. the magnetic field at the centre of the solenoid is
explanation:
For a long solenoid, $B=\mu_{0} n I=\mu_{0} \dfrac{N}{L} I$.
For one layer,
$\Rightarrow B=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{10^{3}}{1} \times 7 . T=28 \pi \times 10^{-4} T$
For, 5 layers
B Total $=5 \times 28 \pi \times 10^{-4} T$
$=140 \pi \times 10^{-4} T=140 \times \dfrac{22}{7} \times 10^{-4} T$
$=4.4 \times 10^{-2} T$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Solenoid

question: A long straight wire of radius a carries a steady current $I$. The current is uniformly distributed across its cross-section. The ratio of the magnetic field at $\dfrac{a}{2}$ and  $2\,a$ from axis of the wire is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956503/preptracker/questions/q_43877_explanation_1764956503374.png
Using Ampere's circuital law, $\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu I_{\text {enclosed }}$
at $A, \oint \vec{B}_{A} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I_{\text {enclosed }}$
$I_{\text {enclosed }}=\dfrac{I}{\pi a^{2}} \times \pi\left(\dfrac{a}{2}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{I}{4}$
$\therefore B_{A} \times \dfrac{2 \pi a}{2}=\mu_{0} \dfrac{I}{4}$
or $B_{A}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{I}{a}$
Similarly at $B, B_{B}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{I}{a} \Rightarrow B_{A}=B_{B}$
So, $\dfrac{B_{A}}{B_{B}}=\dfrac{1}{1}=1: 1$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field by an Infinite Sized Thin Current Carrying Sheets

question: In a co-axial straight cable, the central conductor and the outer conductor carry equal currents in opposite directions. The magnetic field is zero
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950667/preptracker/questions/q_43869_explanation_1764950666808.png
Given that, the central conductor and the outer conductor carry equal currents in opposite direction, thus, $i_{1}=i_{2}$. Using Amperes circuital law,
$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}=\mu_{o} I_{\text {enclosed }}$
For any point, outside the cable,
$i_{\text {enclosed }}=0(\because$ No current out side $)$
$i.\,e.,$ $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}=0$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field by an Infinite Sized Thin Current Carrying Sheets

question: A system consists of two parallel planes carrying currents producing a uniform magnetic field of induction ${B}$ between the planes. Outside this space there is no magnetic field. The magnetic force acting per unit area of each plane is found to be ${\dfrac{B^{2}}{N \mu_{0}}}$.  Find  the value of ${N}$ is
explanation:
Let ${J}$ is the current per unit length.
$B=2\left[\dfrac{\mu_{0} J}{2}\right]=\mu_{0} J$
Magnetic field due to one plane ${=B / 2}$
Force on other plane ${F=\dfrac{B}{2}\left(J l_{1}\right) l_{2}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956475/preptracker/questions/q_43651_explanation_1764956475572.png This force is on the shaded part.
Force per unit area: ${\dfrac{F}{l_{1} l_{2}}=\dfrac{B}{2} J=\dfrac{B}{2} \dfrac{B}{\mu_{0}}=\dfrac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}}}$
Hence ${N=2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on Ampere’s Law

question: A cylindrical conductor of radius $R$ carries a current $i$. The value of magnetic field at a point which is $\dfrac{R}{4}$ distance inside from the surface is $10\,T$. The value of magnetic field at point which is $4 R$ distance outside from the surface is
explanation:
Magnetic field inside the long cylindrical conductor,
$B_{\text {in }}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{2 I r}{R^{2}}$
Magnetic field outside the cylinder at a distance $r^{\prime}$ from the axis
$B_{\text {out }}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{2 I}{r^{\prime}}$
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{B_{\text {in }}}{B_{\text {out }}}=\dfrac{r r^{\prime}}{R^{2}} ; B_{\text {out }}=B_{\text {in }} \dfrac{R^{2}}{r r^{\prime}}$
Here, ${B_{{\text{out}}}} = 10\,T,r' = R + 4R = 5R$
$r=R-\dfrac{R}{4}=\dfrac{3 R}{4}$
${B_{{\text{out}}}} = 10 \times \frac{{{R^2}}}{{\left( {\frac{{3R}}{4}} \right)(5R)}} = \frac{8}{3}\,T$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field by an Infinite Sized Thin Current Carrying Sheets

question: Two wires with currents ${2 A}$ and ${1 A}$ are enclosed in a circular loop. Another wire with current ${3 A}$ is situated outside the loop, as shown. The ${\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{I}}$ around the loop is
explanation:
According to Ampere Circuital law,
${\int B . d l=\mu_{o} I_{\text {enclosed }}}$
${=\mu_{o}(2-1)=\mu_{o}}$
[Since, current ${2 A}$ and ${1 A}$ are in the opposite direction]
subject: Physics
subtopic: Ampere’s Law

question: A long straight wire of circular cross-section (radius $a$ ) is carrying steady current $I$. The current $I$ is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. The magnetic field is
explanation:
Magnetic field due to a infinitely long straight wire is given by Ampere's circuital law
In region $r < a$
So, $B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I r}{2 \pi a^{2}}$ or $B \propto r$
In region $r>a$,
Similarly, $B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}$ or $B \propto \dfrac{1}{r}$
So, correct option is (3)

options.text: zero in the region $r < a$ and inversely proportional to $r$ in the region $r>a$.
uniform in the region $r < a$ and inversely proportional to distance $r$ from the axis, in the region $r>a$.
directly proportional to $r$ in the region $r < a$ and inversely proportional to in the region $r>a$.
inversely proportional to $r$ in the region $r < a$ and uniform throughout in the region $r>a$.

subject: Physics

question: A solenoid of 1200 turns is wound uniformly in a single layer on a glass tube $2\,m$ long and $0.2\,m$ in diameter. The magnetic intensity at the center of the solenoid when a current of $2\,A$ flows through it is
explanation:
$N=1200$, length, $l=2 m$, diameter, $d=0.2 m$
No. of turns per unit length, $n=\dfrac{N}{l}$
current, $I=2 A$
Magnetic field at the centre of solenoid is given by
$B=\mu_{0} n I$
$B=\mu_{0} \dfrac{N I}{l}$
$H=\dfrac{B}{\mu_{0}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\mu_{0}}{\mu_{0}} \times \dfrac{N I}{l}$
$H=\dfrac{N I}{l} \Rightarrow \dfrac{1200 \times 2}{2}$
$H = 1.2 \times {10^3}\,A/m$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Solenoid

question:
An electron is allowed to move with constant velocity along the axis of current carrying straight solenoid.
(A) The electron will experience magnetic force along the axis of the solenoid.
(B) The electron will not experience magnetic force.
(C) The electron will continue to move along the axis
(D) The electron will be accelerated along the axis of the solenoid.
(E) The electron will follow parabolic path inside the solenoid.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
explanation:
As we know that in current carrying long solenoid the magnetic field is only inside the solenoid and parallel to the axis. When, an electron is projected with uniform velocity along the axis of a current carrying long solenoid it will experience a force due to the magnetic field $i.e.,$ $F=q v B \sin \theta$
Here, $\theta=0^{\circ}$, so $F=0$
Hence the electron will continue to move along the axis of the solenoid.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Solenoid

question: A long solenoid is formed by winding 70 turns $c{m^{ - 1}}$. If $2.0\,A$ current flows, then the magnetic field produced inside the solenoid is  ____ . $\left( {{\mu _0} = 4\pi  \times {{10}^{ - 7}}T{m^{ - 1}}} \right)$
explanation:
Magnetic field inside a solenoid is given by,
$B=\mu_{0} n i$
Where $n=$ Number of turns per unit length
Here, $n=70\left(\right.$ turns $c{m^{ - 1}}) = 70 \times {10^2}\,{\text{turns}}/m$
$i = 2.0\,A$
$B=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 70 \times 10^{2} \times 2$
$\Rightarrow B=176 \times 10^{-4} T$
So correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Solenoid

question:
Assertion: A linear solenoid carrying current is equivalent to a bar equivalent magnet.

Reason: The magnetic field lines of both are same.
explanation: A linear solenoid carrying current generates a magnetic field similar to that of a bar magnet. The reason correctly explains this, stating that both produce similar magnetic field configurations running from one end to the other. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956377/preptracker/questions/q_42969_explanation_1764956376830.png So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: A loosely round helix made of stiff wire is suspended vertically with the lower end just touching a dish of mercury. When a current is passed through the wire, the helical wire executes oscillatory motion with the lower end jumping out of and inside of mercury.

Reason: When electric current is passed through helix, a magnetic field is produced both inside and outside the helix.
explanation:
The windings of helix carrying currents in the same direction.
$\Rightarrow$ They experience an attractive force pulling the lower end out of mercury.
$\Rightarrow$ circuit breaks
$\Rightarrow$ Current becomes 'Zero'
$\Rightarrow$ Force of attraction $=0$
$\Rightarrow$ Helix comes back to its position
$\Rightarrow$ Circuit again gets formed.This process gets repeated and so helix does 'oscilatory motion'. So correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Solenoid

question: A long solenoid has 200 turns per $cm$ and carriers a current $i$. The magnetic field at its centre is $6.28 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,Wb{\rm{/}}{m^2}$. Another long solenoid has 100 turns per $cm$ and it carriers a current $i / 3$. The value of magnetic field at its centre is nearly
explanation:
Magnetic field due to a long solenoid is given by, $B=\mu_{0} n I$
$B = 6.28 \times {10^{ - 2}}wb{\rm{/}}{m^2};n = 200 \times {10^{ - 2}}$
$6.28 \times 10^{-2}=\mu_{0} \times 200 \times 10^{-2} \times I$
Magnetic field when another solenoid has 100 turns per $cm$ and current is $\dfrac{I}{3}$, then,
$B=\mu_{0} \times 100 \times 10^{-2} \times \dfrac{I}{3}$
From equation (1) and (2),
$B = 1.05 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,Wb{\rm{/}}{m^2}$

Five very long, straight, insulated wires are closely bound together to form a small cable. Currents carried by the wires are:

question: A long solenoid has 200 turns per $cm$ and carries a current $I$. The magnetic field at its centre is $16 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,Wb\,{m^{ - 2}}$. Another long solenoid has 100 turns per $cm$ and it carries a current $I/4$. The value of the magnetic field at its centre is
explanation:
Magnetic field at the centre of a long solenoid is $B=\mu_{0} n I$ or $B \propto n I$ (as $\mu_{0}$ is constant) where, $n$ is the number of turns per unit length of solenoid.
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \dfrac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=\dfrac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}\left(\dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}\right)=\dfrac{200}{100} \times \dfrac{I}{(I / 4)}=8 \\& \therefore B_{2}=\dfrac{B_{1}}{8}=\dfrac{16 \times 10^{-2}}{8}=2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~Wb} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Three infinite long wires $P, Q$ and $R$ respectively are present. These wires are placed symmetrically inside an equilateral triangular loop as shown in the figure. Current in 3 wires are as shown in figure. If $\int_{A}^{B} \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}=4 \mu_{0}$ and $\int_{C}^{A} \vec{B} . d \vec{l}=-15 \mu_{0}$. Considering SI units, the value of $\int_{B}^{C} \bar{B} \cdot d \bar{l}$ is
explanation:
According to Ampere's law
$\begin{aligned}& \int_{A}^{B} \bar{B} \cdot d \bar{l}+\int_{B}^{C} \bar{B} \cdot d \bar{l}+\int_{C}^{A} \bar{B} \cdot d \bar{l}=\mu_{o} \\& 4 \mu_{o}+\int_{B}^{C} \bar{B} \cdot d \bar{l}+15 \mu_{o}=20 \mu_{o} \\& \int_{B}^{C} \bar{B} \cdot d \bar{l}=\mu_{o}\end{aligned}$

question: An infinitely long hollow conducting cylinder with inner radius \[R/2\] and outer radius \[R\] carries a uniform current density along its length. The magnitude of the magnetic field, \[\left| B \right|\] as a function of the radial distance \(r\) from the axis is best represented by
explanation:
Case I: $r < \dfrac{R}{2}$,
$B = 0$
Case II: $\frac{R}{2} \leqslant {\text{r < R}}$
$\oint {{\text{B}}{\text{.dl}}} {\text{ = }}{\mu _0}I$
$B2\pi r = {\mu _0}\left[ {\pi {r^2} - \pi \left( {\frac{{{R^2}}}{2}} \right)} \right]\,J$
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}J}}{{2r}}\left( {{r^2} - \frac{{{R^2}}}{4}} \right)$
$\therefore \,B,r$ follows parabolic relation
Case III: ${\text{r}} \geqslant {\text{R}}$
$\int {B.dI = } {\mu _0}I$
$B2\,\pi r = {\mu _0}\left[ {\pi {R^2} - \pi {{\left( {\frac{R}{2}} \right)}^2}} \right]J$
$B = \frac{{3{\mu _0}J{R^2}}}{{8r}}$
$ \Rightarrow B\alpha \frac{1}{r}$ Rectangle hyperbola relation.

question: A 25 $cm$ long solenoid has radius 2$cm$ and 500 total number of turns. It carries a current of 15$A$. If it is equivalent to a magnet of the same size and intensity of magnetisation $\overrightarrow M $, then $\left| {\overrightarrow M } \right|$ is :
explanation:
$M = \frac{{magnetic\,moment}}{{volume}}$
$ = \frac{{NiA}}{V}$
$ = \frac{{Ni \times \pi {r^2}}}{{\pi {r^2} \times l}} = \frac{{Ni}}{l} = \frac{{500 \times 15}}{{25 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}$
$ = 2000 \times 15 = 30 \times {10^3}$

question:
The magnetic field at the ends of a very long current carrying solenoid is one fourth of that at the centre
If the solenoid is sufficiently long, the field within it is uniform.
explanation:
The magnetic field of a solenoid is given by,
$B=\dfrac{1}{2} \mu_{o} n I[\sin \alpha+\sin \beta]$
For a very long current carrying solenoid, the magnetic field at the ends of a very long solenoid is given by:
$\alpha=90^{\circ}, \beta=0$
$B=\dfrac{1}{2} \mu_{o} n I=\dfrac{B_{\text {centre }}}{2}$
For a long solenoid field inside is constant.
So statement A is wrong and statement B is correct. Option (2) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question: The torque required to hold a small circular coil of 10 turns, area $1\,m{m^2}$ and carrying a current of $\left( {\frac{{21}}{{44}}} \right)A$ in the middle of a long solenoid of $10^{3}$ turns /$m$ carrying a current of 2.5$A$, with its axis perpendicular to the axis of the solenoid is
explanation:
Here, For small circular coil, Number of turns, $N = 10$
Area,$A = 1\,m{m^2} = 1 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,{m^2}$ Current, ${I_1} = \frac{{21}}{{44}}\,A$
For a long solenoid,
Number of turns per metre, $n = {10^3}$per $m$
Current, ${I_2} = 2.5\,A$
Magnetic field due to a long solenoid on its axis
is $\,\,\,\,B = {\mu _0}n{I_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Magnetic moment of a circular coil is
$M = NA{I_1}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)\,\,\,\,\,$
Torque, $\bar \tau = \overrightarrow M \times \overrightarrow B $
$\tau = MB\sin \theta = MB(\because \,\theta - 90^\circ )\,$ (Given))
$\tau = \left( {NA{I_1}} \right)\left( {{\mu _0}n{I_2}} \right)$ (Using (1) and (2))
$\tau=10 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{21}{44} \times 4 \times \dfrac{22}{7} \times 10^{-7}$
$\times 10^{3} \times 2.5$
$ = 1.5 \times {10^{ - 8}}\,N\,m$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Solenoid

question:
Six very long insulated copper wires are bound
together to form a cable. The currents carried by the wires are
${I_1} =  + 10\,A,{I_2} =  - 13\,A,{\rm{ }}{I_3} =  + 10\,A,{I_4}$
$ =  + 7\,A,{I_5} =  - 12\,A,{I_6} =  + 18\,A$,
The magnetic induction at a perpendicular
distance of 10$cm$ from the cable is
$\left( {{\mu _0} = 4\pi  \times {{10}^{ - 7}}\,Wb/A - m} \right)$
explanation:
Net current due to all wires
${i_{net{\rm{ }}}} = {i_1} + {i_2} + {i_3} + {i_4} + {i_5} + {i_6}$
${i_{net{\rm{ }}}} = 10 - 13 + 10 + 7 - 12 + 18 = 20\,A$
We know, magnetic field due to an infinitely long straight conductor at a perpendicular distance $r$ from it is given by
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 \pi r}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i_{n e t}}{2 \pi r}$
where $i=$ current in wire
and $r=$ perpendicular distance
$=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 20}{2 \pi \times 10 \times 10^{-2}}=4 \times 10^{-5}$
$B=40 \mu T$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Ampere’s Law

question: A toroid has 500 turns per meter length. If it carries a current of 2$A$, the magnetic energy density inside the toroid is
explanation:
Energydensity $=\dfrac{1}{2 \mu_{0}} B^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2 \mu_{0}}\left(\mu_{0} \mathrm{nI}\right)^{2}$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_{0} \mathrm{n}^{2} \mathrm{I}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 500 \times 500 \times 4 \\& =0.628 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Figure shows an amperian path $ABCDA$. Part $ABC$ is in vertical plane $PSTU$ while part $CDA$ is in horizontal plane $PQRS$. explanation:

$\begin{aligned}& \oint B d l=\mu_{o}(\text { inclosed }) \\& =\mu_{o}\left(i_{1}+i_{2}-i_{3}+i_{3}\right) \\& =\mu_{o}\left(i_{1}+i_{2}\right)\end{aligned}$

question: The system shown in figure is the set of a wire of infinite length that carries a current \['I'\] and radius ' \(R\) ' which coincides with axis of pipe of inner radius ' \(2 R\) ' outer radius ' \(3 R\) ' carrying same current ' \(i\) ' uniformly in opposite direction to current in the wire. The correct graph which represents \['B'\] dependency on \['x'\] ((\perp \operatorname{lr}\) distance from the axis of pipe) among the following is
explanation:
The cross-sectional view of the wire and pipe is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950197/preptracker/questions/q_41309_explanation_1764950197491.png From ampere's law. The magnetic field at a distance $r$ from the centre is
For $r < R$
Case I: $\oint {\overline B } .d\overline {\text{l}} = {\mu _0}\left( {\frac{I}{{\pi {R^2}}}} \right)\pi {r^2}$
$B2\pi r = \frac{{{\mu _o}I{r^2}}}{{{R^2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow B\,\alpha \,r$
Case II: For $R < r < 2R$
$\oint {\overline B } .d\overline {\text{l}} = {\mu _0}I$
$ \Rightarrow B\,2\pi \,r = {\mu _0}I$
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2\pi r}} \Rightarrow B \propto \frac{1}{r}$
Case III: For $2R,\, < r < 3R$
$\oint {\overline B } .d\overline {\text{l}} = {\mu _0}I$
$ \Rightarrow B\,2\pi \,r = {\pi _o}I$
$ = {\mu _0}\left( {I - \left( {\frac{I}{{\pi {{\left( {3R} \right)}^2} - \pi {{\left( {2R} \right)}^2}}}} \right)\left( {\pi {r^2} - \pi {{\left( {2R} \right)}^2}} \right)} \right)$
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2\pi r}}\left( {1 - \frac{{{r^2} - 4{R^2}}}{{5{R^2}}}} \right)$
$ = \frac{{{\mu _0}I[9{R^2} - {r^2}]}}{{10\pi {R^2}r}}$ is zero at $r = 3R$ and more than 3$R$ effective i will be zero so $B = 0$

question: The ratio of magnetic field at the centre of a current carrying circular coil to its magnetic moment is $x$. If the current and the radius both are doubled the new ratio will become
explanation:
Magnetic field at the centre of a circular current carrying coil.
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 r}$
Magnetic moment
$\begin{aligned}& M=\pi r^{2} i \\& \dfrac{B}{M}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi r^{3}}=x \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{x_{1}}{x_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)^{3}=8 \\& x_{2}=\dfrac{x_{1}}{8}=\dfrac{x}{8}\end{aligned}$

question: In the given figure, the magnetic field at $O$ .

question: The magnetic field at the center of a current carrying loop of radius 0.1 $m$ is $5 \sqrt{5}$ times that at a point along its axis. The distance of this point from the centre of the loop is
explanation:
Let us consider a point $P$ at a distance $x$ from the centre of loop, where magnetic field is given by $B_{0}=5 \sqrt{5} B$. ( $B_{0}$ is the magnetic field at centre).
If current $I$ is flowing through loop and radius of loop is $R$, then
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I{R^2}}}{{2{{\left( {{x^2} + {R^2}} \right)}^{3/2}}}}$
According to question,
$5\sqrt 5 \frac{{{\mu _0}I{R^2}}}{{2{{\left( {{x^2} + {R^2}} \right)}^{3/2}}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2R}}$
$ \Rightarrow {(\sqrt 5 R)^3} = {\left( {{x^2} + {R^2}} \right)^{3/2}} \Rightarrow 5{R^2} = \left( {{x^2} + {R^2}} \right)$
or $x^{2}+4 R^{2}$ or $x= \pm 2 R$
Here, $R = 0.1\,m \Rightarrow x = 0.2\,m$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Current Carrying Circular Loop

question: Two points $A$ and $B$ on the axis of a circular current loop are at distances of $4\,cm$ and $3\sqrt 3 \,cm$ from the centre of the loop. If the ratio of the induced magnetic fields at points $A$ and $B$ is $216: 125$, the radius of the loop is
explanation:
Magnetic field at a point on the axis of a current carrying circular loop is given by
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{o}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{2 \pi N i r^{2}}{\left(x^{2}+r^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \quad \therefore B \alpha \dfrac{r^{2}}{\left(x^{2}+r^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}$
Here, $r=$ radius of circular loop $x=$ distance of a point from the centre of circular loop
$\therefore \dfrac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=\dfrac{r^{2}}{\left[r^{2}+(4)^{2}\right]^{3 / 2}} \times \dfrac{\left[r^{2}+(3 \sqrt{3})^{2}\right]^{3 / 2}}{r^{2}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{216}{125}=\dfrac{\left[r^{2}+27\right]^{3 / 2}}{\left[r^{2}+16\right]^{3 / 2}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{6}{5}=\dfrac{\left(r^{2}+27\right)^{1 / 2}}{\left(r^{2}+16\right)^{1 / 2}}$
On solving we get, $r = 3\,cm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Current Carrying Circular Loop

question: A straight wire of length $20\,cm$ carrying a current of $\frac{3}{{{\pi ^2}}}\,A$ is bent in the form of a circle. The magnetic field at the centre of the circle is
explanation:
$l = 20\,cm,I = \frac{3}{{{\pi ^2}}}\,A$
For circle, $l=2 \pi R \Rightarrow 20 \times 10^{-2}=2 \pi R$
$\therefore R=\dfrac{10^{-1}}{\pi} m$
$\therefore \quad$ Magnetic field at the centre of circle,
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{3}{\pi^{2}}}{2 \times \dfrac{10^{-1}}{\pi}}$
$ = 6 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,T$

question: Two long straight parallel wires $A$ and $B$ separated by $5\,m$ carry currents $2\,A$ and $6\,A$ respectively in the same direction. The resultant magnetic field due to the two wires at a point of $2\,m$ distance from the wire $A$ in between the two wires is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957934/preptracker/questions/q_111307_explanation_1764957934032.png ${I_1} = 2\,A,{I_2} = 6\,A$
$r_{1}=2 m, r_{2}=3 m$
The resultant magnetic field at point $P$ is
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{2\pi }}\left( {\frac{{{I_2}}}{{{r_2}}} - \frac{{{I_1}}}{{{r_1}}}} \right) = 2 \times {10^{ - 7}}\left( {\frac{6}{3} - \frac{2}{2}} \right)$
$ = 2 \times {10^{ - 7}}T$

question: Match the terms related to magnetic field and magnetic force in Column I with their appropriate descriptions in Column II and select the correct code:
explanation:
A: The Lorentz Force is the force experienced by a moving charge in an applied magnetic and electric field.
B: Tesla is the SI unit of the magnetic field (B), where one tesla is defined as the field strength required to exert a force of one newton on a charge of one coulomb moving at a velocity of one meter per second perpendicular to the field (Q).
C: The Biot-Savart Law relates to the magnetic field at a point due to a small current element, which is proportional to the current, the size of the element, and inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the element to the point (S).
D: A cyclotron is a type of particle accelerator that uses a magnetic field to move charged particles in a spiral path, allowing them to gain energy with each pass (P). Correct option is (1)

question: A long straight wire carries a current of $18 A$. The magnitude of the magnetic field at a point  $12{\text{ }}cm$  from it is
explanation:
Given,
$I=18 A$
Distance from wire, where magnetic field is to find $=12 {~cm}=0.12 {~m}$
Using Ampere's law for long straight wire
$\oint B \cdot dl = {\mu _0}i \Rightarrow B = \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{2\pi r}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
Put the value of all terms in above equation
$B=\dfrac{\left(4 \pi \times 10^{-7}\right) \times 18}{2 \pi \times(0.12)} \Rightarrow B=3 \times 10^{-5} T$
So, the correct option is (3)

question: The magnetic field at a perpendicular distance of one metre from a wire carrying current of $1 A$ is
explanation:
Given,
Distance $(r)=1 m$
Current $(i)=1 A$ direction of magnetic field is perpendicular According to Ampere's law, the magnetic field is given by $B = \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{2\pi r}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
where $\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} {H} / {m}$
Put the value in Eq. (1)
$B=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{2 \pi \times 1} \times 1=2 \times 10^{-7} T$
So, the magnetic field is $2 \times 10^{-7} T$.
So, the correct option is (1)

question: It is found that a non-zero current element is unable to produce any magnetic field at a particular point. Then the angle between the current element and the position vector of that point with respect to the current element is
explanation:
Given,
Magnetic field $B=0$
Let $i$ be the non zero current.
$d l$ is the length element. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957932/preptracker/questions/q_111242_explanation_1764957932374.png
$r$ is the separation between wire element.
Using Biot-Savart's Law
$|d \vec{B}|=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{i d l \sin \theta}{r^{2}}$
or $B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{i L \sin \theta \hat{{r}}}{r^{2}}$
[ $L$ is total length of current carrying element]
$B=0 \quad$ [given]
For $B$ to be zero, either $i$ should be zero or
$\sin \theta$ be zero. But in question, it is mention current is non zero.
$\therefore \sin \theta=0 \quad \theta=0, \pi$
So, the angle between current and position vector is $0^{\circ}$ or $180^{\circ}$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Small Current Element

question: A current $i=5 A$ flows along a thin wire shaped as shown in figure. The radius of curved part of the wire is equal to $R=100 {~mm}$, the angle $2 \phi=90^{\circ}$. The magnitude of magnetic field at the point $O$ is approximately (use,  $\frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }} = {10^{ - 7}}Tm{A^{ - 1}})$
explanation:
The given situation is shown as https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952777/preptracker/questions/q_111237_explanation_1764952776916.png
Magnitude of magnetic field due to straight current carrying wire $A B$ is given as
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i}{O M}(\sin \phi+\sin \phi)$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{i}{R \sin \phi}(2 \sin \phi) \quad(\because O M=R \sin \phi)$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{2 i}{R}=10^{-7} \times \dfrac{2 \times 5}{0.1}$
$(\because R=100 {~mm}=0.1 {~m}$ and $i=5 A)$
$=10^{-5} {~T}$
Magnitude of magnetic field due to current carrying circular arc,
$B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 R} \times \dfrac{(2 \pi-2 \phi)}{2 \pi}$
$=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2 \times 01} \times \dfrac{\left(2 \pi-\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)}{2 \pi}$
$\left(\because 2 \phi=90^{\circ}=\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$
$=\dfrac{4 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{0.2} \times \dfrac{3}{4}$
$B_{2}=2.36 \times 10^{-5} T$
$\therefore$ Total magnetic field at $O$,
$B=B_{1}+B_{2}=10^{-5}+2.36 \times 10^{-5}$
$=10^{-5}(1+2.36)=3.36 \times 10^{-5} T$
$=33.6 \times 10^{-6} T=33.6 \mu T$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field due to a finite sized current wire/ closed coil in the form of Triangle, Rectangle etc.

question: A current carrying loop $A B C D$ has two circular arcs $A D$ and $B C$ with radius  $1{\text{ }}cm$  and  $2{\text{ }}cm$  respectively, as shown in the figure. The two $\operatorname{arcs} A D$ and $B C$ subtend a common $30^{\circ}$ angle at the centre $O$. If the current flowing in the loop is $\dfrac{12}{\pi} {~A}$.    Then, the magnitude of net magnetic field at point $O$ is (given, $\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}$ in SI units)
explanation:
Current carrying loop $A B C D$ is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952771/preptracker/questions/q_111229_explanation_1764952770832.png
Current, $i=\dfrac{1.2}{\pi} A$
Magnetic field due to the straight part $A B$ & $C D$ is zero.
Magnetic field at centre $O$ due to current carrying arc $A D$,
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 r_{1}} \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{360^{\circ}}=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{12}{\pi}}{2 \times 1 \times 10^{-2}} \times \dfrac{30^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}}$
$=24 \times 10^{-5} \times \dfrac{1}{12}=2 \times 10^{-6} {~T}$ [upward direction $r \odot$ ]
Similarly, magnetic field at centre $O$ due to current carrying arc $B C$,
$B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 r_{2}} \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{360^{\circ}}=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{1.2}{\pi}}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-2}} \times \dfrac{30}{360^{\circ}}$
$=1 \times 10^{-6} T$ [(downward direction) or $\otimes$ ]
$\therefore$ Net magnetic field at centre $O$,
$B_{\text {net }}=B_{1}-B_{2}=2 \times 10^{-6}-1 \times 10^{-6}$
$=1 \times 10^{-6} T=1 \mu T$ [upward or $\odot$ ]
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: Two infinitely long thin wires are placed at $(1 {~cm}, 0 {~cm})$ and $(2 {~cm}, 0 {~cm})$ as shown in the figure.    https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957924/preptracker/questions/q_111227_question_1764957923968.png    The same current $i$ flows in both the wires in the same direction, say, into the page. Let the magnetic field at the origin due to these wires is $B$. If $B_{0}$ is the magnitude of the magnetic field, if only the wire at ( $1 {~cm}, 0 {~cm}$ ) was present, then the value of $B / B_{0}$ is
explanation:
Magnetic field at the origin $O$ due to both Wire-1 and Wire -2 is given as https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957925/preptracker/questions/q_111227_explanation_1764957924678.png
$B=B_{1}+B_{2}$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \times \dfrac{i}{r_{1}}+\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \times \dfrac{i}{r_{2}}$
$=2 \times 10^{-7} i\left[\dfrac{1}{r_{1}}+\dfrac{1}{r_{2}}\right]$
$=2 \times 10^{-7} i\left[\dfrac{1}{1 \times 10^{-2}}+\dfrac{1}{2 \times 10^{-2}}\right]=3 \times 10^{-5} i$
$\therefore$ Magnetic field at $O$ due to Wire-l alone
$B_{0}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \times \dfrac{i}{r_{1}}$
$=2 \times 10^{-7} \dfrac{i}{1 \times 10^{-2}}=2 \times 10^{-5} i$
$\therefore \dfrac{B}{B_{0}}=\dfrac{3 \times 10^{-5} i}{2 \times 10^{-5} i}=\dfrac{3}{2}$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: $A B C D$ is a rectangular loop made of uniform wire. If $A D=B C=2 {~cm}$, what is the magnetic force per unit length acting on wire $D C$ due to wire $A B$, if ammeter reads $20 A$ ? (The length of $A B$ and $D C$ are large in comparison with other two sides)
explanation:
Given, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957922/preptracker/questions/q_111220_explanation_1764957922290.png
$A D=B C=d=2 {~cm}=2 \times 10^{-2} {~m}$
Since, current in main branch is $20 A$, then current in separate branches be $i_{1}$ and $i_{2}$. where, $i_{1}=i_{2}=10 {~A}$
As we know that,
$\dfrac{F}{l}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \dfrac{i_{1} i_{2}}{d}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{F}{l}=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{2 \pi} \times \dfrac{10 \times 10}{2 \times 10^{-2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{F}{l}=2 \times 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{10^{4}}{2}=10^{-3} {Nm}^{-1}$
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field due to a finite sized current wire/ closed coil in the form of Triangle, Rectangle etc.

question: Two circular coils $P$ and $Q$ are made of two identical wires of the same length. The number of turns in the two coils are 4 and 2, respectively. The magnetic inductions at the centre of $P$ and $Q$ are $B_{P}$ and $B_{Q}$, respectively. Then the ratio $\dfrac{B_{P}}{B_{Q}}$ is
explanation:
Let the two coil $P$ and $Q$ having same length and number of turns $N_{P}=4$ and $N_{Q}=2$
Then, $l=N_{P} 2 \pi R_{P}=N_{Q} 2 \pi R_{Q}$
where, $R_{P}=$ radius of coil $P$.
and $R_{Q}=$ radius of coil $Q$.
Now, $\dfrac{R_{P}}{R_{Q}}=\dfrac{N_{Q}}{N_{P}}=\dfrac{2}{4}=\dfrac{1}{2}$
If same current is passing through $P$ and $Q$.
Then,
$\dfrac{B_{P}}{B_{Q}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{N_{P} \mu_{0} i}{2 R_{P}}}{\dfrac{N_{Q} \mu_{0} i}{2 R_{Q}}}=\dfrac{R_{Q} N_{P}}{R_{P} N_{Q}}=2 \times 2 \Rightarrow \dfrac{B_{P}}{B_{Q}}=4$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: A coil having 2000 turns is wound tightly in the form of a spiral with inner and outer radii  $1{\text{ }}cm$  and  $3{\text{ }}cm,$  respectively. When a current of $1 / \pi m A$ passes through the coil, then the magnetic field at the centre is $K \ln 3 \times 10^{-6} T$.   The value of $K$ is
explanation:
Number of turns, $N=2000$
Inner radius, $r_{1}=1 {~cm}$
Outer radius, $r_{2}=3 {~cm}$
Current, $I=\dfrac{1}{\pi} m A=\dfrac{10^{-3}}{\pi} A$
Magnetic field at the centre of the coil,
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N i}{2 r}$
Mean radius,
$r=\dfrac{r_{1}+r_{2}}{2}=\dfrac{1+3}{2}=2 {~cm}=0.02 {~m}$
$\therefore \quad B=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2000 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 0.02 \times \pi}=4 \times 10^{-5} T$
$\Rightarrow K \ln 3 \times 10^{-6}=4 \times 10^{-5}$
$\Rightarrow \quad K=\dfrac{40}{\ln 3}=36.4 \simeq 36$
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: Figure below shows three circuits consisting of concentric circular arcs and straight radial lines. The centre of the circle is shown by the dot. Same current flows through each of the circuits. If $B_{1}, B_{2}, B_{3}$ are the magnitudes of the magnetic field at the centre. Which of the following is true?
explanation:
Suppose, current $i$ is flowing each of the circuit
Case I https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952766/preptracker/questions/q_111214_explanation_1764952765622.png
Net magnetic field at centre is
$B_{1}=B_{\text {upper part }}+B_{\text {lower part }}$
$=\left|\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4(3 r)}+\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4(r)}\right|=\left|\dfrac{4 \mu_{0} i}{12 r}\right|=\left|\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{3 r}\right|=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{3 r}$
Case II https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952767/preptracker/questions/q_111214_explanation_1764952766588.png
$B_{2}=\left|B_{\text {upper part }}-B_{\text {lower part }}\right|$
$=\left|\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4(3 r)}-\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4(r)}\right|=\left|\dfrac{-2 \mu_{0} i}{12 r}\right|$
$=\left|\dfrac{-\mu_{0} i}{6 r}\right|=\mu_{0} i / 6 r$
Case III https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952768/preptracker/questions/q_111214_explanation_1764952767867.png
$B_{3}=\left|B_{\text {upper part }}+B_{\text {lower part }}+B_{\text {smallest part }}\right|$
$=\left|\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4(3 r)}+\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{8(2 r)}+\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{8 r}\right|=\dfrac{13}{48} \dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{r}$
Clearly, $B_{1}>B_{3}>B_{2}$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: A current $i$ flows in a straight wire bent in the shape of a quarter ring of radius $R$ as shown in figure. The magnetic field induction $B$ at point $O$ is
explanation:
Magnetic field at centre of a complete current carrying circular loop is
$B_{c}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957920/preptracker/questions/q_111213_explanation_1764957920056.png
For one quarter of loop,
$B=\dfrac{1}{4} \times \dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}=\dfrac{1}{8}\left(\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{R}\right)$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: A conducting wire is in the shape of a regular hexagon which is inscribed inside an imaginary circle of radius $R$. If a current $i$ flows the wire, the magnitude of magnetic field at the centre of the circle is
explanation:
The given situation is shown in the following figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952762/preptracker/questions/q_111207_explanation_1764952761550.png
First we will calculate magnetic field at centre due to current segment $A B$.
$\therefore$ In $\triangle O A M$,
$\sin 60^{\circ}=\dfrac{O M}{R} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\dfrac{O M}{R}$
$\Rightarrow O M=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} R \Rightarrow r=O M=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} R$
$\therefore$ Magnetic field at point due to current segment is given as
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i}{r}\left[\sin 30^{\circ}+\sin 30^{\circ}\right]$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{i}{\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} R}\left[\dfrac{1}{2}+\dfrac{1}{2}\right]$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 \sqrt{3} \pi R}$ [downward]
Magnitude to total magnetic field at centre is given as,
$B=6 B_{1}=6 \times \dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 \sqrt{3} \pi R}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3} \mu_{0} i}{\pi R}$ [downward]
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field due to a finite sized current wire/ closed coil in the form of Triangle, Rectangle etc.

question: A coil having 100 turns is wound tightly in the form of a spiral with inner and outer radii  $1{\text{ }}cm$  and  $2{\text{ }}cm$, respectively.   When a current of   $1{\text{ }}A$    passes through the coil, the magnetic field at the centre of the coil is
explanation:
Consider a small circular element of thickness $d r$ at a distance $r$ from the centre of spiral as shown in the figure, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957917/preptracker/questions/q_111206_explanation_1764957917083.png
Total number of turns in this element
$=\dfrac{N}{b-a} d r$
Current passing through this element,
$d i=\dfrac{N}{b-a} d r i$
$\therefore$ Magnetic field at the centre of spiral due to this element is given as
$d B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} d i}{2 r}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 r} \cdot \dfrac{N i}{b-a} d r$
$\therefore$ Total magnetic field,
$B=\int d B=\int_{a}^{b} \dfrac{\mu_{0} N i}{2(b-a)} \cdot \dfrac{d r}{r}$
$\Rightarrow \quad B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N i}{2(b-a)} \ln \left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)$
Given, $\quad N=100, i=1 A, a=1 c m=10^{-2} m$,
$b=2 {~cm}=2 \times 10^{-2} {~m}$
$\therefore \quad B=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 100 \times 1}{2\left(2 \times 10^{-2}-1 \times 10^{-2}\right)} \ln \left(\dfrac{2 \times 10^{-2}}{1 \times 10^{-2}}\right)$
$=2 \pi \times 10^{-3} \ln (2)=2 \pi \ln (2) \times 10^{-3} T$
$=2 \pi l {n}(2) m T$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: Two infinite wires carrying opposite electrical currents $I$ and $i$ are placed at a distance $x$ apart. A point $P$ at a distance $y$ away from the wire carrying current is shown in the figure. If the magnetic field is zero at the point , then the magnitude of $i$ is
explanation:
The given situation is shown in the following figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952760/preptracker/questions/q_111202_explanation_1764952759604.png
Magnetic field at point $P$ due to the wire having current $I$ is given as
${B_I} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{2\pi }} \cdot \frac{I}{{x + y}}\quad \quad $ (Inwards)
Magnetic field at point $P$ due to the wire having current $i$ is given as
${B_i} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{2\pi }} \cdot \frac{i}{y}\quad \quad \quad $ (Outwards)
According to question, net magnetic field at point $P$ is zero, hence $B_{I}-B_{i}=0, B_{I}=B_{i}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{I}{x+y}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i}{y}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{I}{x+y}=\dfrac{i}{y}$
$\Rightarrow \quad i=I\left(\dfrac{y}{x+y}\right)$
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: Two infinitely long straight wires $A$ and $B$, each carrying current $I$ are placed on $x$ and $y$-axis, respectively. The current in wires $A$ and $B$ flow along $-\hat{i}$ and $\hat{j}$ directions respectively. The force on a charged particle having charge $q$, moving from position, $\vec{r}=d(\hat{i}+\hat{j})$ with velocity $v=v \hat{i}$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957915/preptracker/questions/q_111200_explanation_1764957915513.png
Position vector of charge particle,
$\vec{r}=d(\hat{i}+\hat{j})=d \hat{i}+d \hat{j}$
The magnetic field intensity due to the wire along $-\hat{i}$ direction,
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i}{d}(-\hat{k})$
Similarly, magnetic field intensity due to wire along $\hat{j}$ direction.
$B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i}{d}(-\hat{k})$
$\therefore$ Resultant magnetic field at point $P$ due to both the wires,
$B=B_{1}+B_{2}$
$=2 \cdot \dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i}{d}(-\hat{k}) \Rightarrow B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{i}{d}(-\hat{k})$
$\therefore$ Force on the charged particles,
$\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$
$=q\left[v \hat{i} \times \dfrac{\mu_{0}}{\pi} \dfrac{i}{d} \cdot(-\hat{k})\right]=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i q v}{\pi d} \cdot \hat{j}$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: A long straight wire carrying current $16 A$ is bent at $90^{\circ}$ such that half of the wire lies along the positive $x$ - axis and other half lies along the positive $y$ - axis. What is the magnitude of the magnetic field at the point, $r=(-2 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}) {mm}$    (Assume, $\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}=10^{-7} {Hm}^{-1}$)
explanation:
According to the question, a long straight wire is bent at $90^{\circ}$ as shown in the figure, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952758/preptracker/questions/q_111199_explanation_1764952757858.png
Where, current flowing through the wire,
$i=16 {~A}$
$\vec{r}=O \vec{P}=(-2 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}) {mm}$
Magnitude of the magnetic field at point $P$ due to current carrying wire along $x$-axis is zero because post $P$ lies in the direction of conducting wire along $x$-axis.
$\therefore$ Magnitude of the magnetic field due to current carrying wire along $y$-axis at point $P$ is given by
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i}{r}$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{16}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \quad\left[\because r=2 {~mm}=2 \times 10^{-3} {~m}\right]$
$=10^{-7} \times 8 \times 10^{3} \quad T=0.8 m T$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: A dielectric circular disc of radius $R$ carries a uniform surface charge density $\sigma$. If it rotates about its axis with angular velocity $\omega$, the magnetic field at the center  of disc is:
explanation:
Let us assume a ring like element of the disc of radius $r$ and thickness $d r$. If $\sigma$ is the surface charge density, then the charge on the element.
$dq = \sigma (2\pi r)dr\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
Current di associated with rotating charge $d q$ is
$di = \frac{{dq}}{T} = \frac{{dq\omega }}{{2\pi }}\quad \left( {\because T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega }} \right)\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952743/preptracker/questions/q_111194_explanation_1764952742816.png
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$\Rightarrow \quad d i=\sigma \omega r d r$
As, the magnetic field $d B$ at centre due to the element,
$d B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} d i}{2 r}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \sigma \omega r d r}{2 r} \Rightarrow d B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \sigma \omega}{2} d r$
$\Rightarrow B_{\text {net }}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \sigma \omega}{2} \int_{0}^{R} d r$
$\Rightarrow B_{\text {net }}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \sigma \omega R}{2}$
Hence, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Rotating Charge

question: A magnetic field of $5 \times 10^{-5} T$ is produced at a perpendicular distance of  $0.2\,m$  from a long straight wire carrying electric current. If the permeability of free space is $4 \pi \times 10^{-7} {Tm} / {A}$. The current passing through the wire (in $A$) is
explanation:
Given, magnetic field, $B=5 \times 10^{-5} T$
$r=0.2 m$
and $\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} {Tm} / {A}$
Magnetic field produced by long straight current (i) carrying wire is given by
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i}{r} \Rightarrow 5 \times 10^{-5}=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{2 \pi} \times \dfrac{i}{0.2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad i=\dfrac{5 \times 10^{-5} \times 2 \pi \times 0.2}{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}=0.5 \times 10^{2}=50 {~A}$
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: A very long wire carrying a current of $4 \sqrt{2} A$ is bent at a right angles. The magnitude of magnetic field $(|B|)$ at a point $P$ lying on a line perpendicular to the bent wire at a distance, $d=20 {~cm}$ from the point of the bending will be (Let $\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} {H} / {m}$ )
explanation:
Given, $\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} H / m$
distance, $d=20 {~cm}=0.2 {~m}$
and current, $i=4 \sqrt{2} {~A}$
$\therefore$ Magnetic field at a point $P$ due to current carrying wire 1,
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952752/preptracker/questions/q_111190_explanation_1764952752460.png
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i}{d}=10^{-7} \times \dfrac{4 \sqrt{2}}{0.2}=2 \sqrt{2} \times 10^{-6} T(\hat{j})$
Magnetic field at point $P$ due to current carrying wire 2,
$B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i}{d}=10^{-7} \times \dfrac{4 \sqrt{2}}{0.2}=2 \sqrt{2} \times 10^{-6} T(\hat{i})$
Here $B_{1} \perp B_{2}$ hence the resultant magnetic field at point $P$,
$B=\sqrt{B_{1}^{2}+B_{2}^{2}}$
$=\sqrt{\left(2 \sqrt{2} \times 10^{-6}\right)^{2}+\left(2 \sqrt{2} \times 10^{-6}\right)^{2}}$
$=2 \sqrt{2} \times 10^{-6} \sqrt{1+1}=4 \times 10^{-6} T=4 \mu T$
Hence, the magnitude of the magnetic field is $4 \mu T$.
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: Two circular loops $L_{1}$ and $L_{2}$ of wire carrying equal and opposite currents are placed parallel to each other with a common axis. The radius of loop $L_{1}$ is ${R}_{1}$ and that of $L_{2}$ is ${R}_{2}$. The distance between the centres of the loops is $\sqrt{3} R_{1}$. The magnetic field at the centre of $L_{2}$, shall be zero, if
explanation:
Magnetic field at centre of $L_{2}$ will be zero, when
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957910/preptracker/questions/q_111189_explanation_1764957909624.png
$B_{L_{1}}=B_{L_{2}}$ (magnitude)
$\dfrac{\mu_{0} i R_{1}^{2}}{2\left(R_{1}^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 R_{2}}$
Here $\quad x=\sqrt{3} R_{1}$
$\therefore \dfrac{R_{1}^{2}}{2\left(R_{1}^{2}+3 R_{1}^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}=\dfrac{1}{2 R_{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{R_{1}^{2}}{8 R_{1}^{3}}=\dfrac{1}{R_{2}} \Rightarrow R_{2}=8 R_{1}$
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Combination of Long Straight and Circular Current Wires

question: A steady current $i$ flows through a wire with one end at $O$ and the other end extending upto infinity as shown in the figure. The magnetic field at a point $P$, located at a distance $d$ from $O$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952751/preptracker/questions/q_111186_explanation_1764952750866.png
From result of field of a long wire, we have
$\beta=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{i}{r} \times\left(\sin \theta_{1}-\sin \theta_{2}\right)$
Here, $r=d \sin \alpha$
$\sin \theta_{1}=\sin 90^{\circ}=1$
$\sin \theta_{2}=\sin (90-\alpha)=\cos \alpha$
So, $\beta=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4 \pi d \sin \alpha}(1-\cos \alpha)$
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: Consider a current carrying wire as shown in the figure. If the radius of the curved part of the wire is $R$ and the linear parts are assumed to be very long, then the magnetic induction of the field at the point $O$ is
explanation:
Magnetic field at point $O=$ Magnetic field due to wire 1+ Magnetic field due to semicircle + Magnetic field due to wire 2
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952749/preptracker/questions/q_111185_explanation_1764952749483.png
$\therefore B_{a t}{ }_{O}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4 \pi R}+\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4 R}+\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4 \pi R}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4 \pi R}(2+\pi)$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Combination of Long Straight and Circular Current Wires

question: For a wire, as shown in the figure, carries a current of  $10\,A,$  then the magnetic induction field at the point $O$ is [take, $\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} {H} / {m}$ ]
explanation:
Magnetic field due to a semi infinite current carrying wire at a distance 'd' from one end is given by $B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4 \pi d}$
For both the wire,
$i=10 {~A}, d=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10^{-2} {~m}$
Magnetic field due to one wire,
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \times 10}{4 \pi \times\left(\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10^{-2}\right)}$
$B=\dfrac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 10}{10^{-2}}=2 \times 10^{-4} {~T}$
Due to both wires magnetic field is in same direction, so they will add up.
$\Rightarrow B_{\text {net }}=2\left(2 \times 10^{-4}\right) T=4 \times 10^{-4} T$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field due to a finite sized current wire/ closed coil in the form of Triangle, Rectangle etc.

question: The net magnetic field at point $O$ due to the two infinite current carrying wires shown in the figure is
explanation:
${B_{net}} = - {B_1} + {B_2}\quad \left( {\because \quad {B_1}( - \hat k) + {B_2}\hat k} \right)$
$B_{\text {net }}=\left(\dfrac{-\mu_{0}(5 A)}{2 \pi \times 0.1}+\dfrac{\mu_{0} \times 4}{2 \pi \times 0.04}\right) T$
$\therefore B_{\text {net }}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi}(-50+100) T$
$ = 2 \times {10^{ - 7}} \times 100\,T = 2 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,T$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952744/preptracker/questions/q_111174_explanation_1764952744315.png
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: Two insulated circular loops $A$ and $B$ of radius '$a$' carrying a current of $\,\,\,\,$'$I$' in the anti clockwise direction as shown in the figure. The magnitude of the magnetic induction at the centre will be
explanation:
Magnetic field induction due to loop $A$ at the centre $O$ is
${B_A} = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2a}}\,\,\,\left( \uparrow \right)$
Magnetic field induction due to loop $B$ at the centre $O$ is
$B_{B}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a}(\leftarrow)$
$\therefore \quad B_{A}=B_{B}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a}$
$\because \quad B_{A}$ and $B_{B}$ are perpendicular to each other, therefore, the resultant magnetic field induction at centre $O$ is,
$B = \sqrt {B_A^2 + B_B^2} = \sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2a}}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2a}}} \right)}^2}} $
$ = \sqrt 2 \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2a}} \Rightarrow B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{\sqrt 2 a}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: An element $\Delta \vec{l}=\Delta x \hat{i}$ is placed at the origin and carries a large current $I = 10\,A.$  The magnetic field on the $y$ -axis at a distance of $0.5\,m$ from the elements $\Delta \,x$ of   $1\,cm$ length is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950672/preptracker/questions/q_43873_explanation_1764950671719.png
Given that, $\Delta \,l = \Delta \,x = l\,cm = {10^{ - 2}}\,m$
$I = 10\,A,\,\,r = 0.5\,m$ and $\theta = 90^\circ $
Using Biot - Savart's law, $d B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{I d l \sin \theta}{r^{2}}$
or $d B=10^{-7} \times \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-2} \times \sin 90^{\circ}}{(0.5)^{2}}$
$\therefore \quad d B=4 \times 10^{-8} T$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Small Current Element

question: Two parallel, long wires carry currents ${i_{1}}$ and ${i_{2}}$ with  ${i_1} > {i_2}.$  When the currents are in the same direction, the magnetic field at a point midway between the wire is 10 $mT$ . If the direction of ${i_{2}}$ is reversed, the field becomes 30 $mT$ . Find the ratio ${i_{1} / i_{2}}$.
explanation:
$10 {mT}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi(r / 2)}\left(i_{1}-i_{2}\right)$$30 {mT}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi(r / 2)}\left(i_{1}+i_{2}\right)$
Dividing $: i_{1} i_{2}=2$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: An elevator carrying a charge of 0.5 $C$ is moving down with a velocity of ${5 \times 10^{3} {~m} {~s}^{-1}}$. The elevator is 4 $m$ from the bottom and 3 $m$ horizontally from ${P}$ as shown in figure. What magnetic field (in ${\mu {T})}$ does it produce at point ${P}$?
explanation:
According to Biot-Savart law,
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{qv}}{{{r^2}}}\sin \theta $
$ = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 7}} \times 0.5 \times 5 \times {{10}^3}}}{{{5^2}}}\left( {\frac{3}{5}} \right) = 6\mu T$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Moving Charge

question: A wire carrying current ${i}$ has the configuration shown in figure. Two semi-infinite straight sections, each tangent to the same circle, are connected by a circular arc, of angle ${\theta}$, along the circumference of the circle, with all sections lying in the same plane. What must ${\theta}$   be in order for ${B}$ to be zero at the center of circle?
explanation:
Magnetic field at $O$ due to wire 1 is, $\vec{B}_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4 \pi R} \odot$
and that due to wire 2 is
${{\vec B}_2} = \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{4\pi R}} \odot $
$ \Rightarrow \quad \vec B = {{\vec B}_1} + {{\vec B}_2} = \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{2\pi R}} \odot $ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950653/preptracker/questions/q_43836_explanation_1764950652640.png Magnetic field at the centre due to circular arc subtending angle $\theta$ at the centre is given by
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{{\vec B}_{{\text{arc}}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{2R}}\left( {\frac{\theta }{{2\pi }}} \right) \otimes $
${\text{If}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{{\vec B}_{{\text{net}}}} = 0 \Rightarrow \vec B + {{\vec B}_{{\text{arc}}}} = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\vec B = - {{\vec B}_{{\text{arc}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{2\pi R}} \odot = - \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{2R}}\left( {\frac{\theta }{{2\pi }}} \right) \otimes $
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{2\pi R}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{2R}}\left( {\frac{\theta }{{2\pi }}} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\pi } = \frac{\theta }{{2\pi }}$
$ \Rightarrow \theta = 2{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} rad$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Combination of Long Straight and Circular Current Wires

question: Two coplanar concentric circular wires made of same material have radius ${R_{1}}$ and ${R_{2}\left(=2 R_{1}\right)}$. The wires carry current due to identical source of emf having no internal resistance. Find the ratio of radii of cross section of the two wires $\left( {\left. {\frac{{{r_1}}}{{{r_2}}} = ?} \right)} \right.$ if the magnetic induction field at the centre of the circle is zero.
explanation:
The wires must have current in opposite sense and
$\frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}}}{{2{R_1}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_2}}}{{2 \cdot \left( {2{R_1}} \right)}}$
$\therefore \quad 2{I_1} = {I_2}\quad \quad \quad (1)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950647/preptracker/questions/q_43812_explanation_1764950647195.png and $\quad \varepsilon=I_{1} \dfrac{\rho\left(2 \pi R_{1}\right)}{\pi r_{1}^{2}}=I_{2} \dfrac{\rho\left(2 \pi 2 R_{1}\right)}{\pi r_{2}^{2}}$
[$r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ are radii of cross section]
$\Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{r_{1}^{2}}{r_{2}^{2}}=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{{r_1^2}}{{r_2^2}} = \frac{1}{4}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,[{\text{using}}\,\,1]$
$ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{{{r_1}}}{{{r_2}}} = \frac{1}{2} \approx 0.5$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: A thin hemispherical shell having a uniform surface charge density ${\sigma}$ spins with an angular speed ${\omega}$. If the magnetic field at ${O}$ is found to be ${\dfrac{N \mu_{0} \sigma \omega}{3}}$, find the value of ${N}$.
explanation:
The magnetic field due to the ring is $d B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}(d i) r^{2}}{2 R^{3}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950645/preptracker/questions/q_43800_explanation_1764950645479.png The total magnetic field is
$B=\int d B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 R^{3}} \int r^{2} d i$ ,
where $d i=(2 \pi r \cdot R d \theta \sigma) \dfrac{\omega}{2 \pi}$
or $B = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{2{R^3}}}\int {{r^2}} (2\,\pi rRd\theta \,\sigma )\frac{\omega }{{2\pi }}$
$ = \frac{{\pi {\mu _0}\sigma \omega }}{{2{R^2}}}\int {{r^3}} d\theta $
$ = \frac{{\pi {\mu _0}\sigma \omega }}{{2{R^2}}}\int_0^{\pi /2} {{R^3}} {\sin ^3}\theta d\theta $
Evaluating the integration, we have $B=\dfrac{2 \mu_{0} \sigma \omega}{3}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Rotating Charge

question: A square loop of side ${a=6 {~cm}}$ carries a current ${I=1 {~A}}$. Calculate magnetic induction ${B}$ (in ${\mu {T}}$ ) at point ${P}$, lying on the axis of loop and at a distance ${x=\sqrt{7} {~cm}}$ from the center of loop.
explanation:
Axis of the loop means a line passing through center of loop and normal to its plane. Since distance of the point ${P}$ is ${x}$ from center of loop and side of square loop is ${a}$ as shown in Figure. (a). https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950617/preptracker/questions/q_43646_explanation_1764950617177.png Therefore, perpendicular distance of ${P}$ from each side of
$r=\sqrt{x^{2}+\left(\dfrac{a}{2}\right)^{2}}=4 {~cm}$
Now, consider only one side ${A B}$ of the loop as shown in Figure. (b).
$\begin{aligned}& \tan \alpha=\tan \beta=\dfrac{(a / 2)}{r}=\dfrac{3}{4} \\& \alpha=\beta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right)=37^{\circ}\end{aligned}$
Magnitude of magnetic induction at ${P}$, due to current in this side ${A B}$, is
$B_{0}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi r}(\sin \alpha+\sin \beta)=3 \times 10^{-6} {~T}$
Now, consider magnetic inductions, produced by currents in two opposite sides ${A B}$ and ${C D}$ as shown in Figure. (c). https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950618/preptracker/questions/q_43646_explanation_1764950617962.png Components of these magnetic inductions, parallel to plane of loop neutralise each other. Hence, resultant of these two magnetic inductions is ${2 B_{0} \cos \theta}$ (along the axis).
Similarly, resultant of magnetic inductions produced by currents in remaining two opposite sides ${B C}$ and ${A D}$ will also be equal to ${2 B_{0} \cos \theta}$ (along the axis in same direction).
Hence, resultant magnetic induction,
$B = 4{B_0}\cos \theta $
$B = 4 \times \left( {3 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right)\frac{{(a/2)}}{r}$
$ = 9 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,T = 9\,\mu T$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field due to a finite sized current wire/ closed coil in the form of Triangle, Rectangle etc.

question: Two similar coils of radius $R$ are lying concentrically with their planes at right angles to each other. The currents flowing in them are $21$ and $41$ respectively. The resultant magnetic field induction at the centre is found to be $N$ times $\frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{R}$.  Find  the value of  $N$ is   (Take $\sqrt{5}=2.24$ )
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956469/preptracker/questions/q_43629_explanation_1764956469396.png Magnetic field induction due to vertical loop at centre $O$ is $B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} 2 I}{2 R} \hat{i}$
Magnetic field due to horizontal loop at centre $O$ is $B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} 4 I}{2 R} \hat{j}$
Now,
${B_{net{\rm{ }}}} = \sqrt {B_1^2 + B_2^2} $
$ = \sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{{{\mu _0}2I}}{{2R}}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{{{\mu _0}4I}}{{2R}}} \right)}^2}} $
$ = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2R}}\sqrt {{{(2)}^2} + {{(4)}^2}} $
$ = \frac{{\sqrt {20} }}{2}\frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{R}$
${B_{net{\rm{ }}}} = \frac{{2\sqrt 5 }}{2} = 2.24\frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{R}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: As shown in figure, two infinitely long, identical wires are bent by $90^{\circ}$ and placed in such a way that the segments $LP$ and $QM$ are along $x$-axis, while segments $PS$ and $QN$ are parallel to $y$-axis. If $OP = OQ = 5\,cm$, and the magnitude of the magnetic field at $O$ is  ${10^{ - 5}}\,T,$  and the two wires carry equal currents (see figure), the magnitude of current in each wire is
explanation:
The magnetic field due to wires $LP$ and $MQ$ will be zero at point $O$.
Magnetic field at point $O$ due to vertical wires is given as,
${B}={B}_{{SP}}+{B}_{{QN}}$
$\therefore \quad B_{S P}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} {I}}{4 \pi}\left(\sin 0+\sin 90^{\circ}\right)$;
${B}_{{QN}}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} {I}}{4 \pi}\left(\sin 0+\sin 90^{\circ}\right)$$B=2 \times \dfrac{\mu_{0} {I}}{4 \pi}\left(\sin 0+\sin 90^{\circ}\right)$
$\Rightarrow 10^{-5}=\dfrac{2 \times 4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{1}{0.05}$
$\Rightarrow {I}=2.5 {~A}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: The magnetic field at the point of intersection of diagonals of a square loop of side $L$ carrying a current $I$ is found to be $N \dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{\pi L}$. find the value of $N$.
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950610/preptracker/questions/q_43596_explanation_1764950609583.png Here ${r}={OE}=\dfrac{{L}}{2}$.
Magnetic field at the centre ${O}$ due to the current element ${AD}$ is given as
${B}_{{AD}}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} {I}}{4 \pi {r}}\left[\sin 45^{\circ}+\sin 45^{\circ}\right]=\dfrac{\mu_{0} {I}}{4 \pi {r}} \cdot \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{2}}$
Similarly
${B_{DC}} = {B_{BC}} = {B_{AB}} = {B_{AD}}$
${B_{net}} = 4\,{B_{AD}} = 4 \cdot \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{4\pi r}}\frac{2}{{\sqrt 2 }}$
$ = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{\pi \left( {\frac{L}{2}} \right)}} \cdot \frac{2}{{\sqrt 2 }}$
$ = 2\sqrt 2 \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{\pi L}}$
${B_{net{\rm{ }}}} = 2.82\frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{\pi L}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field due to a finite sized current wire/ closed coil in the form of Triangle, Rectangle etc.

question: $AB$ and $CD$ are straight wires and $BC$ (curved) is a semicircle. A current ' $i$ ' is passed through it. The magnetic field due to all three segments at point $P$ (centre of semicircle) is found to be $Q$ $\frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{d},$  find  the value of $Q$
explanation:
Magnetic field due to $A B$ ,
$B_{1}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{\mu_{0} {i}}{2 \pi\left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} {i}}{2 \pi {d}}$ out of plane
Magnetic field due to $C D$,
$B_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{\mu_{0} {i}}{2 \pi\left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} {i}}{2 \pi {d}}$ out of plane
Magnetic field due to ${BC}$,
$B_{3}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{\mu_{0} {i}}{2\left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} {i}}{2 {~d}}$ out of plane
$B_{\text {net }}=B_{1}+B_{2}+B_{3}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,[\because $all in same directions $]$
$ = \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{2\,d}}\left[ {1 + \frac{2}{\pi }} \right]$
$ = 0.82\frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{d}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Combination of Long Straight and Circular Current Wires

question: A current loop, having two circular arc is joined by two radial lines (as shown in fig.) and carries a current of $10A$. The magnetic field at point $0$ is $N \times {10^{ - 5}}\,T$. Then find $N.$

question: Shown in the figure is a conductor carrying a current ' ${I}$ '. The magnetic field intensity at the common centre of all the three arcs (point $O$) is
explanation:
Magnetic field intensity at $O = {B_{AB}} + {B_{BC}} + {B_{CD}} + {B_{DE}} + {B_{EF}} + {B_{FG}}$
$ = 0 + \frac{{ - {\mu _0}/\theta }}{{4\pi (3r)}} + 0 + \frac{{{\mu _0}/\theta }}{{\pi (2r)}} + 0 - \frac{{{\mu _0}I\theta }}{{\pi r}}$
$ = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{4\pi }}\theta \left[ { - \frac{1}{{3r}} + \frac{1}{{2r}} - \frac{1}{r}} \right]$
$ = - \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi r}}I\theta \times \frac{5}{{6r}} = - \frac{{5{\mu _0}I\theta }}{{24\pi r}}$
$\therefore \,\,\,\left| B \right| = \frac{{5{\mu _0}I\theta }}{{24\pi r}}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950599/preptracker/questions/q_43516_explanation_1764950599008.png
So correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

explanation:
A moving charge produces both electric and magnetic field.
So correct option is (3)
