question: In the hydrogen atom, the electron is making $6.6 \times {10^{15}}rps$. If the radius of the orbit is $0.53 \times {10^{ - 10}}\,m$, then magnetic field produced at the centre of the orbit is
explanation:
Here, $v = 6.6 \times {10^{15}}rps = 6.6 \times {10^{15}}\,Hz$
$q = e = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}C,r = 0.53 \times {10^{ - 10}}\,m$
Magnetic field, $B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} q v}{2 r} \quad(\because I=q v)$
$ = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 6.6 \times {{10}^{15}}}}{{2 \times 0.53 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}$
$ = 12.5\,T$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Rotating Charge

question: Figure shows a straight wire of length $l$ carrying current $i$. The magnitude of magnetic field produced by the current at point $P$ is
explanation:
The given situation can be redrawn as follows. As we know the general formula for finding the magnetic field due to a finite length of wire https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957114/preptracker/questions/q_43492_explanation_1764957114016.png $B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i}{r}\left(\sin \phi_{1}+\sin \phi_{2}\right)$
Here, $\phi_{1}=0^{\circ}, \phi=45^{\circ}$
$\therefore \quad B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i}{r}\left(\sin 0^{\circ}+\sin 45^{\circ}\right)$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i}{\sqrt{2} l}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2} \mu_{0} i}{8 \pi l}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field due to a finite sized current wire/ closed coil in the form of Triangle, Rectangle etc.

question: Magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop of area $A$ is $B$. The magnetic moment of the loop will be
explanation:
Magnetic field at the centre of the circular loop is given by
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r} ; I=\dfrac{2 B r}{\mu_{0}}$
Also, $A=\pi r^{2} ; r=\sqrt{\dfrac{A}{\pi}}$
Magnetic moment of the loop, $M=I A$
or, $M=\dfrac{2 B r}{\mu_{0}} A=\dfrac{2 B}{\mu_{0}} \sqrt{\dfrac{A}{\pi}} A$
$M=\dfrac{2 B A^{3 / 2}}{\mu_{0} \pi^{1 / 2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

subject: Physics

question: In the figure shown, the magnetic field induction at the point $O$ will be
explanation:
The magnetic field induction at $O$ is
$B=B_{C D}+B_{D E F}+B_{F G}$
Here, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950577/preptracker/questions/q_43456_explanation_1764950576220.png
$B_{C D}=B_{F G}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{i}{r}\left(\sin 90^{\circ}+\sin 0^{\circ}\right)=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{i}{r} \odot$
and $B_{D E F}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{i \pi}{r} \odot$
$\therefore B = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{i}{r} + \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{i\pi }}{r} + \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{i}{r} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{i}{r}(\pi + 2)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Combination of Long Straight and Circular Current Wires

question: In a hydrogen atom, an electron is revolving in the orbit of radius $0.53{\rm{ }}A^\circ $ with $6.6 \times 10^{15}$ revolutions per second. Magnetic field produced at the centre of the orbit is
explanation:
The revolving electron is equivalent lo a current loop.
If $u$ is the frequency of revolution of the electron, then the current in the loop is
${\mkern 1mu} I = ev\quad \quad \quad (1)$
The magnetic field produced at the centre of the orbit is
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2r}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}ev}}{{2r}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( {{\text{using}}(1)} \right)$
Here, ${\mu _0} = 4\pi \times {10^{ - 7}}\,Wb\,{A^{ - 1}}\,{m^{ - 1}}$
$c{\rm{ }} = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}C$
$v{\rm{ }} = 6.6 \times {10^{15}}rps$
$r = 0.53{\rm{ }}A^\circ {\rm{ }} = 0.53 \times {10^{ - 10}}m$
$\therefore \quad B{\rm{ }} = \frac{{\left( {4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}}} \right)\left( {1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}} \right)\left( {6.6 \times {{10}^{15}}} \right)}}{{2\left( {0.53 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}} \right)}}$
$ = 12.5\,Wb\,{m^{ - 2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Rotating Charge

$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I \theta}{4 \pi r}$

question: The incorrect statement regarding the lines of force of the magnetic field $B$ is
explanation: Inside a magnet, its magnetic lines of force move from south pole of a magnet towards its north pole.

magnetic lines of force form a close curve.

question: A circular current carrying coil has a radius $R$. The distance from the centre of the coil on the axis, where the magnetic induction Will be ${\left( {\frac{1}{8}} \right)^{th}}$ to its value at the centre of the coil is
explanation:
The magnetic induction at the centre of a circular current $I$ carrying coil of radius $R$ is
$B_{\text {centre }}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}$
The magnetic induction at a point on the axis of the coil at a distance $x$ from the centre is
$B_{\text {axis }}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}$
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{B_{\text {centre }}}{B_{\text {axis }}}=\dfrac{\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{R^{3}}=\left(1+\dfrac{x^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)^{3 / 2}$
As $B_{\text {axis }}=\dfrac{1}{8} B_{\text {centre }}$ (given)
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{8}{1}=\left(1+\dfrac{x^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)^{3 / 2}$
$4=1+\dfrac{x^{2}}{R^{2}}$ or $3=\dfrac{x^{2}}{R^{2}}$
$x^{2}=3 R^{2}$ or $x=\sqrt{3} R$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Current Carrying Circular Loop

question: For a circular current carrying loop having a magnetic moment $\vec m$, the magnetic field  $\vec{B}$, at a distance $x$ on the axis of the loop  $(x >  > R)$  is
explanation:
For an electric dipole, the electric field on the axis of the dipole is $\vec{E}=\dfrac{2 K \vec{P}}{x^{3}}$
Here by analogy, we replace $\vec{E}$ by $\vec{B}, K$ by $\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}$ and $\vec{P}$ by $\vec{m}$.

question: A particle is moving with velocity $\vec v = \widehat i + 3\widehat j$ and it produces an electric field at a point given by $\vec E = 2\widehat k$. It will produce magnetic field at that point equal to (all quantities are in SI units)
explanation:
$\vec B = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}q\frac{{\vec v \times \vec r}}{{{r^3}}}$ and $\vec E = \frac{1}{{4\pi { \in _0}}}\frac{{q\vec r}}{{{r^3}}}$
$\therefore \mu_{0} \in_{0}(\vec{v} \times \vec{E})=\dfrac{\vec{v} \times \vec{E}}{c^{2}}=\dfrac{(\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) \times 2 \hat{k}}{c^{2}}$
$ = \frac{{ - 2\hat j + 6\hat i}}{{{c^2}}} = \frac{{6\hat i - 2\hat j}}{{{c^2}}}$
$\left[ {c = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{\mu _0}{\smallint _0}} }} \Rightarrow {\mu _0}{ \in _0} = \frac{1}{{{c^2}}}} \right]$

options.text: cannot be determined from the given data

question: Calculate the magnetic field at the centre of a coil in the form of a square of side 2 a carrying a current $I$.
explanation:
The current carrying coil $ABCD$ may be assumed to be made of four current-carrying conductors $AB,\,\,BC,\,\,CD$ and $DA$.Magnetic field at $O$ due to current-carrying conductor $AB$ is :
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{4\pi a}}\left[ {\sin 45^\circ + \sin 45^\circ } \right]$
$ = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{4\pi a}}2\sin 45^\circ $
or $B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a} \times 2 \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
or $B = \frac{{\sqrt 2 {\mu _0}I}}{{4\pi a}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950574/preptracker/questions/q_43410_explanation_1764950573990.png Total magnetic field at $O$
${B^\prime } = 4\,B = 4 \times \frac{{\sqrt 2 {\mu _0}I}}{{4\pi a}} = \frac{{\sqrt 2 {\mu _0}I}}{{\pi a}}$
subject: Physics

question: A horizontal overhead powerline is at height of $4\,m$ from the ground and carries a current of  $100\,A$  from east to west. The magnetic field directly below it on the ground is (${\mu _0} = 4\pi  \times {10^{ - 7}}T\,m{A^{ - 1}}$)
explanation:
The magnetic field is
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{2I}}{r} = {10^{ - 7}} \times \frac{{2 \times 100}}{4} = 5 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,T$

question: Magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop of area $A$ is $B$. The magnetic moment of the loop will be
explanation:
Magnetic field at the centre of circular loop,
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{2 \pi I}{r}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r} \Rightarrow I=\dfrac{2 B r}{\mu_{0}}$
Also, $A=\pi r^{2}$ or $r=(A / \pi)^{1 / 2}$
Magnetic moment, $M=I A=\dfrac{2 B r}{\mu_{0}} A$
$=\dfrac{2 B A}{\mu_{0}} \times\left(\dfrac{A}{\pi}\right)^{1 / 2}=\dfrac{2 B A^{3 / 2}}{\mu_{0} \pi^{1 / 2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: A vertical straight conductor carries a current upward. A point $P$ lies to the East of it at a small distance and another point $Q$ lies to the West at the same distance. The magnetic field at $P$ is
explanation: Magnitude of magnetic field at any point due to a straight current carrying conductor depends only on the distance of the point with respect to current carrying conductor but not on the direction. Hence, $B_{P}=B_{Q}$ (at same distance).

question: Electron revolving with speed $v$ is producing magnetic field $B$ at centre. Find the relation between radius of path, $B$ and $v$.
explanation:
$\because$ Magnetic field at centre, $B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}$
where, $I=\dfrac{e}{t}$ or $I=\dfrac{e}{\left(\dfrac{2 \pi r}{v}\right)}=\dfrac{e v}{2 \pi r}$
Hence, $\quad B=\left(\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 r}\right)\left(\dfrac{e v}{2 \pi r}\right)=\dfrac{\mu_{0} e v}{4 \pi r^{2}}$
or $\quad B \propto \dfrac{v}{r^{2}}$
subject: Physics

question: Two concentric circular loops of radii $R$ and $2 R$ carry currents of $2 i$ and $i$ respectively, in opposite sense ($i.\,e.,$  clockwise in one coil and counter clockwise in the other coil). The resultant magnetic field at their common centre is
explanation:
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \times 2 i}{2 \times R}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950546/preptracker/questions/q_43297_explanation_1764950545966.png
Similarly, $B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4 R}$
$\therefore \quad B_{\text {net }}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{R}-\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4 R}=\dfrac{3 \mu_{0} i}{4 R}$

question: For the magnetic field to be maximum due to a small element of current carrying conductor at a point, the angle between the element and the line joining the element to the given point must be
explanation:
The magnetic field due to small element conductor of length is given by
$d B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i d l \sin \theta}{r^{2}}$
This value will be maximum when
$\begin{aligned}\sin \theta & =1=\sin 90^{\circ} \\\theta & =90^{\circ}\end{aligned}$

question: A long wire having a semicircular loop of radius $r$ carries a current $i$ as shown in figure. The magnetic induction at the centre $O$ due to entire wire is
explanation:
According to Biot-Savart's law, the magnetic induction at a point to a current carrying element $i \delta l$ is given by
$\delta B = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }} \cdot \frac{{i\delta 1 \times r}}{{{r^3}}}$
or $B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i \delta l \sin \theta}{r^{2}}$
Directed normal to plane containing $\delta 1$ and $r,\theta $ being angle between $\delta 1$ and $r$.
Field due to semicircular arc
Now, angle between a current element $\delta 1$ of semicircular arc and the radius vector of the element to point $c$ is $\pi / 2$.
Therefore, the magnitude of magnetic induction $B$ at $O$ due to this element is
$\delta B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i \delta l \sin \pi / 2}{r^{2}}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i \delta l}{4 \pi r^{2}}$
Hence, magnetic induction due to whole semicircular loop is
$\begin{aligned}B & =\Sigma \delta B=\Sigma \dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i \delta l}{r^{2}} \\& =\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{i}{r^{2}} \Sigma \delta l \\& =\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4 \pi r^{2}}(\pi r)=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4 r}\end{aligned}$
The magnetic field due to $a b$ and $d e$ is zero, because $\theta=0^{\circ}$ or $180^{\circ}$. So, net magnetic field is
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4 r}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Combination of Long Straight and Circular Current Wires

question: The position of point ${P}$ from wire ${B}$ where net magnetic field is zero, due to following current distribution, will be
explanation:

${\dfrac{5}{1}=\dfrac{6-x}{x}}$
${5 x=6-x}$
${x=1 {~cm}}$
Thus ${B}$ will be zero at point ${P}$ at a distance ${1 cm}$ from wire carrying ${2.5 A}$ current.

options.text: $5\,cm$ at a point lying outside the wires

question: In the given figure, magnetic field at the center of ring ${(O)}$ is ${8 \sqrt{2} T}$. Now, it is turned through a ${90^{\circ}}$ angle about ${x x^{\prime}}$ axis, so that two semicircular parts are mutually perpendicular. Find the value of magnetic field (in $T\,)$  at centre
explanation:

question: An electron is moving at ${10^{6} {~m} / {s}}$ in a direction parallel to a current of $5\,A$ flowing through an infinite long straight wire separated by a perpendicular distance of $10\,cm$ in air. Magnetic force experienced by the electron
explanation:
Magnetic field of wire is perpendicular to the direction of motion of electron. So magnetic force on electron. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950537/preptracker/questions/q_43234_explanation_1764950536892.png ${F_{m}=q v B\left(o=90^{\circ}\right)}$,
${=q v\left(\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi d}\right) \quad\left(\because B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi d}\right)}$
${=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{6} \times\left(\dfrac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{10 \times 10^{-2}}\right)}$
${=1 \cdot 6 \times 10^{-18} N}$

question: A coil having 9 turns carrying a current produces magnetic filed ${B_{1}}$ at the centre. Now the coil is rewounded into 3 turns carrying same current. Then the magnetic field at the centre ${B_{2}=}$
explanation:
Magnetic at the centre of current carrying circular coil is given by

${B_1} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}i}}{{2R}} = \frac{{9{\mu _0}i}}{{2R}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950534/preptracker/questions/q_43211_explanation_1764950534281.png Similarly, ${B_2} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{N_2}i}}{{2r}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{2R}}$
$\frac{{{B_1}}}{{{B_2}}} = \frac{{\frac{{9{\mu _0}i}}{{2R}}}}{{\frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{2R}}}} = 9 \Rightarrow {B_1} = 9{B_2}$
$\therefore {B_2} = \frac{{{B_1}}}{9}$

question: A long conducting wire having a current $I$ flowing through it. It is bent into a circular coil of $N$ turns. Then it is bent into a circular coil of $n$ turns. The magnetic field is calculated at the centre of coils in both the cases. The ratio of the magnetic field in first case to that of second case is
explanation:
Magnetic field at the centre of the circular coil having $N$ turns
${B_{1({\rm{ centre }})}} = \frac{{N{\mu _0}I}}{{2R}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950530/preptracker/questions/q_43195_explanation_1764950529660.png Magnetic field at the centre of the circular coil having $n$ turns
${B_{2{\rm{ (centre) }}}} = \frac{{n{\mu _0}I}}{{2r}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
As length of the conductor remains constant
$\therefore l = N2\pi R = n2\pi r\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950531/preptracker/questions/q_43195_explanation_1764950530675.png Using (3) in (1) and (2)
$B_{1}=\dfrac{N \mu_{0} I}{2 R}=\dfrac{N \mu_{0} I}{2\left(\dfrac{l}{2 \pi N}\right)}$ or
$B_{2}=\dfrac{n \mu_{0} I}{2 r}=\dfrac{n \mu_{0} I}{2\left(\dfrac{l}{2 \pi n}\right)}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=\dfrac{N^{2}}{n^{2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question:
As shown in the figure, a long straight conductor with semi-circular arc of radius $\dfrac{\pi}{10} m$ is carrying current $I = 3\,A$. The magnitude of the magnetic field at the center $O$ of the arc is

explanation:
$I=3 A, r=\dfrac{\pi}{10} m$

$B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{\pi I}{r}=10^{-7} \times \dfrac{\pi \times 3}{\pi} \times 10=3 \times 10^{-6} T$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950528/preptracker/questions/q_43187_explanation_1764950527479.png $B_{\text {net }}=B_{2}=3 \mu T$. So, correct option is (3).

question: As shown in the figure, a current of $2 A$ flowing in an equilateral triangle of side $4\sqrt 3 \,cm$. The magnetic field at the centroid $O$ of the triangle is (Neglect the effect of earth's magnetic field)
explanation:
$\triangle B O D$
$\tan 60^{\circ}=\dfrac{2 \sqrt{3}}{d} ; d=2 \mathrm{~cm}$
Magnetic field due to $B A=B_{1}$
Magnetic field due to triangular loop is $3 B_{1}$
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{I}{d}\left[\sin 60^{\circ}+\sin 60^{\circ}\right]$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950520/preptracker/questions/q_43148_explanation_1764950520439.png $B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{2}{2} \times 2 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 100=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \sqrt{3}}{4 \pi} \times 100$
Net magnetic field,
${B_{net{\rm{ }}}} = 3{B_1} = 3\sqrt 3 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,T$
So, correct option is (4).

question: The electric current in a circular coil of four turns produces a magnetic induction $32 T$ at its centre. The coil is unwound and is rewound into a circular coil of single by the same current will be
explanation:
Given: $N=4, B=32 T, B^{\prime}=$ ?,
$N=1$
Let the radius of smaller coil is $r$ and bigger coil is $R$.
Total circumference of smaller coil of 4 turns $=$ circumference of Bigger coil
$2 \pi r N=2 \pi R$
$4 r=R$
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi I}{r}$
so, $32 = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }} \cdot \frac{{2\pi I}}{r} \times 4\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
and ${B^\prime } = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }} \cdot \frac{{2\pi I}}{R} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }} \cdot \frac{{2\pi I}}{{4r}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Divide equation (2) by (1), we get
$\dfrac{B^{\prime}}{32}=\dfrac{r}{4 r \times 4} \Rightarrow B^{\prime}=2 T$
subject: Physics

explanation:

$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi(a / 2)} \cdot\left(\sin \dfrac{\pi}{2}+\sin 0^{\circ}\right)=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi a}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956391/preptracker/questions/q_43124_explanation_1764956391409.png $B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi(a / 2)} \cdot\left(\sin \dfrac{\pi}{2}+\sin 0^{\circ}\right)=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi a}$

So, $B_{\text {net }}=B_{1}+B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{\pi a}$

question: Match Column I with Column II.Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
explanation:
$(A)-(R)$
(A) $B_{1}$ (due to circular coil)
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi I}{r}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r} \otimes$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956385/preptracker/questions/q_43108_explanation_1764956385070.png Net magnetic field at $O$
$B_{\text {net }}=B_{1}-B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}-\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}\left[1-\dfrac{1}{\pi}\right]$
$B_{\text {net }}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}\left(\dfrac{\pi-1}{\pi}\right) \otimes$
$(B)-(P)$
(B) $B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{I}{r} \odot$
$B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{\pi I}{r}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 r} \odot$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956386/preptracker/questions/q_43108_explanation_1764956385758.png $B_{3}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{I}{r} \odot$
Net magnetic field at $O$
$B_{\text {net }}=B_{1}+B_{2}+B_{3}$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4} \dfrac{I}{r}\left[\dfrac{1}{\pi}+1+\dfrac{1}{\pi}\right]$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 r}\left[\dfrac{2}{\pi}+1\right]$
$B_{\text {net }} \dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 r}\left(\dfrac{2+\pi}{\pi}\right)$
(C) - (S)
(C) Net magnetic field at $O$
$B_{1}=0, B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{\pi I}{r}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 r} \otimes$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956387/preptracker/questions/q_43108_explanation_1764956386994.png $B_{3}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{I}{r} \otimes$
$B_{\text {net }}=B_{2}+B_{3}+B_{1} \dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 r}\left[1+\dfrac{1}{\pi}\right]$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I(\pi+1)}{4 \pi r} \otimes$
(D) - (R)
(D) $B_{1}=B_{3}=0$
$B_{\text {net }}=B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{\pi I}{r}$
$\Rightarrow B_{n e t}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 r}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956388/preptracker/questions/q_43108_explanation_1764956387992.png (A)-(R), (B)-(P), (C)-(S), (D)-(Q).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Combination of Long Straight and Circular Current Wires

question: A circular loop of radius $r$ is carrying current 1$A$. The ratio of magnetic field at the centre of circular loop and at a distance $r$ from the centre of the loop on its axis is
explanation:
Magnetic field on the axis of the loop is given by,
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I r^{2}}{2\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}$
Where, $r=$ radius of the loop
$x=$ distance from the centre.
At the centre $x=0 ; B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}$
At, $x=r ; B^{\prime}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I r^{2}}{2\left(r^{2}+r^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I r^{2}}{2\left(2 r^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}=\dfrac{\mu \cdot I}{(2 r) 2^{3 / 2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{B}{B^{\prime}}=2^{3 / 2}=2 \sqrt{2}: 1$
So option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Magnetic field due to an infinite straight conductor varies inversely as the distance from it.

Reason: The magnetic field at the centre of the circular coil is zero.
explanation:
At distance d from an infinitely long current carrying conductor,
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}\left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{d}\right) \Rightarrow B \propto \dfrac{1}{d}$
Right hand thumb rule implies that it is in the from of concentric circles whose centre lie on that it is in the from of concentric circles whose centres lie on that straight conductor https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956378/preptracker/questions/q_42977_explanation_1764956377574.png But magnetic field at centres of circular loop (of radius $r$)
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \pi}{2 \pi} \neq 0$
So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: Figure shows a coil of radius $2\,cm$ concentricwith a coil of radius $4\,cm$. Each coil has 1000 turns with a current of $5\,amp$. In larger coil, then the current needed in the smaller coil to give the total magnetic field at the centre equal to $2\,mT$ is
explanation:
$\dfrac{\mu_{0} n i_{2}}{2 r}-\dfrac{\mu_{0} n i_{1}}{4 r}=2 \times 10^{-3}$
$\dfrac{\mu_{0} n}{2 r}\left[i_{2}-\dfrac{i_{1}}{2}\right]=2 \times 10^{-3}$
$\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1000}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-2}}\left(i_{2}-\dfrac{i_{1}}{2}\right)=2 \times 10^{-3}$
${i_2} - \frac{{{i_1}}}{2} = \frac{1}{{5\pi }}$
$ \Rightarrow {i_2} = 2.56\,A.$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: In the given figure the magnetic induction at the point $O$ is
explanation: $B=B_{1}+B_{2} \quad B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 r}+\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi r}$

question: A current of $30\,amp$. is flowing in a conductor as shown in fig. The magnetic induction at point $O$ will be-
explanation: The magnetic field due to a current-carrying arc at its center is given by $B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I \theta}{4 \pi r}$, where $\mu_{0}$ is the permeability of free space, $I$ is the current, $\theta$ is the angle in radians, and $r$ is the radius of the arc. With $I=30 A, \theta=\dfrac{3 \pi}{2}$ and $r=0.03 \mathrm{~m}$ we can calculate $B$. Let's perform the calculation. The magnetic induction at point $\mathrm{O}$ due to the current-carrying arc is $1.57 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{Telsa}$, which corresponds to option (2), and justifies that the field strength is determined by the arc's current, angle, and radius.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Combination of Long Straight and Circular Current Wires

question: The magnetic induction at centre $O$ due to the arrangement shown in fig.
explanation: $\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot i}{R}+\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i}{R} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i}{R}(\pi+1)$
subject: Physics

question: Two concentric circular coils $A$ and $B$ have radii $25\,cm$ and $15\,cm$ and carry currents $10\,A$ and $15\,A$ respectively. $A$ has 24 turns and $B$ has 18 turns. The direction of currents are in oppositeorder. The magnetic induction at the common centre of the coil is
explanation:
The magnetic field at the center of a single circular coil is given by $B=\mu_{0} \dfrac{N I}{2 R}$, where $\mu_{0}$ is the permeability of free space, $N$ is the number of turns, $I$ is the current, and $R$ is the radius.
For coil A: $B_{A}=\mu_{0} \dfrac{24 \times 10}{2 \times 25}$ and
For coil B: $B_{B}=\mu_{0} \dfrac{18 \times 15}{2 \times 15}$
The coils have opposite currents, so their fields at the center will subtract. The net field is
$B_{\text {net }}=B_{A}-B_{B}$.
The calculation yields a value of $-4.2 \mu_{0}$.
However, since we're dealing with magnitudes and the direction of currents are opposite, we take the absolute value. Therefore, the correct magnetic induction at the common center of the coils is $4.2 \mu_{0}$. Correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: $A$ and $B$ are the two concentric circular conductors of center $O$ and carrying currents $I_{1}$ and $I_{2}$ as shown in the adjacent figure. If ratio of their radii is $1: 2$ and ratio of the flux densities at $O$ due to $A$ and $B$ is $1: 3$ then the value of $I_{1} /$ $I_{2}$ is

question: Two similar coils of radius $R$ and number of turns $N$ are lying concentrically with their planes at right angles to each other. The currents flowing in them are $I\,\& \,I\sqrt 3 $ respectively. Find the magnetic field induction at the center of the coils.
explanation:
$B=\sqrt{B_{1}^{2}+B_{2}^{2}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950460/preptracker/questions/q_42837_explanation_1764950459889.png $B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 R} \sqrt{1+3}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N I \times 2}{2 R}$
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N I}{R}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: A current is flowing in a hexagonal coil of side a shown in figure. Find the magnetic field induction at the centre of the coil.
explanation:
Here $\angle E O D=60^{\circ}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957101/preptracker/questions/q_42833_explanation_1764957101369.png $\begin{aligned}& \angle E O N=30^{\circ}=\angle N O D \\& O E=O D=E D=a \\& O N=O E \cos 30^{\circ}=a \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\end{aligned}$
Total magnetic field induction at $O$ due to current through all the six sides of hexagonal is
$B=6 \times \dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{I}{a \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}\left(\sin 30^{\circ}+\sin 30^{\circ}\right)$
$\Rightarrow B=\dfrac{\sqrt{3} \mu_{0} I}{\pi a}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field due to a finite sized current wire/ closed coil in the form of Triangle, Rectangle etc.

question: The magnetic field at the centre of square of side $a$ is
explanation:
Magnetic field,
$B = 2\left[ \begin{array}{l} \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{2I/3}}{{a/2}}\left( {\sin 45^\circ + \sin 45^\circ } \right) - \\ \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }} \cdot \frac{{I/3}}{{a/2}}\left( {\sin 45^\circ + \sin 45^\circ } \right) \end{array} \right]$
$ = \frac{{\sqrt 2 {\mu _0}I}}{{3\pi a}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950456/preptracker/questions/q_42829_explanation_1764950455622.png
subject: Physics

question: The magnetic field due to a long straight wire carrying a current $I$ is proportional to:
explanation: $B=\dfrac{I d \ell \sin \theta}{r^{2}} \times \dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \quad B \propto I$

subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: A long straight wire in the horizontal plane carries a current of $75\,A$ in north of south direction, magnitude and direction of field $B$ at a point $3\,m$ east of the wire is
explanation:
From Ampere circuital law
$\begin{aligned}& \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I_{e n c} \\& B \times 2 \pi R=\mu_{0} I_{e n c}\end{aligned}$
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{e n c}}{2 \pi R}=2 \times 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{75}{3}=5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~T}$
The direction of field at the given point will be vertical up determined by the screw rule or right hand rule.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: Two circular loops having same radius $(R = 10\,cm)$ and same current $\frac{7}{2}\,A$ are placed along same axis as shown in the figure. If distance between their centres is $10\,cm$, find the value of net magnetic field at point $P$
explanation:
Given, radius of identical circular loops, $R = 10\,cm = {10^{ - 1}}\,m$
Current, $I=\dfrac{7}{2} A$
Distance from both circular loop at point $P$,
${x_1} = {x_2} = x = 5\,cm$
$ = 5 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m$
From figure, according to Maxwell's right hand thumb rule, it is clear that magnetic field will be in same direction by both the coils,
i.e. $B=B_{1}+B_{2}$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}+\dfrac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}$
$\begin{aligned}& =\dfrac{\mu_{0} \times 7 / 2 \times\left(10^{-1}\right)^{2}}{\left[(0.1)^{2}+(0.05)^{2}\right]^{3 / 2}} \\& =\dfrac{\mu_{0} \times 7 \times 10^{-2}}{2\left(10^{-2}+25 \times 10^{-4}\right)^{3 / 2}}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \times 7 \times 10^{-2}}{2\left(125 \times 10^{-4}\right)^{3 / 2}} \\& =\dfrac{\mu_{0} \times 7 \times 10^{4}}{2 \times 5 \sqrt{5}}=\dfrac{7000}{\sqrt{5}} \mu_{0}\end{aligned}$
So, correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Current Carrying Circular Loop

question: A long straight wire, carrying current $I$ is bent at its mid-point to form an angle of $45^{\circ}$. Induction of magnetic field (in tesla) at point $P$, distant $R$ from point of bending is equal to
explanation:
$\because B($ at $P)=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi d}\left(\cos \theta_{1}-\cos \theta_{2}\right)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950439/preptracker/questions/q_42746_explanation_1764950439264.png In given case, $d=R \sin 45^{\circ}=R / \sqrt{2}$
$\theta_{1}=135^{\circ}, \theta_{2}=180^{\circ}$
$\therefore B($ at $P)$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi(R / \sqrt{2})}\left[\cos 135^{\circ}-\cos 180^{\circ}\right]$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi R} \sqrt{2}\left[\dfrac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}-(-1)\right]$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi R} \sqrt{2}\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{2}-1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$
or $B($ at $P)=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi R}(\sqrt{2}-1) T$
So, correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: A square conducting loop of side length $L$ carries a current $I$. The magnetic field at the centre of the loop is
explanation:
Magnetic field at the centre due to either arm https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950438/preptracker/questions/q_42739_explanation_1764950437595.png $\begin{aligned}& B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{I}{(L / 2)}\left[\sin 45^{\circ}+\sin 45^{\circ}\right] \\& =\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{2 \sqrt{2} I}{L}\end{aligned}$
Field at centre due to the four arms of the square,
$\begin{aligned}& B=4 B_{1} \\& =4\left(\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{2 \sqrt{2} I}{L}\right) \\& =\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{\pi} \times \dfrac{2 \sqrt{2} I}{L}\end{aligned}$
i.e. $B \propto 1 / L$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field due to a finite sized current wire/ closed coil in the form of Triangle, Rectangle etc.

question: Two parallel infinitely long current carrying wires are shown in the figure. If resultant magnetic field at point $A$ is zero, then determine the value of current $I$.
explanation:
Resultant magnetic field due to current carrying parallel wire $1(10 A)$ and wire $2(I A)$ at point $A$ is zero. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957096/preptracker/questions/q_42735_explanation_1764957095950.png i.e. $B_{1}=B_{2}$
$\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 r_{1}}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{2}}{2 r_{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \dfrac{I_{1}}{r_{1}}=\dfrac{I_{2}}{r_{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{10}{9 \times 10^{-2}}=\dfrac{I}{27 \times 10^{-2}}$
$\Rightarrow I=30 A$
So, correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: Two concentric coils each of radius equal to $2\,\pi \,cm$ are placed at right angles to each other. $3\,A$ and $4\,A$ are the current flowing in each coil, respectively. The magnetic induction (in $Wb{m^{ - 2}}$) at the centre of the coils will be (Take, ${\mu _0} = 4\pi  \times {10^{ - 7}}\,Wb\,{A^{ - 1}}\,{m^{ - 1}}$).
explanation:
$B_{P}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{2}}{2 R}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950436/preptracker/questions/q_42731_explanation_1764950436145.png $=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 4}{2 \times 0.02 \pi}$
$ = 4 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,Wb\,{\rm{ - }}\,{m^{ - 2}}$
and $B_{Q}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 R}=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3}{2 \times 0.02 \pi}$
$ = 3 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,Wb\,{m^{ - 2}}$
$\therefore B=\sqrt{B_{P}^{2}+B_{Q}^{2}}$
$=\sqrt{\left(4 \times 10^{-5}\right)^{2}+\left(3 \times 10^{-5}\right)^{2}}$
$ = 5 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,Wb\,{m^{ - 2}}$
So, correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: A very long conducting wire is bent in a semicircular shape from $A$ to $B$ as shown in figure. The magnetic field at point $P$ for steady current configuration is given by:
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950435/preptracker/questions/q_42723_explanation_1764950434602.png The magnetic field at point $'P'$.
$B = {B_1} + {B_2} + {B_3}$
Where ‘Semicircular coil’ contributes
${B_1} = \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{2\pi R}} \times \frac{\pi }{2} = \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{4R}} \odot $
Where $ \odot $ indicates ${{\vec B}_1}$ that is ‘out of page’ and each to long wire contributes
${B_2} = {B_3} = \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{4\pi R}} \otimes $
Where $ \otimes $ indicates ‘into page’.
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{4\,R}} - \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{4\pi R}} - \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{4\,\pi R}}$
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{4R}}\left[ {1 - \frac{2}{\pi }} \right]$
As$\left( {\frac{1}{4}} \right)$is greater than$\left( {\frac{1}{{2\,\pi }}} \right)$, $B$ is ‘out of page’.
Correct option is (2).

options.text: $\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4 R}$ pointed away from the page
$\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4 R}\left[1-\dfrac{2}{\pi}\right]$ pointed away from page
$\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4 R}\left[1-\dfrac{2}{\pi}\right]$ pointed into the page
$\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4 R}$ pointed into the page

subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Combination of Long Straight and Circular Current Wires

question: The magnetic field at the centre of a circular coil of radius $r$, due to current I flowing through it, is $B$. The magnetic field at a point along the axis at a distance $\dfrac{r}{2}$ from the centre is:
explanation:
Use subscript $C$ for centre, a for axis.
Here coil has one loop $\mathrm{n}=1$.
$B_{c}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}$
$B_{a}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I r^{2}}{2\left(X^{2}+r^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}$
At $x=\dfrac{r}{2}$
$B_{a}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I r^{2}}{2\left(\dfrac{r^{2}}{4}+r^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I r^{2}}{2\left(\dfrac{5}{4} r^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}\left(\dfrac{4}{5}\right)^{3 / 2}$
${B_a} = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2r}}{\left( {\frac{2}{{\sqrt 5 }}} \right)^3} = {B_C}{\left[ {\frac{2}{{\sqrt 5 }}} \right]^3}$
Note: in question ${B_C}$ is stated as $B$.

question: A square conducting loop of side length $L$ carries a current $I$. The magnetic field at the centre of the loop is
explanation:
Magnetic field at the centre of due to either arms
$\begin{aligned}& B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{1}{(L / 2)}\left[\sin 45^{\circ}+\sin 45^{\circ}\right] \\& =\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{2 \sqrt{2} I}{L}\end{aligned}$
Field at centre due to the four arms of the square

$ \Rightarrow {10^{ - 6}} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 7}} \times 2 \times i}}{{0.02}}$
$ \Rightarrow i = 0.1A$

question: An equilateral triangle is made by uniform wires $AB$, $BC$, $CA$. A current $I$ enters at $A$ and leaves from the mid point of $BC$. If the lengths of each side of the triangle is $L$. The magnetic field $B$ at the centroid $O$ of the triangle is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950410/preptracker/questions/q_42566_explanation_1764950410357.png $\begin{aligned}& \vec{B}_{1}+\vec{B}_{2}=0 \\& B_{1} \rightarrow \text { due to left part } \\& B_{2} \rightarrow \text { due right part }\end{aligned}$

question: A wire of finite length carries a steady current. It is first bent to form a circular coil of one turn. The same length is now bent more sharply to give a loop of two turns of smaller radius. The magnetic field at the centre caused by the same current now will be
explanation:
$r=\dfrac{L}{2 \pi} ; r^{\prime}=\dfrac{L}{2(2 \pi)}=\dfrac{r}{2}$
Magnetic field due to I case loop $B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i}{2 r}$
now radius is halved so with the same wire 2 loops will form
$\therefore$ $B$ in II case $B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i}{2\left(\dfrac{r}{2}\right)} \times$ no. of turns $=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i}{r} \cdot 2$
$\therefore B_{2}=4 B_{1}$

options.text: A quarter of its first value
Same as that of the first value

question: The magnetic field due to a current carrying circular loop of radius 3$cm$ at a point on the axis at a distance of 4$cm$ from the centre is $54\mu T$. What will be its value at the centre of the loop?
explanation:
As magnetic field at a point on the axis of current carrying loop is given by
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}i{r^2}}}{{2{{\left( {{r^2} + {d^2}} \right)}^{3/2}}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Where, $d=$ distance on axis
Now, magnetic field at centre
${\mathop{\rm Now}\nolimits} ,\,{B_1} = \frac{{{\mu _0}i{r^2}}}{{2{{\left( {{r^2} + {0^2}} \right)}^{3/2}}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}i{r^2}}}{{2{r^3}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Dividing eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$\begin{gathered}\dfrac{B}{B_{1}}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i r^{2} \times 2 \times\left(r^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{2\left(r^{2}+d^{2}\right)^{3 / 2} \times \mu_{0} i r^{2}} \\\Rightarrow \dfrac{B}{B_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{r^{2}}{r^{2}+d^{2}}\right)^{3 / 2} \Rightarrow \dfrac{54 \mu T}{B_{1}}=\dfrac{27}{5^{3}} \\B_{1}=\dfrac{54 \times 125}{27} \Rightarrow B_{1}=250 \mu T\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: A part of a long wire carrying a current is bent into a circle of radius $r$ as shown in figure. The net magnetic field at the centre $O$ of the circular loop is
explanation:
The magnitude of the magnetic field at point $O$ due to straight part of wire is
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 \pi r}(\text { Upward })$
The field at the centre $O$ due to the current loop of radius $r$ is
$B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 r}$
$\therefore$ Resultant field at $O$ is $B=B_{1}+B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 r}\left(\dfrac{1}{\pi}+1\right)=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 \pi r}(\pi+1)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: The magnetic induction at the centre $O$ in the figure shown is
explanation:
In the following figure, magnetic field at $O$ due to sections 1,2,3 and 4 are considered as $B_{1}, B_{2}, B_{3}$ and $B_{4}$ respectively https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957100/preptracker/questions/q_42550_explanation_1764957099705.png $\begin{aligned}& B_{1}=B_{3}=0 \\& B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{\pi i}{R_{1}} \otimes \\& B_{4}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{\pi i}{R_{2}} \odot\end{aligned}$
As $\quad\left|B_{2}\right|>\left|B_{4}\right|$
So $\quad B_{n e t}=B_{2}-B_{4} \Rightarrow B_{n e t}=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i}{4}\left(\dfrac{1}{R_{1}}-\dfrac{1}{R_{2}}\right) \otimes$

question: An arc of a circle of radius $R$ subtends an angle $\pi / 2$ at the centre. It carries a current $i$. The magnetic field at the centre will be
explanation: $B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{\theta i}{r}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{\pi}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{R}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{8 R}$

question: Current ' $I$ ' is flowing in a conductor shaped as shown in the figure. The radius of the curved part is $r$ and the length of straight portion is very large. The value of the magnetic field at the centre $O$ will be
explanation:
Let us divide the given conductor into 3 parts https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950409/preptracker/questions/q_42542_explanation_1764950408653.png For the circular part $ABC$, the angle subtended at the centre is $3 \pi / 2$.
Due to $\mathrm{ABC}, B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{I}{r}\left(\dfrac{3 \pi}{2}\right)$
Due to $AD$,$A$ is at the end of the wire, therefore at $O$,
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r} \times \dfrac{1}{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi r}$
$\therefore$ Total induction $=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi r}\left(\dfrac{3 \pi}{2}+1\right)$

question: Magnetic field at a point on the line of current carrying conductor is
explanation:
Biot-savart's law says 'B' due a current carrying wire
$B=\int \dfrac{\mu_{o}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{i(\overline{d l} \times \bar{r})}{r^{3}}$
On the line of current wire $\overline{d l} \times \bar{r}=0 \quad \therefore B=0$

explanation: Magnetic lines of force due to current carrying current are concentric circles.

question: A wire carrying current $i$ and other parallel wire carrying $2i$ in the same direction produce a magnetic field $B$ at the mid point. What will be the field when $2i$ current is switched off?
explanation:
Magnetic field,
$\begin{aligned}& B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{2 i}{R} \text { and } B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{2 \cdot 2 i}{R} \\& B_{2}-B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{2 i}{R}=\mathrm{B} \\& \therefore B_{1}=B\end{aligned}$
Now, $B_{2}=0$
$\Rightarrow B_{n e t}=B_{1}=B$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: An electric current passes through a long straight copper wire. At a distance 5$cm$ from the straight wire, the magnetic field is $B$. The magnetic field at 20 $cm$ from the straight wire would be:
explanation:
The magnetic field at a distance $r$ from the straight wire
$\begin{aligned}& B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 \pi r} \\& B \alpha \dfrac{1}{r} \Rightarrow \dfrac{B_{2}}{B_{1}}=\dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}} \\& \dfrac{B_{2}}{B}=\dfrac{5}{20}=\dfrac{1}{4} \Rightarrow B_{2}=\dfrac{B}{4}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: A straight conductor carrying current $I$. If the magnetic field at a distance $r$ is 0.4$T$, then magnetic field at a distance 2$r$ will be:
explanation:
$B_{r}=\left(\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}\right)=0.4 T$
$\begin{aligned}& B_{2 r}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \dfrac{I}{2 r} \\& B_{2 r}=\dfrac{1}{2} B_{r}=\dfrac{1}{2}(0.4)=0.2 T\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A circular wire of radius $R$ is made with uniform material. A current enters at $A$ and leaves from $B$. The magnetic field at the centre $C$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956324/preptracker/questions/q_42309_explanation_1764956323610.png $\overrightarrow {{B_1}} + \overrightarrow {{B_2}} = 0$
${B_1} \to $ due to left part
${B_2} \to $ due to right part
$B_{1}, B_{2}$ at centre are numerically equal $\&$ opposite to each other. (i,e. one is into the paper and the other is towards the reader)

question: A certain length of insulated wire can be bent to form either a single circular loop (case I) or a double loop of smaller radius (case II). When the same steady current is passed through the wire, the ratio of the magnetic field at the centre in the case I to that in case II is:
explanation:
Magnetic field at the centre of a current carrying circular wire,
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} n I}{2 r}$
Let length of the wire be $\mathrm{L}$.
Case:1
$2 \pi r_{1}=L, r_{1}=\dfrac{L}{2 \pi} \therefore B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2(L / 2 \pi)}$
Case:2
$2 \times\left(2 \pi r_{2}\right)=L, r_{2}=\dfrac{L}{4 \pi} \quad \therefore B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}(2 I)}{2(L / 4 \pi)}$
Hence, $\dfrac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{4}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: A circular coil carrying a certain current produces a magnetic field $B_{0}$ at its centre. The coil is now rewound so as to have 4 turns and the same current is passed through it. The new magnetic field at the centre is:
explanation:
The magnetic field produced at the centre of the circular coil carrying current $I$ is given by
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 r}$
For one turn, $\mathrm{N}=1$
$B_{0}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}$
As the coil is rewound
$\begin{gathered}r^{\prime}=\dfrac{r}{4} \cdot N^{\prime}=4 \\\therefore B^{\prime}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I \times 4}{2 \times\left(\dfrac{r}{4}\right)}=\dfrac{16 \mu_{0} I}{2 r}=16 B_{0}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: The distance at which the magnetic field on axis as compared to the magnetic field at the centre of the coil carrying current $I$ and radius $R$ is $1 / 8$, would be
explanation:
If a coil of radius $R$ is carrying current $I$, then magnetic field on its axis at a distance $x$ from its centre is given by
${B_{{\rm{Axis }}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{2\pi I{R^2}}}{{{{\left( {{x^2} + {R^2}} \right)}^{3/2}}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
${B_{{\rm{centre }}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2R}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Dividing Eq. (1) by Eq. (2), we get
$\begin{gathered}\dfrac{B_{\text {Axis }}}{B_{\text {Centre }}}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \times \dfrac{2 R}{\mu_{0} I} \\=\dfrac{R^{3}}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}=\dfrac{1}{8} \\\Rightarrow \dfrac{R}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}}=\dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \sqrt{3} R\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Current Carrying Circular Loop

question: A circular coil $A$ of radius $r$ carries current $I$. Another circular coil $B$ of radius 2$r$ carries current of $i$. The magnetic fields at the centres of the circular coil are in the ratio of
explanation:
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{2 \pi i}{r}$ and
$\begin{gathered}B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{2 \pi(i)}{(2 r)}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{2 \pi i}{2 r} \\\therefore \dfrac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=\dfrac{2}{1}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: An electron moves in a circular orbit with a uniform speed $v$. It produces a magnetic field $B$ at the centre of the circle. The radius of the circle is proportional to
explanation:
Field due to a current carrying loop of the radius ' $r$ ' is
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i}{2 r}$
here $i=f q=\dfrac{\omega}{2 \pi} q=\dfrac{v}{2 \pi r} q$
$\therefore B=\dfrac{\mu_{o} v q}{2 r .2 \pi r}$
$r^{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{o} v q}{4 \pi B} \Rightarrow r \alpha \sqrt{\dfrac{v}{B}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Rotating Charge

question: A circular coil has one turn and carries a current $'i'$. The same wire is wound into a smaller coil of 4 turns and the same current is passed through it. Field at the center
explanation:
Magnetic field in I case
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i}{2 r}$
Magnetic filed $M$in II case (it becomes 4 turns with $\dfrac{1}{4}$ th to the previous radius)
$B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i 4}{2(r / 4)}$
$\therefore B_{2}=16 B_{1}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: Two concentric circular loops of radii $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ carry clockwise and anticlockwise currents $i_{1}$ and $\mathrm{i}_{2}$. If the centre is a null point, ${i_1}/{i_2}$ must be equal to
explanation:
Magnetic field due to both loops should be equal and opposite to each other
$\begin{aligned}& B_{1}=-B_{2} \\& \dfrac{\mu_{o} i_{1}}{2 \pi r_{1}}=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i_{2}}{2 \pi r_{2}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{i_{1}}{i_{2}}=\dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: A current $i$ ampere flows in a circular arc of wire whose radius is $R$, which substends an angle $3 \pi / 2$ radian at its centre. The magnetic induction $B$ at the centre is
explanation: Magnetic field due to arc $B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{(2 \pi-\theta) i}{R}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{\left(2 \pi-\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right) \times i}{R}=\dfrac{3 \mu_{0} i}{8 R}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: Equal current $\mathrm{i}$ follows in two segments of a circular loop in the direction shown in figure. Radius of the loop is $r$. The magnitude of magnetic field induction at the centre of the loop is
explanation:
Magnetic fileld induction at $O$ due to current
$ABC$ is $\quad B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i \theta}{4 \pi r}$
It is acting perpendicular to the paper downwards.
Magnetic field induction at $O$ due to current through $ABD$ is
$B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{i(2 \pi-\theta)}{r}$
It is acting perpendicular to paper upwards.
$\therefore$ Total magnetic field at $O$ due to current loop is
$B=B_{2}-B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{i}{r}(2 \pi-\theta)-\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{i}{r} \theta=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \dfrac{i}{r}(\pi-\theta)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: A wire bent as shown in the figure carries a current $I$. The magnetic field at $P$ is
explanation:
Let us divide the given conductor into 3 parts. $B$ due to straight parts $\left(B_{1}, B_{2}\right)$ is zero and $B$ due arc is
$\begin{gathered}B_{2}=\left(\dfrac{\mu_{o} I}{2 R} \dfrac{\pi / 2}{2 \pi}\right) \\B=B_{1}+B_{2}+B_{3}=0+\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}\left(\dfrac{\pi / 2}{2 \pi}\right)+0=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{8 R}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Combination of Long Straight and Circular Current Wires

question: A long, straight wire turned into a loop of radius 10$cm$ shown in figure. If a current of 8 amperes is passed through the loop, then the value of the magnetic field and its direction at the centre $C$ of the loop shall be close to
explanation:
$B$ at the centre of a coil carrying a current, $\mathrm{i}$ is
$B_{\text {coil }}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 r} \odot$
$B$ due to wire $B_{\text {wire }}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 \pi r} \otimes$
Given $\mathrm{i}=8 \mathrm{~A} ; r=10 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$
$\begin{aligned}& B_{\text {coil }}=\dfrac{2 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 8}{10^{-1}}=16 \pi \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~T} \odot \\& B_{\text {wire }}=\dfrac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 8}{10^{-1}}=16 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~T} \otimes\end{aligned}$
Magnetic field at centre $C$,
$\begin{aligned}& B_{C}=B_{\text {coil }}+B_{\text {wire }} \\& =\left(16 \pi \times 10^{-6}-16 \times 10^{-6}\right) \\& =3.424 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T} \odot\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Combination of Long Straight and Circular Current Wires

question: A circular coil connected to a battery of emf $E$ produces some magnetic field at its centre. The coil is unwound, stretched to double its length and rewound into a coil of $1 / 3 \mathrm{rd}$ of the original radius and connected to a battery of emf $E$ ' such that magnetic field at its centre is same as earlier. Then $E^{\prime}=$
explanation:
$B = n\left( {\frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{2r}}} \right)\frac{{{\mu _0}nE}}{{2rR}}$
$\left( {i = \frac{E}{R},R\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{is}}\,{\rm{the}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{resistance}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{the}}\,{\rm{wire}}} \right)$
$E \propto \frac{{rR}}{n}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{Here}}\,r{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{changes}}\,{\rm{to}}\,\,\frac{r}{{\,3}}$
When the coil is streched its length increases but volume remains constant.
${l_1}{A_1} = {l_2}{A_2} \Rightarrow lA = 2l{A_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Where the new resistance is
$\frac{{{R_1}}}{{{R_2}}} = \frac{{{l_1}}}{{{A_1}}} \times \frac{{{A_2}}}{{{l_2}}}$
$ = \frac{{{l_1}}}{{{A_1}}} \times \left( {\frac{1}{{2{l_1}}}} \right)\left( {\frac{{{l_1}{A_1}}}{{{l_2}}}} \right)$
$ = \frac{{{l_1}}}{{{A_1}}} \times \frac{1}{{2{l_1}}} \times \frac{{{l_1}{A_1}}}{{2{l_1}}}$
$\frac{{{R_1}}}{{{R_2}}} = \frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow {R_2} = 4{R_1}$
The ratio of number of turns is
${l_2} = {n_2}2\pi \left( {\frac{r}{3}} \right)$
${l_1} = {n_1}2\pi r$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{l_2}}}{{{l_1}}} = \frac{{{n_2}}}{{3{n_1}}} = \frac{{2{l_1}}}{{{l_1}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {n_2} = 6{n_1}$
$R$ changes to $4R$, $n$ chamges to $6n$
$\frac{E}{{E'}} = \frac{{rR}}{n} \times \frac{{\left( {6n} \right)}}{{\frac{r}{3}\left( {4R} \right)}} = \frac{9}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow E' = \frac{{2E}}{9}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: A plastic disc of radius ' $R$ ' has a charge ' $q$ ' uniformly distributed over its surface. If the disc is rotated with a frequency  $'f$ about its axis, then the magnetic induction at the centre of the disc is given by
explanation:
$dB = \frac{{{\mu _o}di}}{{2x}},dq = \frac{q}{{\pi {R^2}}}(2\pi x)dx$
$di = dqf = \frac{{2qxdx}}{{{R^2}}}f$
$dB = \frac{{{\mu _o}2qxdx}}{{2x{R^2}}} \Rightarrow B = \int {\frac{{{\mu _o}2qdx}}{{2{R^2}}}(f)} $
$B = \frac{{{\mu _o}qf}}{{{R^2}}}(R) \Rightarrow B = \frac{{{\mu _o}qf}}{R}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: An element $\Delta L=\Delta x . \hat{i}$ is placed at the origin and carries a large current $i = 10\,A$. The magnetic field on the $y$-axis at a distance of 0.5$m$ is $(\Delta x = 1\,cm)$
explanation:
According to Biot-Savart law,
$\therefore|d B|=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{i d l \sin \theta}{r^{2}}$
Given, $\Delta x=d l=10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}, i=10 \mathrm{~A}, r=0.5 \mathrm{~m}$
$\theta=90^{\circ}$
$\therefore|d B|=\dfrac{10^{-7} \times 10 \times 10^{-2}}{25 \times 10^{-2}}=4 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~T}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Small Current Element

question: Three rings, each having equal radius $R$, are placed mutually perpendicular to each other and each having its center at the origin of coordinate system. If a current $I$ is flowing through each ring, then the magnitude of the magnetic field at the common center is:
explanation:
$\vec{B}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}( \pm \hat{i})+\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}( \pm \hat{j})+\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}( \pm \hat{k})$
$|B|=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R} \sqrt{3}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: A wire of length ' $L$ ' is bent into an arc of a circle and found to subtend an angle of ' $\theta$ ' radians at the center. If a current of $'I'$ is passed through it, the magnetic induction at the center of the circle is
explanation:
Magnetic field due to Arc
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i \theta}{4 \pi R} \quad L=R \theta \Rightarrow R=\dfrac{L}{\theta}$
$\therefore \quad B=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i \theta^{2}}{4 \pi L}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: Find the magnetic field intensity at point $O$ due to a thin wire carrying current $I$ in the figure
explanation: $\begin{aligned}& B_{\text {arc }}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi R}(2 \pi-2 \alpha) \\& B_{\text {line }}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I(\sin \alpha+\sin \alpha)}{4 \pi R \cos \alpha}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi} \dfrac{\sin \alpha}{R \cos \alpha}\end{aligned}$  https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956298/preptracker/questions/q_42061_explanation_1764956297125.png  $B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi R}(\pi-\alpha+\tan \alpha)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field due to a finite sized current wire/ closed coil in the form of Triangle, Rectangle etc.

question: The magnetic field normal to the plane of a wire of $n$ turns and radius $r$ which carries a current $i$ is measured on the axis of the coil at a small distance $h$ from the centre of the coil. This is smaller than the magnetic field at the centre by the fraction
explanation:
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{1}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{2 \pi n i}{r}$ and $B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{2 \pi n i r^{2}}{\left(r^{2}+h^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}$
So, $\quad \dfrac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=\left(1+\dfrac{h^{2}}{r^{2}}\right)^{3 / 2}$
Fractional decrease in the magnetic field will be
$\begin{gathered}=\dfrac{B_{1}-B_{2}}{B_{1}}=\left(1-\dfrac{B_{2}}{B_{1}}\right) \\=\left[1-\left(1+\dfrac{h^{2}}{r^{2}}\right)^{3 / 2}\right]=1-\left(1-\dfrac{3 h^{2}}{2 r^{2}}\right)=\dfrac{3 h^{2}}{2 r^{2}}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Current Carrying Circular Loop

question: Two long parallel conductors carry currents in opposite direction as shown. One conductor carries a current of 10$A$ and the distance between the wires is $d = 10\,cm$. Current $I$ is adjusted so that the magnetic field at $P$ which is at a distance of 5$cm$ to the right of 10$A$ current is zero.Value of $I$ is
explanation:
Here ${I_1} = 10\,A,\,d = 10\,cm$
Magnetic field at $P$ due to ${I^{st}}$ conductor
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 \pi r_{1}}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \times 10}{2 \pi \times 5 \times 10^{-2}}$
Magnetic field at $p$ due to $I{I^{nd}}$ conductor https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950330/preptracker/questions/q_41986_explanation_1764950329663.png $B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{2}}{2 \pi r_{2}}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi\left(d+5 \times 10^{-2}\right)}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi \times 15 \times 10^{-2}}$
At $P$ net magnetic field is zero.
$\therefore B_{1}=B_{2}$
$\dfrac{\mu_{0} \times 10}{2 \pi \times 5}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi \times 15}$
$\therefore 2 = \frac{I}{{15}} \Rightarrow I = 30\,A$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: Magnetic field at the centre of a circular coil of radius $R$ due to current I flowing through it is $B$. The magnetic field at a point along the axis at distance $R$ from the centre is
explanation:
The magnetic field at the centre of a circular coil of radius $R$ carrying current $I$ is
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} 2 \pi I}{4 \pi R}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}$
The magnetic field at a point along the axis at distance $\mathrm{R}$ from the centre of a circular coil of radius $R$ carrying current $I$ is
$\begin{aligned}& B_{A}=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i R^{2}}{2\left(R^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \\& B_{A}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \sqrt{8} R}=\dfrac{B}{\sqrt{8}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: The magnetic field strength at $O$ due to current $I$ in the figure is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956289/preptracker/questions/q_41978_explanation_1764956288807.png If we divide the total loop into four parts then field = sum of field due to individual parts.
$\begin{gathered}B=B_{I}+B_{I I}+B_{I I I}+B_{I V} \quad B_{I I}=B_{I V}=0 \\B=B_{I}+B_{I I I}=\dfrac{\mu_{o} I}{2 R}\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right)+\dfrac{\mu_{o} I}{4 R}\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right) \\=\dfrac{7 \mu_{o} I}{16 R}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: Two long straight wires are connected by a circular section which has a radius $R$. All the three segments lie in the same plane and carry a current $I$. The magnetic induction at the centre $O$ of the circular segments is.
explanation:
Magnetic field due $AB$ and $CD$ is zero.
Magnetic field due $B C=\dfrac{\mu_{o} I \alpha}{4 \pi R}$
subject: Physics

question: A particle carrying a charge equal to 100 times the charge on the electron is rotating per second in a circular path of radius 0.8 metre. The value of the magnetic field produced at the centre will be $\left(\mu_{0}=\right.$ permeability for vacuum $)$
explanation:
The equivalent current $i=\dfrac{q}{T}$
$\begin{aligned}& B_{\text {centre }}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi i}{r}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi \times 100 e}{r} \\& =\dfrac{\mu_{0} \times 100 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{2 \times 0.8}=10^{-17} \mu_{0}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Rotating Charge

question: Circular loop of a wire and a long straight wire carry currents $I_{c}$ and $I_{e}$, respectively as shown in figure. Assuming that these are placed in the same plane. The magnetic field will be zero at the centre of the loop when the separation $H$ is
explanation:
The magnetic fields due to both wires must be equal and opposite direction.
$\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{c}}{2 R}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{e}}{2 \pi H} \Rightarrow H=\dfrac{I_{e} R}{\pi I_{c}}$
subject: Physics

question: A wire carrying current I has the shape as shown in adjoining figure. Linear parts of the wire are very long and parallel to $X$-axis while semicircular protion of radius $R$ is lying in  $YZ$ plane. Magnetic field at a point $O$ is
explanation:
${\overline B _1} = {\left( {{{\overline B }_{at\,0}}} \right)_{due\,to\,semicircle}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2R}} \cdot \frac{1}{{2\pi }} \cdot \pi \left( { - \widehat i} \right)$
${\overline B _2} = {\left( {{{\overline B }_{at\,0}}} \right)_{due\,to\,St.lines}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{4\pi R}}\left( 2 \right)\left( { - \widehat k} \right)$
${\overline B _{net}} = {\overline B _1} + {\overline B _2} = \frac{{ - {\mu _0}I}}{{4\pi R}}\left( {\pi \widehat i + 2\widehat k} \right)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Combination of Long Straight and Circular Current Wires

question: The graph between magnetic field induction ' \(B\) ' due to a current carrying coil and distance ' \(x\) ' along the axis from the centre is
explanation:
The magnetic field on the axis of a coil which is perpendicular to its plane and passing through its centre is
$\mathrm{B}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{iR}^{2}}{2\left(\mathrm{R}^{2}+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Current Carrying Circular Loop

question: A charge \[Q\]is uniformly distributed over the surface of non-conducting disc of radius \[R\]. The disc rotates about an axis perpendicular to its plane and passing through its centre with an angular velocity \(\omega\). As a result of this rotation a magnetic field of induction \(B\) is obtained at the centre of the disc. If we keep both the amount of charge placed on the disc and its angular velocity to be constant and vary the radius of the disc then the variation of the magnetic field at the centre of the disc will be represented by the figure
explanation:
$d B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} d i}{2 r}\left(d i=\dfrac{d q}{T}\right)=\dfrac{\mu_{0}(d q)}{2 r}\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2 \pi}\right)$
$\mathrm{dq}=\sigma 2 \pi \mathrm{rdr}$
$B=\int d B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \omega}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{Q}{\pi R^{2}} 2 \pi \int_{0}^{R} \dfrac{r d r}{r}$
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \omega Q}{2 \pi R^{2}} \cdot R$
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \omega Q}{2 \pi R} \Rightarrow B \alpha \dfrac{1}{R}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Rotating Charge

question: Which of the following graph represents the variation of magnetic flux density \[B\] with distance \(r\) for a straight long very thin wire carrying an electric current?
explanation: Magnetic flux density at a point due to a long current carrying thin wire is related with distance by relation $B \propto \dfrac{1}{\mathrm{r}}$
subject: Physics

question: A uniform beam of positively charged particles is moving with a constant velocity parallel to another beam of negatively charged particles moving with the same velocity in opposite direction separated by a distance \[d\]. The variation of magnetic field \[B\] along a perpendicular line drawn between the two beams with the distance from a wire is best represented by:
explanation:
The motion of two beams with opposite charge and moving in opposite directions can be assumed as two parallel current carrying wires with same currents travelling in the same direction.
The net magnetic field at their mid point is zero.

question: A semi - infinite current carrying wire is placed along $y$-axis with one end at origin as shown in fig. $P(x,y)$ is a point (in $xy$ - plane) at which magnetic field $\vec{B}=10^{-8} T(-\hat{k})$. The incorrect statement from the following. ($x$ and $y$ are in meter) 20$m$
explanation:
$10^{-8}=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i}{4 \pi d}(1+\sin \theta)=\dfrac{10^{-7} \times 1}{d}(1+\sin \theta)$
$\dfrac{d}{10}=1+\sin \theta$
Maximum value of ' $\mathrm{d}$ ' is $20 \mathrm{~m}$ for $\theta=90^{\circ}$ By varying $\mathrm{d}$ and $\theta$, we get many possibilities of ' $\mathrm{P}$ '. There are two possibilities of ' $\mathrm{P}$ ' on the line $2 x-y=20$.

options.text: Distance of $P(x,y)$ from origin may be 20$m$
Only one possibility (points ) of $P(x,y)$ can exist on the line $2 x-y=20$.
There are many points of $P(x,y)$ in $xy$-plane.

question:
Magnetic field lines always form closed loops.
Moving charges or currents produce a magnetic field.
explanation: Option (3) is correct.

Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question:
Assertion: A direct current flowing through a metallic rod produces magnetic field both inside and outside.

Reason: There is no flow of charge carries inside the rod.
explanation: In the case of metallic rod, the charge carries flow through whole of the cross section. Therefore, the magnetic field exists both inside as well as outside.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question: A long wire carrying a steady current is bent into a circular loop of one turn. The magnetic field at the centre of the loop is $B$. It is then bent into a circular coil of $n$ turns. The magnetic field at the centre of this coil of $n$ turns will be
explanation:
$\dfrac{l}{2 \pi}=R$ one turn
$\begin{aligned}& B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{o}}{2} \dfrac{I}{R}=B \\& \dfrac{l}{2 \pi n}=r=\dfrac{R}{n} \\& \therefore B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{o} n I}{2 r}=\dfrac{\mu_{o} n I}{2 R / B}=n^{2} B\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: An infinitely long straight conductor carries a current of 5$A$ as shown. An electron is moving with a speed of ${10^5}\,m/s$ parallel to the conductor. The perpendicular distance between the electron and the conductor is 20$cm$ at an instant. Calculate the magnitude of the force experienced by the electron at that instant.
explanation:
Magnetic force on the electron is
$\begin{aligned}& F=e v B \\& =e v \dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r} \\& =1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{5} \times \dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{2 \pi} \times \dfrac{5}{0.2} \\& =1.6 \times 50 \times 10^{-21}=8 \times 10^{-20} \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Biot-Savart's law gives us the expression for the magnetic field strength of an infinitesimal current element (Idl) of a current carrying conductor only.
Statement II: Biot-Savart's law is analogous to Coulomb's inverse square law of change $q$, with the former being related to the field produced by a scalar source, Idl while the latter being produced by a vector source, $q$.
In light of above statement choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
explanation:
$\mathrm{d} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}(\operatorname{Id} \vec{\ell} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}})}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^{3}}$
As per Biot Savart law, the expression for magnetic field depends on current carrying element $\mathrm{Id} \vec{\ell}$, which is a vector quantity, therefore, statement-I is correct and statementII is wrong. Note that $\mathrm{q}$ is not vector quantity.

options.text: Both statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct and Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect and Statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct.

question: Two thick wires and two thin wires, all of same material and same length, form a square in three different ways $P,Q$ and $R$ as shown in the figure. With correct connections shown, the magnetic field due to the current flow, at the centre of the loop will be zero in case of
explanation: In $P$ and $R$ only, as current divides equally in the two halves because of equal resistance in the two halves, the current in the two halves than make the field at centre zero.

question: $PQ$ and $RS$ are long parallel conductors separated by certain distance. $M$ is the midpoint between them (see the figure). The net magnetic field at $M$ is $B$. Now, the current 2$A$ is switched off. The field at $M$ now becomes
explanation:
Magnetic field at midpoint $M$ due to current in $PQ$ is
${B_{PQ}} = \frac{{{\mu _0} \times 2 \times 2}}{{4\pi d}} = \frac{{4{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi d}}\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957092/preptracker/questions/q_41678_explanation_1764957091679.png Magnetic field at $M$ due to current in $RS$ is
${B_{RS}} = \frac{{{\mu _0} \times 2 \times 1}}{{4\pi d}} = \frac{{2{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi d}}\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)\,\,\,\,\,$
The net magnetic field at midpoint $M$ is
$B = {B_{PQ}} - {B_{RS}}$
$\left( {\because {{\text{B}}_{{\text{PQ}}}}} \right.$ and ${B_{RS}}$ are in opposite direction)
$ = \frac{{4{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi d}} - \frac{{2{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi d}} = \frac{{2{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi d}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
when the current 2$A$ is switched off, the net magnetic field at $M$ is due to current 1$A$
$B' = \frac{{{\mu _0} \times 2 \times 1}}{{4\pi d}} = B$ (using(3))
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: A circular coil of wire of 100 turns each of radius 9$cm$ carries a current of 0.4$A$. The magnitude of the magnetic field at the centre of coil is $\left[\mu_{0}=12.56 \times 10^{-7}\right.$ Slunit $]$
explanation:
Given, number of turns, $n=100$ radius of coil,$r = 9\,cm = 9 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m$ and current flow in the coil,$I = 0.4\,A$
The magnitude of the magnetic field at the centre of a coil of $n$ turns is given by
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}nI}}{{2r}}\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Substituting given values in eq.(1), we get
$\begin{aligned}& B=\dfrac{12.56 \times 10^{-7} \times 100 \times 0.4}{2 \times 10^{-1} \times 9 \times 10^{-2}} \\& =2.79 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~T}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: Two similar coils of radius $R$ are lying concentrically with their planes at right angles to each other. The currents flowing in them are $I$ and 2$I$, respectively. The resultant magnetic field induction at the centre will be
explanation:
The magnetic field (2) at the centre of circular current carrying coil of radius $\mathrm{R}$ and current $\mathrm{I}$ is
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}$
Similarly, if current is $2 \mathrm{I}$, then
Magnetic field $=\dfrac{\mu_{0} 2 I}{2 R}=2 B$
So, resultant magnetic field
$\begin{aligned}& =\sqrt{B^{2}+(2 B)^{2}}=\sqrt{5 B^{2}}=\sqrt{5} B \\& =\dfrac{\mu_{0} I \sqrt{5}}{2 R}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: An electron moves in a circular orbit with uniform speed $v$. It produces a magnetic field $B$ at the centre of the circle. The radius of the circle is $\left[\mu_{0}=\right.$ permeability of free space, $e=$ eletronic charge]
explanation:
The magnetic field at the centre of a circular orbit of a moving electron is given by
$\begin{aligned}& B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{e}{r^{3}}(v \times r) \\& B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{e v \sin \theta}{r^{2}}\end{aligned}$
As, in circular path the angle between radius vector and velocity vector is $90^{\circ}$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{e v}{r^{2}} \\& \therefore r=\left(\dfrac{\mu_{0} e v}{4 \pi B}\right)^{1 / 2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Rotating Charge

question: A circular coil carrying a certain current produces a magnetic field ${B_0}$ at its centre. The coil is now rewound so as to have 3 turns and the same curent is passed through it. The new magnetic field at the centre is
explanation:
$B_{0}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 a}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I \times I}{2 a}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a}$ for $I$ turn
For rewinding the coil in three turns, new radius $a$/3, number of turns $\left( {N'} \right) = 3$
New magnetic fields $ = \frac{{{\mu _0}I \times 3}}{{2 \times (a/3)}} - \frac{{9{\mu _0}I}}{{2a}} = 9\,{B_0}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: A circular coil of radius $R$ is carrying a current $I_{1}$ in anti clock wise sense. A long straight wire is carrying current $I_{2}$ in the negative direction of X - axis. Both are placed in the same plane and the distance between centre of coil and straight wire is $d$. The magnetic field at the centre of coil will be zero for the value of $d$ equal to
explanation:
Consider the figure shown below, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950247/preptracker/questions/q_41658_explanation_1764950247223.png The magnetic field due to the circular coil at its centre is given by
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 R}$ (out ward)
The magnetic field due to the wire at $\mathrm{O}$ is given by
$B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{2}}{2 \pi d}$
The net magnetic field at the centre of coil is zero
$\therefore B_{1}-B_{2}=0$
$\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 R}-\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{2}}{2 \pi d}=0$
$d=\left(\dfrac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}\right) \dfrac{R}{\pi}$

question: In the loop shown, the magnetic induction at the point $'O'$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956237/preptracker/questions/q_41651_explanation_1764956237445.png Since the point $O$ lies on the lines of the straight wires, they contribute no magnetic induction at $O$. The magnetic induction at $\mathrm{O}$ due to arcs only. The magnetic induction at $O$ due to are of radius $R_{1}$ is
${B_1} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{I}{{{R_1}}}\frac{\pi }{2} = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{8{R_1}}} \oplus $
The magnetic field at $O$ due to arc of radius${R_2}$ is
$B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{I}{R_{1}} \dfrac{\pi}{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{8 R_{2}} \otimes$
The net magnetic field at $O$ is ${B_1} + {B_2}$
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{8{R_1}}} + \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{8{R_2}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{8}\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right) = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{8}\left( {\frac{{{R_2} + {R_1}}}{{{R_1}{R_2}}}} \right)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Combination of Long Straight and Circular Current Wires

question: A current $I$ is flowing through the loop. The direction of the current and the shape of the loop are as shown in the figure. The magnetic field at the centre of the loop is times $\frac{{{\mu _0}{\text{I}}}}{{\text{R}}}$ times $(MA = R,MB = 2R,\angle DMA = 90^\circ )$
explanation:
Magnetic field at the centre $M$ due to current through the curved portion $DA$ is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950244/preptracker/questions/q_41647_explanation_1764950244462.png ${B_1} = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{4\pi R}} \times \left( {\frac{{3\pi }}{2}} \right)$ into the plane of the paper
$ = \frac{{3{\mu _0}I}}{{8R}}$ into the plane of the paper
Magnetic field at the centre $M$ due to current through the straight portion $AB$ is ${B_2} = 0$
Since point $M$ lies on the axis of the straight portion $AB$
Magnetic field at the centre $M$ due to current through the curved portion $BC$ is
${B_3} = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{4\sqrt {\pi 2R} }} \times \frac{\pi }{2}$ into the plane of the paper $ = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{16R}}$ into the plane of the paper
Magnetic field at the centre $M$ due to current through the straight portion $CD$ is ${B_4} = 0$
Since point $M$ lies on the axis of the straingt protion $CD$
The resultant magnetic field at the $M$ is
$B = {B_1} + {B_2} + {B_3} + {B_4} = \frac{{3{\mu _0}I}}{{8R}} + 0 + \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{16R}} + 0 = \frac{{3{\mu _0}I}}{{8R}} + \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{16R}} = \frac{{7{\mu _0}I}}{{16R}}$ into the plane of the paper

options.text: $5 / 16$, but out of the plane of the paper.
$5 / 16$, but into the plane of the paper
$7 / 16$, but out of the plane of the paper
$7 / 16$, but into the plane of the peper

question: The magnetic flux near the axis and inside the air core solenoid of length 60$cm$ carrying current ' $I$ ' is $1.57 \times 10^{-6} w b$. Its magentic moment will be ( cross - sectional area of solenoid is very small as compared to its length $\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}$ SI unit)
explanation:
Given, $L = 60\,cm,\phi = 1.57 \times {10^{ - 6}}wb$
Magnetic induction inside the solenoid is
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}NI}}{L}\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Magnetic flux, $\phi=B A$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N I \cdot A}{L} \quad \because[\text { from eq.(1)] }$
Magnetic moment$ = NIA = \frac{{\phi L}}{{{\mu _0}}}$
Substituting the given values, we get
$M = \frac{{1.57 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} \times 0.6}}{{4 \times 3.14 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}} = 0.75\,A$

question: The correct Biot- Savart law in vector form is
explanation:
The Biot-Savart law in vector form is
$d \vec{B}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{I(d \vec{l} \times \vec{r})}{r^{3}}$

question: Magnetic field at a distance $r$ from an infinitely long straight conductor carrying a steady current varies as
explanation:
Magnetic field due to an infinitely long straight conductor carrrying steady current at a distance $r$ from it is given by
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}21}}{{4\pi r}}{\rm{ }}or{\rm{ }}B \propto \frac{1}{r}$
