question: Two concentric coils each of radius equal to $2 \pi$ $cm$ are placed right angles to each other. If 3$A$ and 4$A$ are the currents flowing through the two coils respectively. The magnetic induction (in $Wb{m^{ - 2}}$ ) at the centre of the coils will be
explanation:
The situation is shown in the figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950228/preptracker/questions/q_41499_explanation_1764950228060.png Radius, ${R_1} = 2\pi cm = 2\pi \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m$
Current,${I_1} = 3\,A$
For coil 2,
Radius, ${R_2} = 2\pi cm = 2\pi \times {10^{ - 2}}$
Current, ${I_2} = 4\,A$
The magnetic induction at the centre $O$ due to the current $I_{1}$ in coil $I$ is
$\mathrm{B}_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}_{1}}{2 \mathrm{R}_{1}}$
It acts in vertically upwards direction.
The magnetic induction at the centre $O$ due to the current $I_{2}$ in coil 2 is
${B_2} = \frac{{{\mu _o}{I_1}}}{{2{R_1}}}$
It acts in the horizontal direction.
As $B_{1}$ and $B_{2}$ are perpendicular to each other, so the net magnetic field at the centre $O$ is
$B = \sqrt {B_1^2 + B_2^2} = \sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}}}{{2{R_1}}}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{{{\mu _0}{I_2}}}{{2{R_2}}}} \right)}^2}} $
Substiting the given values, we get
${\left( {\frac{{{\mu _0}(3\,A)}}{{2\left( {2\pi \times {{10}^{ - 2}}\,m} \right)}}} \right) + {{\left( {\frac{{{\mu _0}(5\,A)}}{{2\left( {2\pi \times {{10}^{ - 2}}\,m} \right)}}} \right)}^2}}$
$ = \frac{{{\mu _0}\sqrt {{{(3\,A)}^2} + {{(4\,A)}^2}} }}{{2\left( {2\pi \times {{10}^{ - 2}}\,m} \right)}}$
$ = \left( {\frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}}\,Wb\,{m^{ - 1}}\,{A^{ - 1}}(5\,A)}}{{\left( {4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 2}}\,m} \right)}}} \right)$
$ = 5 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,Wb\,{m^{ - 2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: Magnetic field at the centre of a coil in the form of a square of side 2$cm$ carrying a current of 1.414$A$ is:
explanation: $B_{\text {centre }}=4\left[\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{I}{(a / 2)}\right]\left(\sin 45^{\circ}+\sin 45^{\circ}\right)$  https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950227/preptracker/questions/q_41495_explanation_1764950226671.png   $ = \frac{{4\pi  \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 1.414 \times 2 \times \sqrt 2 }}{{\pi  \times 2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}} = 8 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,T\,W$

question: The magnetic field at the centre of a circular current carrying conductor of radius $r$ is $B_{c}$. The magnetic field on its axis at a distance $r$ from the centre is $B_{a}$. The value of $B_{c}: B_{a}$ will be
explanation:
The magnetic induction at the centre of circular wire $ = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{2\pi i}}{r}$
Along the axis at a distance $r$ from the centre,
$ = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi r}}\frac{{2\pi i}}{{2\sqrt 2 }}$
$\therefore \frac{{{B_c}}}{{{B_{r - axial}}}} = 2\sqrt 2 :1$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Current Carrying Circular Loop

question: The magnitude of the magnetic induction at a point on the axis at a large distance (r) from the centre of a circular coil of ' $n$ ' turns and area ' $A$ ' carrying current ($I$) is given by
explanation:
As we know that the magnetic field on the axis of a circular current carrying loop,
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} n I a^{2}}{2\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}$
Where, $I=$ current through the coil, $a=$ radius of a circular loop,
$r=$ distance of point from the centre along the axis
$n=$ number of turns in the coil
Area of coil, $A=\pi a^{2}$
$\Rightarrow a^{2}=\dfrac{A}{\pi}$
and it $r \gg > a$ then, $\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}=r^{3}$
from eq.(1), (2) and (3), we get
$B=\left(\dfrac{\mu_{0} n I}{2 r^{3}}\right) \dfrac{A}{\pi} \times \dfrac{2}{2}$
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} n I A}{2 \pi r^{3}}$
So, option (2) is correct

options.text: $B_{a x i s}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{2 n I}{A r^{3}}$

subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Current Carrying Circular Loop

question: A circular coil carrying current $'I'$ has radius ' $R$ ' and magnetic field at the centre is ' $B$ '. At what distance from the centre along the axis of the same coil, the magnetic field will be $\dfrac{B}{8}$ ?
explanation:
According to the question,
Magnetic field at the centre of circular coil is given by
$B_{\text {centre }}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N i}{2 R}$
$B_{a x i s}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{2 \pi N i R^{2}}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}$
$\dfrac{B}{8}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N i R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}$
From eq.(1), we get
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{\mu_{0} N i}{8 \times 2 R}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N i R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \\& \dfrac{1}{8 R^{3}}=\dfrac{1}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \\& 8 R^{3}=\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2} \\& 2 R=\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}(\text { on squaring) } \\& 4 R^{2}=\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right) \\& 3 R^{2}=x^{2} \Rightarrow x=R \sqrt{3}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Current Carrying Circular Loop

question: In the diagram, $I_{1}, I_{2}$ are the strength of the currents in the loop and straight conductors respectively.$OA = OB = R$. The net magnetic field at the centre $O$ is zero.Then the ratio of the currents in the loop and the straight conductors is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957037/preptracker/questions/q_41479_explanation_1764957036784.png Magnetic field at the centre $O$ due to current ${I_1}$ in the loop is
${B_1} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{2\pi {I_1}}}{R}$
Magnetic field at the centre $O$ due to current $I_{2}$ through straight conuctor is
${B_2} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{2{I_2}}}{{2R}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{{I_2}}}{R}$
$\therefore \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{2\pi {I_1}}}{{2R}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{{I_2}}}{R}$
As the net magnetic field at $\mathrm{O}$ is zero (given), therefore $B_{1}$ and $B_{2}$ are equal in magnitude and opposite in directions.

question: Figure shows the circular coil carrying current I kept very close but not touching at a point $A$ on a straight conductor carrying the same current I. The magnitude of magnetic induction at the centre of the circular coil will be
explanation:
Magnetic induction at the centre of circular current carrying loop, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950224/preptracker/questions/q_41475_explanation_1764950223819.png $B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 r}$ (downward direction)
Magnetic induction at the centre $O$ due to straight wire
$B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \dfrac{i}{r}$ (upward direction)
Net magnetic induction at $O$
$B=B_{1}-B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 r}-\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \dfrac{i}{r}$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 r}\left[1-\dfrac{1}{\pi}\right][$ downward direction $]$

question: A conducting wire carrying current $I$ is arranged as shown. The magnetic field at $O$
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957083/preptracker/questions/q_41470_explanation_1764957083046.png ${{\vec B}_O} = {{\vec B}_I} + {{\vec B}_2} + {{\vec B}_3} + {{\vec B}_4}$
${{\vec B}_O} = \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{4\pi {R_1}}}\left( {\frac{\pi }{3}} \right)\left( { - \hat z} \right) + \vec 0 + \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{4\pi {R_2}}}\left( {\frac{\pi }{3}} \right)\left( {\hat z} \right) + \vec 0$
$ = \frac{{{\mu _o}i}}{{12}}\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_2}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_1}}}} \right)\hat z$
$\left| {{{\vec B}_O}} \right| = \frac{{{\mu _o}i}}{{12}}\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right)$

question: Current $'I'$ is flowing in a conductor shaped as shown in the figure. The radius of the curved part is $r$ and the elngth of straight portion is very large. The value of the magnetic field at the centre $O$ will be
explanation:
For the circular part $ABC$, the angle subtended at the centre is $3 \pi / 2$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957087/preptracker/questions/q_41466_explanation_1764957086447.png Due to $ABC,{B_1} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }} \cdot \frac{I}{r}\left( {\frac{{3\pi }}{2}} \right)$
Due to $AD$, $A$ is at the end of the wire, Therefore at $O$,
${B_2} = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2\pi r}} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{4\pi r}}$
$\therefore$ Total induction $ = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{4\pi r}}\left\{ {\frac{{3\pi }}{2} + 1} \right\}$

question: A direct current I flows a long the length of an infinitely long straight thin walled pipe, then the magnetic field is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956223/preptracker/questions/q_41462_explanation_1764956223158.png According to Ampere's circuital law
For an internal point, $r < \mathrm{R}$
$\mathrm{B} \times 2 \pi \mathrm{r}=\mu_{0}(0)$ or $\mathrm{B}_{\text {in }}=0$
For an external point, $r>R$
$\mathrm{B} \times 2 \pi \mathrm{r}=\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}$ or $\mathrm{B}_{\text {out }}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}$.

options.text: Uniform throughout the pipe but not zero
Zero only along the axis of the pipe
Zero at any point inside the pipe
Maximum at th centre and minimum at the edges.

question: A long straight wire along the $z$-axis carries a current $i$ in the negative $z$-direction. The magnetic vector field $B$ at a point having coordinates $(x,y)$ in the $z = 0$ plane is
explanation: The magnetic field vector is shown in the figure.  https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956217/preptracker/questions/q_41410_explanation_1764956217513.png   $\begin{aligned}& \bar{B}=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i}{2 \pi r} \hat{n} \\& \hat{n}=-|\hat{n}| \cos \theta \hat{j}+|\hat{n}| \sin \theta \hat{i} \\& \hat{n}=-\dfrac{x}{r} \hat{j}+\dfrac{y}{r} \hat{i} \\& \bar{B}=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i(y \hat{i}-x \hat{j})}{2 \pi\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to an Infinite Straight Current Carrying Wire

question: A coil having $n$ turns is wound tightly in the form of a spiral with inner and outer radii $a$ and b respectively. When a current $I$ passes through the coil, the magnetic field at the centre is
explanation:
Number of turns per unit width $=\dfrac{N}{b-a}$
Consider an elemental ring of radius $\mathrm{x}$ and thickness $\mathrm{dx}$.
Number of turns in the ring $=d N=\dfrac{N d x}{b-a}$
Magnetic field at the centre due to the elemental ring
$d B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}(d N) I}{2 x}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2} \cdot \dfrac{N d x}{(b-a)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{x} .$
$\therefore$ Field at the centre
$\begin{aligned}& =\int d B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N I}{2(b-a)} \int_{a}^{b} \dfrac{d x}{x} \\& =\dfrac{\mu_{0} N I}{2(b-a)} \ln \dfrac{b}{a}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field at the Centre of a Circular Current Loop/arc

question: In the figure shown, the magnetic field at the point $\mathrm{p}$ is
explanation:
$B_{p}=\left(B_{1}\right)_{p}+\left(B_{2}\right)_{p}+\left(B_{3}\right)_{p}+\left(B_{4}\right)_{p}+\left(B_{5}\right)_{p}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950208/preptracker/questions/q_41361_explanation_1764950207961.png where, $\quad\left(B_{1}\right)=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i(-\hat{j})}{4 \pi\left(\dfrac{3 a}{2}\right)}$ (semi-finite wire)
$\left(B_{2}\right)_{p}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4(3 a / 2)}(+\hat{k}),\left(B_{3}\right)_{p}=0$$\left(B_{4}\right)_{p}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4(a / 2)}(-\hat{k})$$\left(B_{5}\right)_{p}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4 \pi\left(\dfrac{a}{2}\right)}(-\hat{j})$
$\Rightarrow B_{\text {net }}=\sqrt{\left(B_{4}-B_{2}\right)^{2}+\left(B_{5}+B_{1}\right)^{2}}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{3 \pi a} \sqrt{\pi^{2}+4}$
subject: Physics

question: Two identical wires $A$ and $B$, each of length ' $\ell$ ', carry the same current $I$. Wire $A$ is bent into a circle of radius $R$ and wire $B$ is bent to form a square of side ' $a$ '. If $B_{A}$ and $B_{B}$ are the values of magnetic field at the centers of the circle and square respectively, then the ratio $\dfrac{B_{A}}{B_{B}}$ is
explanation:
Field by a coil at its centre is
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{o} I}{2 r}=\dfrac{\mu_{o} I(2 \pi)}{2 l} \quad(l=2 \pi r)$
Field by a square loop of side length a is
$\begin{aligned}& B_{2}=8 \sqrt{2} \dfrac{\mu_{o} I}{4 \pi a}=8 \sqrt{2} \dfrac{\mu_{o} I}{\pi l} \quad(l=4 a) \\& \dfrac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=\dfrac{\pi^{2}}{8 \sqrt{2}}\end{aligned}$

question: A charge of 1$C$ is placed at one end of a conducting rod of length 0.6$m$. The rod is rotated in a vertical plane about a horizontal axis passing through the other end of the rod with angular frequency $10^{4} \pi \mathrm{rads}^{-1}$. The magnetic field at a point on the axis of rotation at a distance of $0.8 m$ from the centre of the path is $B_{1}$. Now, half of the charge is removed from one end and placed on the other end. The rod is rotated in a vertical plane about a horizontal axis passing through the midpoint of the rod with same angular frequency. The magnetic field at a point on the axis at a distance of 0.4$m$ from the centre of the rod is $B_{2}$ then $\dfrac{B_{2}}{B_{1}}=$
explanation:
A revolving charge is equivalent to a current,
${i_1} = q.f = q \cdot \frac{\omega }{{2\pi }} = 1 \times \frac{{{{10}^4}}}{2} = 5 \times {10^3}\,A$
The field at a distance $z$ from the centre of the axis of a current carrying coil is given by https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950202/preptracker/questions/q_41353_explanation_1764950201706.png $B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} a^{2} i_{1}}{2\left(a^{2}+z^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}$
$=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5 \times 10^{3}(0.6)^{2}}{2\left[(0.6)^{2}+(0.8)^{2}\right]^{3 / 2}}=1.13 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~T}$
Equivalent current,
$i_{2}=\dfrac{q}{2} f+\dfrac{q}{2} f=q f=5 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~A}$
Magnetic field at a point $P$ in this case https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950203/preptracker/questions/q_41353_explanation_1764950202494.png ${B_2} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{a^2}{i_2}}}{{2{{({a^2} + {z^2})}^{3/2}}}}$
${B_2} = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times {{(0.3)}^2} \times 5 \times {{10}^3}}}{{2{{({{(0.3)}^2} + {{(0.4)}^2})}^{3/2}}}}$
Here, $a=0.3, z=0.4 m$
which gives, ${B_2} = 2.26 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,T$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field Due to a Rotating Charge

question: Two long parallel wires at a distance 2\[d\] apart. They carry steady equal currents flowing out of the plane of the paper, as shown. The variation of the magnetic field \[B\] along the line \[XX'\] is given by.
explanation:
If the current flows out of the paper, the magnetic field at points to the right of the wire will be upwards and to the left will be downward.
Now magnetic field at $C$ is zero. The field in the region $QX'$ will be upwards $(+ve)$ because all points lying in this region are to the right of both the wires. Similarly, magnetic field in the region $PX$ will be downwards $(-ve)$. The field in the region $PC$ will be upwards $(+ve)$ because all points are closer to $P$ compared to $Q$. Similarly magnetic field in region $BC$ will be downward $(-ve)$.
subject: Physics

question: Consider two thin identical conducting wires covered with very thin insulating material. One of the wires is bent into a loop and produces magnetic field $B_{1}$ at its centre when a current ($I$) passes through it. The second wire is bent into a coil with three identical loops adjacent to each other and produces magnetic field ${B_2}$ at the centre of the loops when current $\frac{I}{3}$ passes through it. The ratio ${B_1}:{B_2}$:-
explanation:
$r=\frac{\ell}{2 \pi}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950196/preptracker/questions/q_41301_explanation_1764950196108.png ${B_1} = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2\ell }} \times 2\pi \quad {B_2} = 3 \cdot \frac{{{\mu _0}I/3}}{{2\ell }} \cdot 6\pi $
$ = \frac{{{\mu _0}I\pi }}{\ell } = 3 \cdot \frac{{{\mu _0}I\pi }}{\ell }$
${B_1}:{B_2} = 1:3$

question: A Helmholtz coil has a pair of loops, each with $N$ turns and radius $R$. They are placed coaxially at distance $R$ and the same current $I$ flows through the loops in the same direction. The magnitude of magnetic field at $P$, midway between the centres $A$ and $C$, is given by [Refer to figure given below]:
explanation:
The resultant magnetic field at $P$ is
$B=2\left(\dfrac{\mu_{0} N I R^{2}}{2\left(R^{2}+\dfrac{R^{2}}{4}\right)^{3 / 2}}\right)=\dfrac{8 \mu_{0} N I}{5^{3 / 2} R}$

question: A current loop, having two circular arcs joined by two radial lines is shown in the figure. It carries a current of 10$A$. The magnetic field at point $O$ will be close to
explanation:
${\overrightarrow B _O} = {\overrightarrow B _{PQ}} + {\overrightarrow B _{QR}} + {\overrightarrow B _{RS}} + {\overrightarrow B _{SP}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950187/preptracker/questions/q_41262_explanation_1764950187358.png $ = \overrightarrow 0 + \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{4\pi {r_1}}}\left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\mathop k\limits^\^ + \overrightarrow 0 + \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{4\pi {r_2}}}\left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\left( { - \mathop k\limits^\^ } \right)$

explanation: The definition of the ampere is indeed based on the magnetic force between two parallel conductors carrying a steady current, and the force per unit length between them is inversely proportional to the distance separating the conductors. Correct option is (1)

question: Two long parallel wires are separated by a distance of  $2.5{\text{ }}cm.$  The force per unit length that each wire exerts on the other is   $4 \times 10^{-5} {~N} / {m}$ and the wires repel each other. The current in one wire is  $0.5{\text{ }}A.$     What is the current in the second wire ?      (Take $\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} S I$ unit)
explanation:
Separation between two wires, $d=2.5 {~cm}=2.5 \times 10^{-2} {~m} \cdot I_{1}$ current is flowing through one of the wires, then $I_{1}=0.5 {~A}$ and $I_{2}$ be the current flowing through other wire. Force per unit length $\dfrac{F}{l}=4 \times 10^{-3} {~N} / {m}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952783/preptracker/questions/q_111251_explanation_1764952782965.png
As we know that,
$\dfrac{F}{l}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \dfrac{I_{1} I_{2}}{d}$
$\therefore \quad I_{2}=\left(\dfrac{F}{l}\right) \times \dfrac{d \times 2 \pi}{\mu_{0} I_{1}}$
$=4 \times 10^{5} \times \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{2} \times 2 \pi}{4 \pi \times 10^{7} \times 0.5}$
$=2 \times 5 \times 10^{7+7}=10 {~A}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between Two Parallel Current Carrying Wires

question: Three long, straight, parallel wires carrying different currents are arranged as shown in the diagram. In the given arrangement, let the net force per unit length on the wire $C$ be $F$. If the wire $B$ is removed without disturbing the other two wires, then the force per unit length on wire $A$ is
explanation:
Given
Force per unit length on $C$ is $F$.
Calculating net force on $C$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957931/preptracker/questions/q_111241_explanation_1764957930813.png
$\dfrac{F}{L}=\dfrac{F_{C A}}{L}+\dfrac{F_{C B}}{L}$
$F$ is given by $\dfrac{F}{L}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{2 i \times i_{2}}{d}$
Where $d$ is separation distance
$\frac{{{F_{CA}}}}{L} = + \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{2 \times 3i \times 2i}}{{2d}}$ $ = + \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }} \times \frac{{12{i^2}}}{{2d}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
$\frac{{{F_{CB}}}}{L} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{2 \times 2i \times i}}{d}$ $ = - \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{4{i^2}}}{d}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
$\frac{{{F_{{\text{net }}}}}}{L} = + \frac{{{\mu _0}{i^2}}}{{2\pi d}} = F{\text{ (given)}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (3)$
Now $B$ is removed, Net Forces (on $A$) is
$\frac{{{F_{(AC)}}}}{L} = - \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\left[ {\frac{{2 \times 3i \times 2i}}{{2d}}} \right]$ $ = - 3\left[ {\frac{{{\mu _0}{i^2}}}{{2\pi d}}} \right] = - 3F$
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between Two Parallel Current Carrying Wires

question: Two parallel conductor each $50 m$ long, separated by $0.2 m$ experience a force of  $1{\text{ }}N.$  If the current in first conductor is twice that of the second conductor, then what is the current in the second conductor?( $\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}$ in SI units)
explanation:
Given,
$\begin{aligned}& l=50 {~m} \\& r=0.2 {~m} \\& F=1 {~N}\end{aligned}$
We know that,
$F=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \dfrac{i_{1} i_{2}}{r} l$
$\Rightarrow 1=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{2 \pi} \times \dfrac{i_{1} i_{2} \times 50}{0.2}$
$\Rightarrow 1=5 i_{1} i_{2} \times 10^{-5} \Rightarrow i_{1} i_{2}=\dfrac{1}{5 \times 10^{-5}}$
$\Rightarrow i_{1} i_{2}=2 \times 10^{4} \Rightarrow 2 i_{1} \cdot i_{2}=2 \times 10^{4}$
[Given, $i_{1}=2 i_{2}$ ]
$\Rightarrow i_{2}=10^{2} {~A}=100 {~A}$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between Two Parallel Current Carrying Wires

question: Three parallel wires $a, b$ and $c$ carrying currents $i_{a}, i_{b}$ and $i_{c}$ as shown in the figure are placed next to each other.    https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957928/preptracker/questions/q_111228_question_1764957927779.png     The magnitude force on a length $l$ of the wire $a$, if $d_{2}=2 d_{1}, i_{b}=i_{a}$ and $i_{c}=4 i_{a}$ is
explanation:
The given situation is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957928/preptracker/questions/q_111228_explanation_1764957928481.png
Magnitude of force on a length $l$ of wire $a$ due to current carrying parallel wire $b$,
$F_{a c}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i_{a} i_{c}}{3 d_{1}} l \quad$ (Repulsive)
Similarly, magnitude of force on a length $l$ of wire a due to current carrying wire $c$,
$F_{a c}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \dfrac{i_{a} i_{c}}{d_{2}} \times l$ (Repulsive)
$\therefore$ Net magnetic force on a length of the wire $a$,
$F_{\text {net }}=F_{a c}-F_{a b}$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \dfrac{i_{a} i_{c}}{3 d_{1}} \times l-\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \dfrac{i_{a} i_{b}}{d_{1}} \times l$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i_{a} \times 4 i_{a} \times l}{3 d_{1}}-\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i_{a} \times i_{a} \times l}{d_{1}}$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i_{a}^{2} l}{d_{1}}\left[\dfrac{4}{3}-1\right]=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i_{a}^{2}}{6 \pi d_{1}} l$
(since, $d_{2}=2 d_{1}, i_{b}=i_{a}$ and $i_{c}=4 i_{a}$ )
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between Two Parallel Current Carrying Wires

question: Two long wires with no contact are placed perpendicular to each other, $i_{1}$ and $i_{2}$ are currents flowing through these wires, respectively. The magnetic force on a small length $d l$ of the second wire situated at a distance $l$ from first wire is proportional to
explanation:
Given, $i_{1}$ and $i_{2}$ are the current flowing through two perpendicular wires as shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952773/preptracker/questions/q_111224_explanation_1764952772847.png
Magnetic field produced by first wire $X$ on second wire $Y$.
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i_{1}}{l}$
$\therefore$ Magnetic force on small length $d l$ of the second wire $Y$,
$F=i_{2} B_{1} d l=i_{2} \cdot \dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \dfrac{i_{1}}{l} \cdot d l$
$\Rightarrow \quad F=\dfrac{\mu_{0} d l}{2 \pi l} \cdot i_{1} i_{2} \Rightarrow F \propto i_{1} i_{2}$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between Two Non-parallel Current Carrying Wires

question: Two particles carrying equal charges move parallel to each other at a speed of $150 {~km} / {s}$. If $\vec{F}_{1}$ and $\vec{F}_{2}$ are magnetic and electric forces between two charged particles then, $\dfrac{\left|\vec{F}_{1}\right|}{\left|\vec{F}_{2}\right|}$ is $\left(\right. $ Let $\left. \mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}=\dfrac{1}{9 \times 10^{16}} s^{2} / m^{2}\right)$
explanation:
Given, two charge particles having same charge, $q$ move with same speed i. e., $v_{1}=v_{2}=150 {~km} / {s}=1.5 \times 10^{5} {~m} / {s}$ Electric force between two moving charge particle is same as they are static, i. e. ,
$\left| {{F_2}} \right| = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{q^2}}}{{{r^2}}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
Magnetic force between two moving charge particles is given by
$F_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{q_{1} \cdot q_{2} v_{1} v_{2}}{r^{2}}$
$\left[\because q_{1}=q_{2}=q\right. $ and $\left. v_{1}=v_{2}=v\right]$
$\left| {{F_1}} \right| = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }} \cdot \frac{{{q^2}{v^2}}}{{{r^2}}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
From Eq. (1) and (2), we get
$\therefore \dfrac{\left|F_{1}\right|}{\left|F_{2}\right|}=\dfrac{\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{q^{2} v^{2}}{r^{2}}}{\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \dfrac{q^{2}}{r^{2}}}=\varepsilon_{0} \mu_{0} v^{2}$
$=\dfrac{1}{9 \times 10^{16}} \times\left(1.5 \times 10^{5}\right)^{2}=2.5 \times 10^{-7}$
Hence, $\dfrac{F_{1}}{F_{2}}$ is $2.5 \times 10^{-7}$
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between Two Parallel Current Carrying Wires

question: Two thin long parallel wires separated by a distance ' $r$ ' from each other in vacuum carry a current $I$ ampere each in opposite directions. Then, they will
explanation:
If F be the magnitude of force experienced by a segment of length $\Delta L$ of each conductor, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952748/preptracker/questions/q_111176_explanation_1764952747618.png $\frac{F}{{\Delta L}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}{I_2}}}{{2\pi r}} = \frac{{\mu {I^2}}}{{2\pi r}}\quad \left[ {{\text{as}}\,{I_1} = {I_2} = I} \right]$
${\vec B_1}$ is the magnetic induction produced by (1) at the location (2) where then ${B_1} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}}}{{2\pi r}}.$
Also, ${\vec F_2} = {I_2}\Delta \vec L \times {\vec B_1}$ and ${\vec F_1} = {I_1}\Delta \vec L \times {\vec B_2}.$
Since ${\vec F_1}$ and ${\vec F_2}$ are oppositely directed, so repulsion.
Hence correct option is (2).

options.text: Attract each other with a force per unit length of $\dfrac{\mu_{0} I^{2}}{2 \pi r}$
Repel each other with a force per unit length of $\dfrac{\mu_{0} I^{2}}{2 \pi r}$
Repel each other with a force per unit length of $\dfrac{\mu_{0} I^{2}}{2 \pi r^{2}}$
Attract each other with a force per unit length of $\dfrac{\mu_{0} I^{2}}{2 \pi r^{2}}$

subject: Physics

question: An infinite wire, placed along ${z}$-axis, has current ${I_{I}}$ in positive ${z}$-direction. A conducting rod ${P Q}$ placed in ${x y}$ plane parallel to ${y}$-axis has current ${I_{2}}$ in positive ${y}$-direction. The ends of the rod subtend angles ${30^{\circ}}$ and ${60^{\circ}}$ at the origin with positive ${x}$-direction as shown in figure. The rod is at a distance ${a}$ from the origin. If the net force on the rod is found to be ${\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{4 \pi} \ln N}$, find the value of ${N}$.
explanation:
To calculate the force on rod $P Q$ we consider an element of width $d y$ at a distance $y$ from the $d y$-axis as shown in figure.https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950651/preptracker/questions/q_43828_explanation_1764950650794.png The magnetic induction at the location of this element due to the wire at origin is given as $B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 \pi \sqrt{y^{2}+a^{2}}}$
The force on element due to the magnetic induction of wire at origin is given as
$d F=B I_{2} d y \sin \theta$
Net force on the $\operatorname{rod} P Q$ is given as $F=\int d F=\int B I_{2} d y \sin \theta$
$ \Rightarrow F = \int\limits_{ - a\tan 30^\circ }^{ + a\tan 30^\circ } {\left( {\frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}}}{{2\pi \sqrt {{a^2} + {y^2}} }}} \right)} $
${I_2}\,dy\left( {\frac{y}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {y^2}} }}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow F = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}{I_2}}}{{2\pi }}\int_{ - \sqrt 3 a}^{ + a/\sqrt 3 } {\frac{{y\,dy}}{{{a^2} + {y^2}}}} $
$ \Rightarrow F = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}{I_2}}}{{2\pi }}\left[ {\frac{1}{2}\ln \left( {{a^2} + {y^2}} \right)} \right]_{ - \sqrt 3 a}^{ + a/\sqrt 3 }$
$ \Rightarrow F = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}{I_2}}}{{4\pi }}\left[ {\ln \left( {\frac{4}{3}{a^2}} \right) - \ln \left( {4{a^2}} \right)} \right]$
$ \Rightarrow F = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}{I_2}}}{{4\pi }}\ln 3$

question: Two thin long parallel wires separated by a distance $b$ are carrying a current $i$ ampere each. The magnitude of the force per unit length exerted by one wire on the other, is
explanation:
Let two long parallel thin wires $X$ and $Y$ carry current $i$ and separated by $a$ distance $b$ apart. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956436/preptracker/questions/q_43362_explanation_1764956436265.png The magnitude of magnetic field $B$ at any point on $Y$ due to current $i_{1}$ in $X$ is given by
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \dfrac{i_{1}}{b}$
The magnitude of force acting on length $l$ of $Y$ is
$F=i_{2} B l=i_{2}\left(\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \dfrac{i_{1}}{b}\right) l$
Force per unit length is
$\dfrac{F}{l}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \dfrac{i_{1} i_{2}}{b}$
Given, $i_{1}=i_{2}=i$, therefore
$\dfrac{F}{l}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{i^{2}}{b}$

question: A current carrying rectangular loop $P Q R S$ is made of uniform wire. The length $PR = QS = 5\,cm$ and $PQ = RS = 100\,cm$. If ammeter current reading changes from $I$ to 2 $I$, the ratio of magnetic forces per unit length on the wire $P Q$ due to wire $R S$ in the two cases respectively $\left(f_{P Q}^{I}: f_{P Q}^{2 I}\right)$ is
explanation:
Force between two straight conductors is given by
$F=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi d}(l)$
As, $F \propto I_{1} I_{2}$.
So, $f_{P Q}^{I}: f_{P Q}^{2 I}=I: 4 I=1: 4$

question: Two long straight wires $P$ and $Q$ carrying equal current $10\,A$ each were kept parallel to each other at $5\,cm$ distance.  Magnitude of magnetic force experienced by $10\,cm$ length of wire $P$ is $F_{1}$. If distance between wires is halved and currents in them are doubled, force $F_{2}$ on $10\,cm$ length of wire $P$ will be
explanation:
Magnetic force per unit length between two parallel current carrying wires is $\dfrac{F}{l}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi d} I_{1} I_{2}$
Therefore, force on length $l$ is given by$F_{1}=\left(\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi d} I_{1} I_{2}\right)(l)$
Similarly, $F_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi(d / 2)}\left(2 I_{1}\right)\left(2 I_{2}\right)(l)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{F_{1}}{F_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{2 \times 2 \times 2}$
$\Rightarrow F_{2}=8 F_{1}$
So option (2) is correct.

question: Two thin long conductors separated by a distance $d$ carry currents $I_{1}$ and $I_{2}$ in the same direction. They exert a force $F$ on each other. Now, the current in one of them is increased to two times and its direction is reversed. The distance is also increased to 3$d$. The new value of force between them is:
explanation:
The situation is shown in the figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956308/preptracker/questions/q_42213_explanation_1764956307636.png Given, wires are long,
Now, $B_{1}=$ Magnetic field at $I_{2}$ (second wire) due to current in the first wire.
$=\left[\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi d} I_{1}\right]$
The field is perpendicularly inward.
Now, force on the second wire due to first wire
$\begin{aligned}& F_{21}=I_{2}\left(\ell \times B_{1}\right) \text { (towards left) } \\& =I_{2} \ell B_{1} \sin 90^{\circ} \\& F_{21}=I_{2} l B_{1}\end{aligned}$
Where, $\ell$ is the length of the wire and $\mathrm{d}$ is separation between the two wires
$F_{21}=I_{2} l\left(\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 \pi d}\right)=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2} l}{2 \pi d}$
According to question, $\left|F_{21}\right|=F$
Now, consider the situation where the current in first wire is doubled and reversed
Proceeding as previous case, we can wire
$F^{\prime}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}\left(2 I_{1}\right)\left(I_{2}\right) l}{2 \pi d \times 3}$
Here, force on second wire will be towards right,
So, $\quad \dfrac{F^{\prime}}{F}=\dfrac{\dfrac{\mu_{0}\left(2 I_{1}\right)\left(I_{2}\right) l}{2 \pi d \times 3}}{\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2} l}{2 \pi d}}$
Here, negative sign is due to opposite directions of $F^{\prime}$ and $F$.
$\dfrac{F^{\prime}}{F}=-\dfrac{2}{3} \Rightarrow F^{\prime}=-\dfrac{2 F}{3}$

question: $A$ and $B$ are two infinitely long straight parallel conductors. $C$ is another straight conductor of length 1$m$ kept parallel to $A$ and $B$ as shown in the figure. Then, the force experienced by $C$ is
explanation:
Mutual force between conductors $A$ and $C$,
${F_1} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{2\pi }}\frac{{{I_1}{I_2}l}}{r}(\ell = 1\,m)$
$ = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{2\pi }}\frac{{2 \times 3 \times 1}}{{0.05}}$
$ = 2.4 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,N\quad $ (towards $A$)
Mutual force between conductors $B$ and $C$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \times \dfrac{8 \times 3 \times 1}{0.08}=6 \times 10^{-5} N(\text { towards B })$
Hence, the resultant force experienced by $C$
$(6 - 2.4) \times {10^{ - 5}}\,N = 3.6 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,N$ (towards $B$)

question: Figure shows an infinitely long wire carrying an outward current \[{I_1}\]. The current is along \[Z\] axis. There is a curved wire in \(x-y\) plane carrying current \(I_{2}\). The magnetic force on this wire between \[\left( {{x_1},{y_1}} \right)\]) and \[\left( {{x_2},{y_2}} \right)\] is:
explanation:
Consider a small current element $d\bar l$ in $xy$ plane so that $\mathrm{d} \bar{l}=\mathrm{d} x \hat{i}+\mathrm{dy} \hat{\mathrm{j}}$
The magnetic field produced by the current wire present along $z$ - axis is
$\overline B = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}}}{{2\pi r}}\widehat n$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956211/preptracker/questions/q_41325_explanation_1764956210906.png $\hat n = - \sin \theta \hat i + \cos \theta \hat j$
$\hat n = - \frac{y}{r}\hat i + \frac{{\text{x}}}{r}\hat j$
The small force on the current element
$d\overline F = {I_2}\,d\overline {\text{l}} \times \overline B \,\, = \,\frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}{I_2}}}{{2\pi {r^2}}}({\text{x}}\,d{\text{x}} + y\,dy)\hat k$
$dF = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}{I_2}}}{{4\pi }}\left( {\frac{{{\text{2x}}\,d{\text{x}} + 2y\,dy}}{{{{\text{x}}^2} + {y^2}}}} \right)$
$\int {dF} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}{I_2}}}{{4\pi }}\int\limits_{({{\text{x}}_1},{y_1})}^{({{\text{x}}_2},{y_2})} {\frac{1}{{{{\text{x}}^2} + {y^2}}}} \,d({{\text{x}}^2} + {y^2})$
$F = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}{I_2}}}{{4\pi }}\ln \left( {\frac{{{\text{x}}_2^2 + y_2^2}}{{{\text{x}}_1^2 + y_1^2}}} \right)$

Similarly when $I_{1} \& I_{2}$ are is same direction then $I_{1} I_{2}=(+) v e$ and they attract each other $F=(-)ve$. The correct graph is option(1)

question: An infinitely long current carrying wire and a small current carrying loop are in the plane of the paper as shown. The radius of the loop is $a$ and distance of its centre from the wire is $d(d>>a)$. If the loop applies a force $F$ on the wire then
explanation:
Magnetic dipole moment of the loop, $M = IA = I\left( {\pi {a^2}} \right)$
Force on the dipole in a non-uniform magnetic field,
$F = \frac{{ - dU}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - d}}{{dx}}(\bar M \cdot \bar B)$
$F = M\frac{{dB}}{{dx}}$
Now, $B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2\pi x}} \Rightarrow \frac{{dB}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2\pi {x^2}}}$
So, $F \propto \frac{M}{{{x^2}}} \Rightarrow F \propto \frac{{{a^2}}}{{\,{d^2}}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between Two Non-parallel Current Carrying Wires

question: A metallic rod of mass per unit length 0.5 $kg{m^{ - 1}}$ is lying horizontally on a smooth inclined plane which makes an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. A magnetic field of strength 0.25 $T$ is acting on it in the vertical direction. When a current $' I '$ is flowing through it, the rod is not allowed to slide down. The quantity of current required to keep the rod stationary is
explanation:
When the rod is stationary, component of the horizontal force (due to the magnetic field) parallel to the inclined plane balances the component of the vertical weight of the rod in that direction.
$\therefore BIl\cos \theta = mg\sin \theta $
$ \Rightarrow BI\cos 30^\circ = (m/l)g\sin 30^\circ $
$\Rightarrow 0.25 \times \mathrm{I} \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=0.5 \times 10 \times \dfrac{1}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow I = \frac{5}{{0.25 \times \sqrt 3 }} \cong 11.55\,A$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Force on a Current Carrying Wire

question: A straight wire carrying a current of $2 \sqrt{2} A$ is making an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the direction of  uniform magnetic field of $3\,T$. The force per unit length on the wire due to the magnetic field is
explanation:
Force on the wire, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957935/preptracker/questions/q_111311_explanation_1764957935346.png $\vec{F}-=I \vec{l} \times \vec{B}$
$F=B I l \sin \theta$
$\therefore$ Force per unit length,
$\frac{F}{l} = BI\sin \theta = 3 \times 2\sqrt 2 \sin 45^\circ = 6N{m^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: An electron falling freely under the influence of gravity enters a uniform magnetic field directed towards south. The electron is initially deflected towards
explanation:
For the freely falling electron. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952785/preptracker/questions/q_111304_explanation_1764952784337.png $\vec{v}=-v_{o} \vec{k}, \vec{B}=-B_{0} \hat{j}$
$\therefore \vec{F}=-e(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})=-e\left[\left(-v_{0} \hat{k}\right) \times\left(-B_{0} \hat{j}\right)\right]$
$=+e v_{0} B_{0} \hat{i}=e v_{0} B_{0}$ (East)
$\therefore$ The electron is deflected towards east
subject: Physics
subtopic: Lorentz Force

question: A magnetic field is applied in $Y$-direction on an $\alpha$-particle travelling along $X$-direction. The motion of the -particle will be
explanation:
$\vec{B}=B_{0} \vec{j}, \vec{v}=v_{0} \hat{i}$
$\therefore$ Force on $\alpha$-particle is
$\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})=q\left(v_{0} \hat{i} \times B_{0} \hat{j}\right)=q v_{0} B_{0} \hat{k}$
$\therefore$ Centripetal force is along $Z$-direction,The motion of -particle will be a circle in $Z X$-plane.

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The magnetic field at a point due to a current carrying conductor decreases with the square of the distance from the conductor.
The force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is dependent on the current, the length of the conductor, and the magnetic field.
explanation: The magnetic field due to a long straight wire decreases inversely with the distance (not with the square of the distance) from the conductor as given by $B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}$. The force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is indeed given by $F=I \times l \times B \sin (\theta)$, where $\theta$ is the angle between the conductor and the magnetic field, which makes Statement B true. Correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
A charged particle moving parallel to a magnetic field experiences no force.
The Lorentz force is always perpendicular to the velocity of the charged particle.
explanation: A charged particle moving parallel to a magnetic field experiences no force because the magnetic component of the Lorentz force depends on the velocity component perpendicular to the magnetic field. Correct option is (1)

Assertion: A charged particle moving in a magnetic field experiences a force only when it has a velocity component perpendicular to the magnetic field.

Reason: The force on a charged particle moving in a magnetic field is given by $q \cdot(v \times B)$, which is zero if velocity is parallel to field.
explanation: The force experienced by a charged particle in a magnetic field is indeed dependent on the component of velocity that is perpendicular to the magnetic field direction. Correct option is (1)

question: A conducting wire $P Q$ carries a current of $10 A$ as shown in the figure. It is placed in a uniform magnetic field $5 T$ which is acting normally outside from the paper. Then, the net force experienced by it is
explanation:
Given
$i_{P Q}=10 {~A}$
$B=5 T$
Which is normal to plane of paper.
Let $P Q$ be a straight wire, then
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952781/preptracker/questions/q_111249_explanation_1764952781172.png
$\left[\triangle P B O, P O^{2}=P B^{2}+B O^{2}\right]$
Length of $P Q=P O+O Q$
$=5+5=10 {~cm}$
Force experienced by wire in uniform magnetic field is given by
$F=B i L \sin \theta$
[Plane and outward (normal) to plane]
$F=5 \times 10 \times \sin 90^{\circ} \times(0.1)\left[\because \theta=90^{\circ}\right]$
$\Rightarrow F=5 {~N}$
So, the correct option is (2)

question: A charge $q$ moves with a velocity $2 {~ms}^{-1}$  along   $X$ -  axis in a uniform magnetic field $B=(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) T$, then charge will experience a force
explanation:
Given $B=(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) T$
$v=2 \hat{i} {~m} / {s}$
Force on charged particle,
$\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})=q[2 \hat{i} \times(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})]$
$=q[0+4 \hat{k}-6 \hat{j}]=q(4 \hat{k}-6 \hat{j})$
$=q(-6 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})$
From the above expression of force, it is clear that charge will experience a force in $Y Z$ Plane.
So, the correct option is (1)

subtopic: Lorentz Force

question: A square loop of side $a$ and carrying a current $I$ is suspended from an insulating hanger of a spring balance as shown in the figure. The transverse magnetic field $B$ directed into the paper occurs only at the bottom side of the loop. When the direction of current in the loop is reversed, the change in the reading of spring balance is
explanation:
Given,
Square loop with side length $=a$
Current in loop $=i$
Magnetic field $=B$
The Net force is given by Lorentz equation, Initially when direction of current clockwise.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952779/preptracker/questions/q_111240_explanation_1764952778548.png
${F_{net{\text{ }}}} = {F_g} + BiL\sin \theta $ $ = mg + iaB\,\left[ {L = a\sin 90^\circ = 1} \right]\quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
When direction of current is reversed,
$F_{\text {net }}=F_{g}+B I L \sin \theta$
$ = mg - iaB\,\left[ {L = a,\sin 180^\circ = - 1} \right]\quad \quad \quad (2)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2)
$F_{\text {net }}=F_{1}-F_{2} \Rightarrow \Delta F_{\text {net }}=2 i a B$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Force on a Current Carrying Wire

question: A horizontal wire carries $160 A$ current below which another wire of linear density $10 {~g} / {m}$ carrying a current is kept at  $4{\text{ }}cm$  distance. If the wire kept below hangs in air, what is the current in this wire, when the direction of current in both the wires is same?    ($g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ and $\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}$ in SI units)
explanation:
Since, wire hangs in air, hence
$m g=I B l \Rightarrow \dfrac{m}{l}=\dfrac{i B}{g}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{m}{l}=\dfrac{I}{g} \cdot \dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r} \quad\left(\because B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}\right)$
Here, $i = 160\,A,\,\frac{m}{l} = 10\,g/m = {10^{ - 2}}\,kg/m$
$r=4 {~cm}=4 \times 10^{-2} {~cm}$
$g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}, \mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} {H} / {m}$
$\therefore \quad 10^{-2}=\dfrac{160}{10} \times \dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times i}{2 \pi \times 4 \times 10^{-2}}$
$\Rightarrow 10^{-2}=8 \times 10^{-5} \times i$
$\Rightarrow i=\dfrac{10^{3}}{8}=125 {~A}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Force on a Current Carrying Wire

question:
Statement I: A uniform electric field and a uniform magnetic field are pointed in the same direction. If an electron is projected in the same direction, the electron velocity will decrease in magnitude.
Statement II : Two infinite long parallel wires are carrying current in the same direction. The magnetic field at a point mid - way between the wires is zero.
Statement III: No net force acts on a rectangular coil carrying a steady current, when suspended in a uniform magnetic field.
Which of the following is correct?
explanation:
When an electron is projected in the direction of electric and magnetic field, magnetic field does not produce any effect on the velocity of electron because no force acts on the electron when it is moving in the direction of magnetic field. But an electric force acts on the electron in opposite direction of electric field, hence electron velocity will decrease in magnitude. Therefore, statement I is true.
If the wire carry unequal current, the magnetic field at a point midway between the wires will not be zero. So statement - II is not a general statement. So statement - II is false. When a current carrying loop of any shape is placed in a uniform magnetic field, net force experienced by it will be zero. As $\vec{F}=i \overrightarrow{l_{e}} \times \vec{B}$ and $l_{e}$ (effective length of a current carrying loop) is zero, $|\vec{F}|=0$.
So, the correct option is (4)

options.text: Statements I, II and III are true
Statements I and II are true, but statement III  is false
Statements II and III are true, but statement I is false
Statements I, III are true, but statement II is false

subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Force on a Current Carrying Wire

question: A wire of length $l$ carries a current $i$ along the $X$-axis. The magnetic force acting on the wire is given by $F=i B_{0} l(\hat{k}-\hat{j}) T$, where $B_{0}$ is a constant. The existing magnetic field $B$ is
explanation:
Given,
Length of wire be $l$ along $X$-axis.
Force, $F=i B_{0} l(\hat{k}-\hat{j})$
As we know that,
$\vec{F}=i(\vec{l} \times \vec{B})$
$\therefore i B_{0} l(\hat{k}-\hat{j})=i(\hat{l} \times B) \Rightarrow B_{0}(\hat{k}-\hat{j})=(\hat{i} \times \vec{B})$
$\Rightarrow \vec{B}=B_{0}(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})$
So, the correct option is (3)

question: The force acting on a current carrying wire joining two fixed points $A$ and $B$ in a uniform magnetic field
explanation:
As we know that, force on a current carrying conductor placed in magnetic field region,
$F=B i l \sin \theta$
where, $F=$ force, $B=$ magnetic field, $i=$ current,
$l=$ length of wire and $\theta=$ angle between $B$ and $l$.
Therefore, force is independent of shape of conductor.
So, the correct option is (3)

options.text: increases exponentially with increase in current.
is inversely proportional to the magnitude of the magnetic field.
is independent of the shape of the wire.
is parallel to the direction of the magnetic field.

explanation:
By Lorentz's force law, force acting on a charge particle in magnetic field $B$ moving with velocity $v$ is given by $F=q(v \times B)$
where $q=e$, according to question,
then, $F=e(v \times B)$
So, the correct option is (1)

question: A straight wire of mass  $0.2{\text{ }}kg$  and length  $1.5{\text{ }}m$  carries a current  $2{\text{ }}A$  is shown in the figure. It is suspended in mid-air by a uniform magnetic field $B$ pointing to the plane of paper. The magnitude of magnetic field is (ignore, earth's magnetic field and assume $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$)
explanation:
Given, $m_{\text {wire }}=0.2 {~kg}$
$i_{\text {wire }}=1.5 {~m}$
Current, $i=2 A$
The force acting on the current carrying wire in uniform magnetic field,
$F=i B l \sin \theta$
$F = iBl\sin 90^\circ \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \left( {\because \quad \theta = 90^\circ } \right)$
$F = iBl\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
Weight of the wire, $w=m g=0.2 \times 10 {~N}$
In the position of suspension,
Bil $=m g \Rightarrow B=\dfrac{m g}{i l}=\dfrac{0.2 \times 10}{2 \times 1.5}=0.67 T$
So, the correct option is (2)

question: The magnitude of the force vector acting on a unit length of a thin wire carrying a current $i=8 A$ at a point $O$, if the wire is bent as shown in the figure with a radius, $R=10 \pi {~cm}$ is
explanation:
Given, current flowing through wire, $i=8 {~A}$ and radius of circular wire, $R=10 \pi {~cm}=10 \pi \times 10^{-2} {~m}$
According to the question, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957914/preptracker/questions/q_111198_explanation_1764957913965.png
$\because$ Magnetic field produced by semi-circular current carrying thin wire at centre $O$.
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{4 R}$
$\Rightarrow B=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 8}{4 \times 10 \pi \times 10^{-2}} \Rightarrow B=8 \times 10^{-6} T$
Magnitude of the force on thin wire per unit length.
$F=i B$
$=8 \times 8 \times 10^{-6}$
$=64 \times 10^{-6} {~N} / {m}=64 \mu {~N} / {m}$
So, the correct option is (1)

question: A semi - circular loop of radius  $30\,cm$  of a wire carries current  $6\,A.$    A uniform magnetic field of $0.5 T$ is present perpendicular to the plane of the loop.    What is the magnitude of force felt by the wire?
explanation:
Given, the radius of a circular loop,
$R=30 \times 10^{-2} m$
current, $i=6 A$ and magnetic field, $B=0.5 T$.
Force exerted on the wire,
$F=i L B \sin \theta$
$F=6 \times 2 \times 30 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.5 \times \sin 90^{\circ}\left[\because \theta=90^{\circ}\right]$
$F=1.8 {~N}$
Hence, the correct option is (2).

explanation:
The force due to vertical wires acts in opposite directions. Thus they will cancel each other. The magnetic force on the wire will be due to the semicircualr part only.
Thus, ${F_3} = il{\rm{ }}(B = i\left( {{l_{{\rm{eff}}}}} \right)B\,;$ Here, $l_{\text {eff }}=2 R$;
$=i 2 R B \sin 90^{\circ}=2 i R B\left(\right.$ acting and $\left.\theta=90^{\circ}\right)$
downward $)=-2 i R B \hat{j}$ (By using Fleming's left hand rule) $i.e.,$ along $ - ve$ $y$-axis.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950713/preptracker/questions/q_43942_explanation_1764950712824.png

explanation:

question: The two conducting rails in the drawing are tilted upward so they each make an angle of ${37^{\circ}}$ with respect to the ground. The vertical magnetic field has a magnitude of 0.050 $T$ . The ${0.20-{kg}}$ aluminium rod (length ${=2.0 {~m}}$ ) slides without friction down the rails at a constant velocity. How much current flows through the rod  ?
explanation:
The following drawing shows a side view of the conducting rails and the aluminium rod. Three forces act on the rod: (1) its weight $m g$, (2) the magnetic force $F$, and the normal force $N$. An application of right-hand rule shows that the magnetic force is directed to the left, as shown in the drawing. Since the rod slides down the rails at a constant velocity, its acceleration is zero. If we choose the $x$-axis to be along the rails, the net force along the $x$-direction is zero: $\Sigma {F_r} = m{a_x} = 0.$ Using the components of $F$ and $m g$ that are along the $x$-axis.
$F\cos 37^\circ = mg\sin 37^\circ \quad \quad \quad (1)$
The magnetic force given by Eq. (1) as $F=I L B \sin \boldsymbol{\theta}$, where $\boldsymbol{\theta}=$ $90^{\circ}$ is the angle between the magnetic field and the current. We can use these two relations to find the current in the rod https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956488/preptracker/questions/q_43743_explanation_1764956488426.png Substituting the expression $F=I L B \sin 90^{\circ}$ into Newton's second law and solving for the current $I$, we obtain
$I = \frac{{mg\sin 37^\circ }}{{\left( {LB\sin 90^\circ } \right)\cos 37^\circ }}$
$ = \frac{{(0.20\,kg)\left( {10\,m/{s^2}} \right)\sin 37^\circ }}{{(2.0\,m)(0.050\,T)\sin 90^\circ \cos 37^\circ }}$
$ = 14\,A$
subject: Physics

question: A rectangular coil $ABCD$ is hung from one side of a balance (as shown in fig.) A $500\,g$ mass is added to the other arm to balance the weight of the coil. A current of $4.9\,A$ is passed through the coil and a constant magnetic field of $0.6\,T$ acting inward (in $x - z$ plane) is switched on such that only arm $CD$ of length $1.5\,cm$ lies in the field. The additional mass $m$ must be added to regain the balance is $N \times {10^{ - 2}}\,kg$. Find $N$.
explanation:
In absence of magnetic field, the weight added in one pan balances the rectangular coil in the other pan of balance,
$\therefore \quad Mgl = {W_{coil{\rm{ }}}}l$
$\Rightarrow {W}_{\text {coil }}={Mg}$
When current $I$ is passed through the coil and the magnetic field is switched on.
Let ${m}$ mass be added in the first pan to regain the balance.
Then ${Mg} l+{mg} l={W}_{\text {coil }} l+{IBL} \sin 90^{\circ} l$
$\Rightarrow {mg}={IBL}$
$\Rightarrow {m}=\dfrac{{IBL}}{{g}}=\dfrac{4.9 \times 0.6 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-2}}{9.8}$
$\therefore {m}=0.45 \times 10^{-2} {~kg}$

explanation:

$dF = Idl{\mkern 1mu} B\sin 90^\circ = Idl{\mkern 1mu} B$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950572/preptracker/questions/q_43406_explanation_1764950572184.png

question: The path of an electron in a uniform magnetic field may be
explanation:
The path of an electron in a uniform magnetic field is either helical or circular as a centripetal force produces a circular motion
perpendicular to the magnetic field $B$ because of force $F = q(v \times B),$ where $v$ is the velocity of electron and $q$ is the charge. If $v$ and $B$ are perpendicular to each other, then particle will describe a circle, but if $v$ is not perpendicular to $B$, then component of$v$ along $B$ remains unchanged as the motion along the magnetic field will not be affected by the magnetic field. Hence, motion perpendicular to $B$ will be circular producing helical motion as given below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950556/preptracker/questions/q_43337_explanation_1764950556491.png https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950558/preptracker/questions/q_43337_explanation_1764950558464.png

question: A proton travelling at $23^{\circ}$ w.r.t. the direction of a magnetic field of strength $2.6\,mT$ experiences a magnetic force of  $6.5 \times {10^{ - 17}}\,N.$  What is the speed of the proton?
explanation:
Given, $\theta=23^{\circ}$,
$B = 2.6\,mT = 2.6 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,T$
and $F = 6.5 \times {10^{ - 17}}\,N$
We know that, $F=q \nu B \sin \theta$
$6.5 \times 10^{-17}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times v \times 2.6$
$\times 10^{-3} \times \sin 23^{\circ}$
$v = \frac{{6.5 \times {{10}^{ - 17}}}}{{2.6 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 0.39}}$
$\therefore v = 4 \times {10^5}\,\,m{s^{ - 1}}$

explanation:

explanation:

question: A massless square loop, of wire of resistance $10 \Omega$, supporting a mass of $1 g$, hangs vertically with one of its sides in a uniform magnetic field of ${10^3}G$, directed outwards in the shaded region. A $d c$ voltage $V$ is applied to the loop. For what value of $V$, the magnetic force will exactly balance the weight of the supporting mass of $1\,g$? (if sides of the loop $10\,cm,\,g = 10\,m{s^2}$)
explanation:
The magnetic force will act on the side inside the field upwards to balance the weight of supporting mass $m g=I \times L \times B$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957259/preptracker/questions/q_43140_explanation_1764957258489.png $\Rightarrow\left(\dfrac{1}{1000}\right)(10)=\dfrac{V}{R} \times\left(\dfrac{10}{100}\right) \times\left(\dfrac{10^{3}}{10^{4}}\right)$
$\left(\therefore \sin 90^{\circ}=1\right)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{100}=\dfrac{V}{100} \times \dfrac{1}{10} \Rightarrow V=10$ volts
Hence, option (4) is correct.

question:
A proton moving with a constant velocity passes through a region of space without any change in its velocity. If $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}$ represent the electric and magnetic fields respectively, then the region of space may have :
$({ m{A}}){\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} E = 0,B = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,({ m{B}}){\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,E = 0,B e 0$
$({ m{C}}){\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} E e 0,B = 0{ m{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,({ m{D}}){\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} E e 0,B e 0{ m{ }}$
explanation: Using the equation of Lorentz force i.e. $\vec{F}_{q}=\vec{F}_{E}+\vec{F}_{B}$. The net force on a moving charge is the vector sum of forces due to$\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}$ fields.

question:
Assertion: A stationary charged particle in a magnetic field does not experience a force.

Reason: The force acting on a charged particle does not depend on velocity of the particle.
explanation: The magnetic force equation, $F=q v B$, clearly shows that the force depends on the velocity (v) of the charged particle. When $v=0$, the force becomes zero. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: If an electron is not deflected while passing through a certain region of space, then only possibility is that there is no magnetic field in this region.

Reason: Force on electron depends on its velocity also.
explanation: No deflection of an electron doesn't necessarily imply the absence of a magnetic field; it could mean the electron's velocity is parallel or antiparallel to the magnetic field, resulting in zero deflection. Force experienced by a charged particle in a magnetic field $B$ depends on charge, velocity, and the angle between velocity and magnetic field.Reason is true but Assertion is false. So correct option is (4).

question:
Assertion: A uniform magnetic field independent of time can change the velocity of a charged particle.

Reason: It is not possible to change the velocity of a particle in a magnetic field as magnetic field does no work on charged particle.
explanation:
A magnetic field changes the direction of $v$ but not the magnitude of a charged particle's velocity. In such circular motion, $W=0$
$\Rightarrow E_{k}=$ constant
$\Rightarrow|v|=$ constant
So correct option is (3).

question:
Assertion: Free electron always keeps on moving in a conductor. Even then no magnetic force acts on them in magnetic field unless a current is passed through it.

Reason: The average velocity of frec electrons is zero.
explanation: It is not stated that there is any electric field. So, in the absence of an electric current, free electrons in a conductor have a random motion, resulting in a zero net velocity and no magnetic force. Only if an electric current is applied, electrons acquire a drift velocity, creating a current, and the Lorentz force in a magnetic field acts on them. So correct option is (1).

question: A wire $P Q R S$ carrying a current runs along three edges of a cube of side $l$ as shown. There exists a uniform magnetic field of magnitude $B$ along one of the sides of the cube. The magnitude of the force acting on the wire is
explanation:
The magnetic force on a current-carrying wire is given by $F=I \times(l \times B)$, where $l$ is the length vector of the wire and $B$ is the magnetic field. For segments $P Q$ and $R S$, the current is parallel or antiparallel to $B$, so $F=0$. For
segment $Q R F=I \times(l \times B)=I \times\left(l B \sin \left(45^{\circ}\right)\right)$ since $\mathrm{QR}$ is at 45 degrees to $B$. The force magnitude is therefore $F=\sqrt{2} I B$.
Correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Force on a Current Carrying Wire

question: A current of $10\,A$ is flowing in a wire of length $1.5\,m$. A force of $15\,N$ acts on it when it is placed in a uniform magnetic field of $2\,T$. The angle between the magnetic field and the direction of the current is
explanation:
$F=B i l \sin \theta$
$\begin{gathered}\Rightarrow \sin \theta=\dfrac{F}{B i l} \\\Rightarrow \sin \theta=\dfrac{15}{10 \times 1.5 \times 2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \theta=30^{\circ}\end{gathered}$

question: A wire carrying a current $I$ along the positive $x - $axis has length $L$. It is kept in a magnetic field $\vec{B}=(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}) T$. The magnitude of the magnetic force acting on the wire is:
explanation:
$\vec{F}=I(\vec{l} \times \vec{B})$
$=I[(L \hat{i}) \times(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})]$
$ = I\left( {4L\hat j + 3L\hat k} \right)$
$|\vec{F}|=5 I L \quad\left(\right.$ Since $\left.\sqrt{4^{2}+3^{2}}=5\right)$
Correct option is (2).

question: A square current carrying loop is suspended in a uniform magnetic field acting in the plane of the loop. If the force on one arm of the loop is $F$, the net force on the remaining three arms of the loop is:

or $\vec{F}_{2}+\vec{F}_{3}+\vec{F}_{4}=-\vec{F}_{1}=-F$

question: A metallic rod of mass per unit length $0.5\,kg\,{m^{ - 1}}$ is lying horizontally on a smooth inclined plane which makes an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The rod is not allowed to slide down by flowing a current through it when a magnetic field of induction 0.25$T$ acting on it in the vertical direction. The current flowing in the rod to keep it stationary is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950259/preptracker/questions/q_41757_explanation_1764950258896.png $F=i(\vec{L} \times \vec{B})=i L B \sin 90^{\circ}$
$F \cos 30^{\circ}=m g \sin 30^{\circ}$
$B i L \cos 30^{\circ}=m g \sin 30^{\circ}$
$i=\dfrac{m g \tan 30^{\circ}}{B L}=\dfrac{0.5 \times 9.8 \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}{0.25}=\dfrac{19.6}{1.73}=11.3 \mathrm{~A}$

question:
In the product
$\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})=q \vec{v} \times\left(B \hat{i} \times B \hat{j} \times B_{0} \hat{k}\right)$
For $q=1$ and $\vec{v}=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}$ and
$\vec{F}=4 \hat{i}-20 \hat{j}+12 \hat{k}$
What will be the complete expression for $\vec{B}$ ?
explanation:
$\begin{aligned}& \vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \\& \vec{F}=\vec{v} \times \vec{B} \quad(q=1) \\& 4 \hat{i}-20 \hat{j}+12 \hat{k}=\left|\begin{array}{llr}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\2 & 4 & 6 \\B & B & B_{0}\end{array}\right| \\& =\hat{i}\left(4 B_{0}-6 B\right)-\hat{j}\left(2 B_{0}-6 B\right)+\hat{k}(-2 B)\end{aligned}$
From by comparision
$\begin{aligned}& -2 B=12 \Rightarrow B=-6 \\& 4 B_{0}-6 B=4 \\& 4 B_{0}=4-36 \\& B_{0}=-\dfrac{32}{8}=-8\end{aligned}$
$\vec{B}=-6 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Lorentz Force

explanation: According to Fleming's left rule, then direction of magnetic fild is towards the east.

question: A straight wire of length 50$cm$ carrying a current of 2.5$A$ is suspended in mid-air by a uniform magnetic field of 0.5$T$ (as shown in figure). The mass of the wire is $\left( {g = 10\,m\,{s^{ - 2}}} \right)$
explanation:
$\because 1 = 50\,cm,I = 2.5\,A,\,B = 0.5\,T$
Magnetic field $\overrightarrow B $exerts force on straight wire given by $\vec{F}=I l \times B$
It is clear from the above expression that this force will act vertically upwards.
For mid-air suspension, this must be balanced by the force due to gravity.
$\therefore$ Weight of wire $=$ force exerted by magnetic field.
$m g=I l B \sin 90^{\circ}$
$m = \frac{{IlB \times 1}}{g} = \frac{{2.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.5}}{{10}} = 625 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,kg = 62.5gm$

explanation: Deuterium ${ }_{1}^{2} \mathrm{D}$ and helium ${ }_{2}^{4} \mathrm{He}$ are both neutral. Only ions are accelerated by an electric field or magnetic field when ions is moving perpendicular to the magnetic field. Therefore both will not be accelerated.

question: A conducting wire bent in the form of a parabola $y^{2}=2 x$ carries a current $I = 2A$ as shown in the figure. The wire is placed in a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}=-4 \hat{k}$ tesla. The magnetic force on the wire is (in newton)
explanation:

$\begin{aligned}& =i[\text { effective length }] \times \bar{B} \\& =2 \times 4(-\hat{j}) \times-4 \hat{k}=32 \hat{i}\end{aligned}$

question: Two long current carrying thin wires, both with current $I$, are held by insulating threads of length $L$ and are in equilibrium as shown in the figure, with threads making an angle $\theta$ with the vertical. If wires have mass $\lambda$ per unit length then the value of $I$ is: ( $g=$ gravitational acceleration)
explanation:
Let $T$ is the tension in the strings and $F_{B}$ is the magnetic force on the wire of length $L$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956213/preptracker/questions/q_41349_explanation_1764956213168.png At equilibrium
$\begin{aligned}& T \cos \theta=F_{g}=\lambda L g \\& T \sin \theta=I L \dfrac{\mu_{o} I}{2 \pi d}\end{aligned}$
From the above equations we get
$I=2 \sin \theta \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi \lambda g L}{\mu_{o} \cos \theta}}(d=2 L \sin \theta)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Force on a Current Carrying Wire

question: A conductor \[ABCDE\], shaped as shown, carries current \[I\]. It is placed in the \[x-y\] plane with the ends \(A\) and \(E\) on the \(x\)-axis. A uniform magnetic field of magnitude \(B\) exists in the region. Predict the wrong option for the force on the wire
explanation:
To find the Ampere's force on a conductor of any shape, replace the conductor by an imaginary straight conductor joining the two ends of the given conductor. So, if $B$ is in $x$-direction, then the imaginary straight conductor will be along the field and the force acting on it will be zero.
If $B$ is in $y$-direction, then the force will be $\lambda BI$ acting along the $z$-direction.
Similary, if $B$ is in the $z$-direction, then the force will be $\lambda BI$, acting along the negative $y$ direction.

options.text: $\lambda BI$ in the $z$-direction if $B$ is in the $y$ direction
$\lambda BI$ in the negative $y$-direction if $B$ is in the $z$-direction

subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Force on a Current Carrying Wire

question: A wire carrying current $I$ is tied between points $P$ and $Q$ and is in the shape of a circular are of radius $R$ due to a uniform magnetic field $B$ (perpendicular to the plane of the paper, shown) in the vicinity of the wire. If the wire subtends an angle $2 \theta_{0}$ at the centre of the circle (of which it forms an arc) then the tension in the wire is :
explanation:
For small arc length
Let $T$ is the tension inthe string

question: A charge $q$ is moved in magnetic field by distance $dl$. Find the work done by magnetic field
explanation:
Force on the moving charge by the magnetic field
$\overrightarrow F = q\overrightarrow V \times \overrightarrow B $
$\therefore$ Force is always perpendicular to the direction of motion.
Hence work done $dW = \overrightarrow F \cdot \overrightarrow {dl} $ is always zero as $\overrightarrow F $ and $\overrightarrow {dl} $ are perpendicular to each other.

question:
Given below two statements One is labelled as ($A$) and the other is labelled as Reason($R$).

Assertion: In an uniform magnetic field, speed and energy remains the same for a moving charged particle.

Reason: Moving charged particle experiences magnetic force perpendicular to its direction of motion.
explanation:
$v$ remains same if q,m, $\vec{B}$ remain same
$\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$
As it is cross product of vectors,
$\vec{F} \perp \vec{v} \Rightarrow \theta=90^{\circ}$
Here, Work done $=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{S}$ (Note that this is dot product)
$=\mathrm{FS} \cos 90^{\circ}=0$.
Work done $=0$

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question: The expression for the magnetic energy stored in a solenoid of length $L$, in terms of magnetic field $B$ and area $A$ is
explanation:
We know that, magnetic energy stored in a solenoid per unit volume
$U_{B}=\dfrac{U}{V}=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{B^{2}}{\mu_{0}}$
$\Rightarrow U=\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{B^{2} V}{\mu_{0}}=\dfrac{1}{2 \mu_{0}} B^{2} A L \quad(\because V=A L)$
So, the correct option is (1)

question: A small cube of side of  $1{\text{ }}mm$  is placed at the centre of a circular loop of radius  $20{\text{ }}cm.$  If the current in the loop is $2 A$, then the magnetic energy stored inside the cube is    (Assume, $\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}$ in SI units)
explanation:
Given, side of cube, $a=1 {~mm}=1 \times 10^{-3} {~m}$ Radius of circular loop,
$R=20 {~cm}=20 \times 10^{-2} {~m}$ Current in loop, $I=2 A$
As we know that, magnetic field at the centre of loop,
$B=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{R}$
where, $\mu_{0}$ is free space permittivity, i. e.
$4 \pi \times 10^{-7} {H} / {m}$ and magnetic energy stored,
$U=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{B^{2} V}{\mu_{0}}$
where, $V=$ volume of cube.
Therefore, $U=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{\left(\mu_{0} i / 2 R\right)^{2} V}{\mu_{0}}$
$E=\dfrac{1}{8} \times \dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 4}{\left(20 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2}} \times 10^{-9}$
$\left[\because V=a^{3}=\left(10^{-3}\right)^{3}=10^{-9} m^{3}\right]$
$=\dfrac{2 \pi}{400} \times 10^{-12}=\dfrac{200}{400} \pi \times 10^{-14}$
$=\dfrac{3.14}{2} \times 10^{-14}=1.57 \times 10^{-14} {~J}$
So, the correct option is (3)

question: In the adjoining circuit diagram, the readings of ammeter and voltmeter are $2\,A$ and $120\,V$, respectively. If the value of $\mathrm{R}$ is $75\,\Omega $, then the voltmeter resistance will be
explanation:
$\because V_{P Q}=\left(I-I_{g}\right) R=I_{g} \cdot G$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950446/preptracker/questions/q_42794_explanation_1764950445626.png where, $G = $ voltmeter (resistance).

$ \Rightarrow {I_g} = 0.4\,A$

$\Rightarrow G=\dfrac{120}{0.4}=300 \Omega$
So, correct option is (3)

question: A magnetic needle suspended parallel to a magnetic field require $\sqrt 3 \,J$ of work to turn it through $60^{\circ}$. The torque needed to maintain the needle in this portion will be
explanation:
$W=U_{f}-U_{i}=M B\left[\cos 0^{\circ}=\cos 60^{\circ}\right]$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{M B}{2}=\sqrt{3} J$
$\Rightarrow M B=2 \sqrt{3} J$
Torque required, $\tau=M \times B=M B \sin 60^{\circ}$
$ = 2\sqrt 3 \times \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} = 3\,J$
So, correct option is (2)

question: A proton, a deuteron and an $\alpha$ particle are projected perpendicular to the direction of a uniform magnetic field with same kinetic energy. The ratio of the radii of the circular paths described by them is
explanation:
When particle is moving perpendicular to a magnetic field, then it moves in circular path of radius, $r=m v / q B$
$\therefore$ Kinetic energy of all the three particles is same.
$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{2}{m_p}v_p^2 = \frac{1}{2}{m_d}v_D^2\frac{1}{2}v_\alpha ^2$
As we know ${m_p} = 1u,\,{m_d} = 2u,\,{m_\alpha } = 4u$
$\therefore {v_p}:{v_d}:{v_\alpha } = 2:\sqrt 2 :1{\rm{ }}$
${\text{or}}\,{v_d} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{v_p}\,{\text{and}}\,{v_\alpha } = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{v_p}$
${r_p}:{r_d}:{r_\alpha } = \frac{{{m_p}{v_p}}}{{{q_q}B}}:\frac{{{m_d}{v_d}}}{{{q_q}B}}:\frac{{{m_\alpha }{v_d}}}{{{q_\varepsilon }B}}$
$ = \frac{{1 \times {v_p}}}{{1 \times B}}:\frac{{2 \times 1 \times {v_p}}}{{2 \times \sqrt {2 \times B} }}:\frac{{4 \times 1 \times {v_p}}}{{2 \times 2 \times B}}$ $ = 1:\sqrt 2 :1$

question: A particle of charge $2\,C$ is moving with a velocity of $(3\hat i + 4\hat j)m{s^{ - 1}}$ in the presence of magnetic and electric fields. If the magnetic field is $(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) T$ and the electric field is $(-2 \hat{k}) N C^{-1}$, then the Lorentz force on the particle is
explanation:
Lorentz force $\vec{F}=q(\vec{E}+\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$
$=2[-2 \hat{k}+(3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}) \times(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})]$
$=2[-2 \hat{k}+6 \hat{k}-9 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}+12 \hat{i}]$
$=2[12 \hat{i}-9 j]=[24 \hat{i}-18 \hat{j}]$
$\therefore F = \sqrt {{{(24)}^2} + {{(18)}^2}} $
$ = \sqrt {576 + 324} = \sqrt {900} = 30\,N$

question: Two charged particles $A$ and $B$ of masses $m$ and $2\,m$, charges $2\,q$ and $3\,q$ respectively moving with same velocity enter a uniform magnetic field such that both the particles make same angle $\left( { < 90^\circ } \right)$ with the direction of the magnetic field. Then the ratio of the pitches of the helical paths of the particles $A$ and $B$ is
explanation:
The pitch of the helical path traced out by a charged particle in uniform magnetic field is given by
$p=\dfrac{2 \pi m}{q B}(v \cos \theta)$
$\Rightarrow p \alpha \dfrac{m}{q}$
$\therefore \dfrac{p_{1}}{p_{2}}=\dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{2}} \cdot \dfrac{q_{2}}{q_{1}}$
but, $m_{1}=m, q_{1}=2 q$ and $m_{2}=2 m, q_{2}=3 q$
$\therefore \dfrac{p_{1}}{p_{2}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{m}{2 q}}{\dfrac{2 m}{3 q}}=\dfrac{3}{4}$

question:
Given the fact that
A. Magnetic field exerts force only on a moving charge.
B. Electric field exerts force on both stationary and moving charge.
C. Magnetic field exerts force on a charge moving parallel to the direction of the field. Which of the following is true?
explanation:
As we know that, magnetic field ($B$) is produced due to current i. e. flow of charge per unit time.
But electric field is generated by any charged particle no matter it is static/moving.
Hence, magnetic field exerts force only on moving charge but electric field exerts force on moving as well as static both. Force on a charged particle, $F=q u B \sin \theta$ For parallel motion, $\theta=0^{\circ}$ or $\theta=180^{\circ}$
$\therefore \sin 0^{\circ}=0$ and $180^{\circ}=0$
$F=0$
So statement $A$ and $B$ are true.
So, the correct option is (2)

question: A proton accelerated by a potential difference of  $500{\text{ }}kV$  flies through a uniform transverse magnetic field of $0.1 T$. The field is spread on a region of  $1.0{\text{ }}cm$  thickness. The angle through which the proton gets deviated from its original direction is (mass of proton $=1.6 \times 10^{-27} {~kg}$ and charge of proton $=1.6 \times 10^{-19} {C}$ )
explanation:
According to the question, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952756/preptracker/questions/q_111197_explanation_1764952756401.png
Here first, we have to find the radius of circular path, (shown in figure) which is given by relation,
$R=\dfrac{\sqrt{2 m(K E)}}{q B}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{q B}$
Now, putting the values, where mass of proton
$m=1.6 \times 10^{-27} {~kg}$,
$q=1.6 \times 10^{-19} {C}$, potential difference,
$V=500 {kV}$, and magnetic field, $B=0.1 T$
putting the given values, we get
$R=\dfrac{\sqrt{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-27} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 500 \times 10^{3}}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.1}$
$\Rightarrow R=1 {~m}$
Since, arc is small, so it can be assumed that it can be equal to the thickness of the field.
Hence, $\phi+\angle A D C=\pi$ (from figure) $\quad \quad (1)$
where, $\phi=\dfrac{\operatorname{arc}}{\text { radius }}=\dfrac{10^{-2}}{1}=0.01 {rad}$
$\because \theta=\pi-\angle A D C$ (from figure) $\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$\phi=\theta$
So, $\phi=0.01 {rad}$
Hence, the correct option is (1).

question: A charge $q$ enters in a region having electric field $\vec{E}$ and magnetic field $\vec{B}$ with velocity $\vec{v}$. If it continues to move with the same velocity, then which of the following statement is not true
explanation:
Given $\vec{B}=\vec{V} \times \vec{E}$
We know that by the relation.
$\vec{E}=-(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$
$\vec{v} \times \vec{E}=-\vec{v} \times[\vec{v} \times \vec{B}]$
By vector triple product
$\vec{v} \times[\vec{v} \times \vec{B}]=\vec{v}(\vec{v} \cdot \vec{B})-\vec{B}(\vec{v} \cdot \vec{B})$
$\vec{v} \times \vec{E}=-\left[v(v \cdot B)-B v^{2}\right]$
$=-v(v \cdot B)+B v^{2}$
This equal to $\vec{B}$ only if
$-v(v \cdot B)+B v^{2}=B$.
$\therefore \vec{v} \times \vec{E}=\vec{B}$ in not true.
So, the correct option is (4)

$\Rightarrow \vec{E}=\vec{v} \times \vec{B} \Rightarrow \vec{E} \perp \vec{B}$
$E=v B=\dfrac{c}{100} \times 9 \times 10^{-4}$
$ = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{100}} \times 9 \times {10^{ - 4}} = 27 \times {10^2}V{m^{ - 1}}$

question: A charged particle of mass ${m=1 {mg}}$ and charge ${q=1 \mu {C}}$ enters along ${A B}$ at point ${A}$ in a uniform magnetic field ${B=1.2 {~T}}$ existing in the rectangular region of size ${a \times b}$, where ${a=4 {~m}}$ and ${b=3 {~m}}$. The particle leaves the region exactly at corner point ${C}$. What is the speed ${v\left(\right.}$ in  of the particle?
explanation:
Let speed of the particle is $v$ (speed will remain constant)
$r = AO = CO = \frac{{mv}}{{qB}}$
${\text{also}}\,{\text{ }}\frac{a}{r} = \sin \theta ,\frac{{r - b}}{r} = \cos \theta $
Solve the above equation to get
$v = \frac{{qB\left( {{a^2} + {b^2}} \right)}}{{2mb}}$
$ = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 6}} \times 1.2\left( {{4^2} + {3^2}} \right)}}{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} \times 3}} = 5\,m/s$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950642/preptracker/questions/q_43783_explanation_1764950642248.png

question: A charged particle enters a uniform magnetic field with velocity ${v_{0}=4 {~m} / {s}}$ perpendicular to it, the length of magnetic field is ${x=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} R}$, where ${R}$ is the radius of the circular path of the particle in the field. Find the magnitude of change in velocity (in $m/s$) of the particle when it comes out of the field.
explanation:
The particle will come out of the magnetic field at an angle $\theta=60^{\circ}$ with the original direction.
$\Delta \vec v = \left( {{v_0}\cos 60\hat i + {v_0}\sin 60\hat j - {v_0}\hat i} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow |\Delta \vec v| = {v_0}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956492/preptracker/questions/q_43779_explanation_1764956491744.png
So correct option is 4.

question: A charged particle ${q=1 \mu {C}}$ enters a region of uniform magnetic field ${B=0.4 {~T}}$ (into the page) and is deflected ${d=5 {~cm}}$ after travelling a horizontal distance ${a=10 {~cm}}$. Find the magnitude of the momentum   of the particle.
explanation: From the figure, $r^{2}=(r-d)^{2}+a^{2}$$\Rightarrow r^{2}=r^{2}+d^{2}-2 r d+a^{2}$   https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956490/preptracker/questions/q_43759_explanation_1764956490090.png   $\Rightarrow \quad r=\dfrac{d^{2}+a^{2}}{2 d} \Rightarrow \dfrac{m \nu}{q B}=\dfrac{d^{2}+a^{2}}{2 d}$$\Rightarrow \quad p=m v=\dfrac{q B}{2}\left[\dfrac{d^{2}+a^{2}}{d}\right]$$\Rightarrow \quad p=\dfrac{10^{-6} \times 0.4}{2}\left[\dfrac{(0.05)^{2}+(0.10)^{2}}{0.05}\right]$$=5 \times 10^{-8} {~kg} {~m} / {s}$

question: A non-uniform magnetic field ${B=B_{0}\left(1+\dfrac{y}{d}\right) \hat{k}}$ is present in region of space in between ${y=0}$ and ${y=d}$. The lines are shown in the figure. A particle of mass ${m}$ and positive charge ${q}$ is moving. Given, an initial velocity ${v=v_{0} \hat{i}}$. The ${x}$-component of velocity of the particle is ${v_{x}=v_{0}-\dfrac{k q B_{0} d}{2 m}}$ when it leaves the field, then  find  the value of ${k}$
explanation:
Let at time ${t}$, particle be at point ${P(x, y)}$ and its velocity be
$\begin{aligned}& v=\left(v_{x} \hat{i}+v_{y} \hat{j}\right) \\& |v|=\left|v_{0}\right| \Rightarrow v_{0}^{2}=v_{x}^{2}+v_{y}^{2}\end{aligned}$
Then, magnetic force on the particle at point ${P}$ is
$\begin{aligned}& F=q\left(v_{x} \hat{i}+v_{y} \hat{j}\right) \cdot B_{0}\left(1+\dfrac{y}{d}\right)(-\hat{k}) \\\Rightarrow \quad & -q B_{0}\left[1+\dfrac{y}{d}\right] d y=m d v_{x}\end{aligned}$
Now, when the particle will be coming out of the at that point ${y=d}$. Let the velocity in ${X}$-direction be ${v_{x}}$, then integrating we get
$ = - \frac{{q{B_0}}}{m}\int_0^d {\left[ {1 + \frac{y}{d}} \right]} \,dy$
$ = - \frac{{q{B_0}}}{m}\left[ {d + \frac{{{d^2}}}{{2d}}} \right] = - \frac{{3q{B_0}d}}{{2m}}$
So, ${\quad v_{x}=v_{0}-\dfrac{3 q B_{0} d}{2 m}}$
${\therefore \quad k=3}$

question:
A proton of mass $1.67 \times {10^{ - 27}}\,kg$ enters a uniform magnetic field of $1\,T$ at point $A$ as shown in figure, with a speed of ${10^7}\,m\,{s^{ - 1}}$

The magnetic field is directed normal to the plane of paper downwards. The proton emerges out of the magnetic field at point $C$, then the distance $A C$ and the value of angle $\theta$ will respectively be
explanation:
From the symmetry of figure, the angle $\theta=45^{\circ}$. The path of moving proton in a normal magnetic field is circular. If $r$ is the radius of the circular path, then from the figure
$A C=2 r \cos 45^{\circ}=2 r \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\sqrt{2} r$
As $B q v=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}$ or $r=\dfrac{m v}{B q}$
$\therefore A C=\dfrac{\sqrt{2} m v}{B q}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2} \times 1.67 \times 10^{-27} \times 10^{7}}{1 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,$$ = 0.14\,m$
