question: A uniform electric field and a uniform magnetic field are acting along the same direction in a certain region. If an electron is projected in the region such that its velocity is pointed along the direction of fields, then the electron
explanation:
The total force acting on the electron due to electric and magnetic fields is
$\vec{F}=\vec{F}_{E}+\vec{F}_{B}=-e \vec{E}+(-e)(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$
As $\vec v$ and $\vec B$ are in the same direction, so $\vec v \times \vec B = 0,$
$\therefore \quad \vec F = - e\vec E + 0 = - e\vec E$
Since the electric field opposes the motion of the electron, hence its speed will decrease.

options.text: will turn towards left of direction of motion
will turn towards right of direction of motion

question: A proton and an $\alpha$-particle accelerated through the same potential difference enter a region of uniform magnetic field normally. If the radius of the proton orbit is $10\,cm$, then the radius of $\alpha$-particle is
explanation:
If $v$ be velocity acquired by a charged particle on being accelerated through potential difference $V$, then
$\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = qV$ or $v = \sqrt {\frac{{2qV}}{m}} $$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
where $q$ is the charge on the particle and $m$ its mass.
On entering a region of uniform magnetic field $B$ normally, it follows a circular path of radius $r$ and it is given by
$r = \frac{{mv}}{{qB}} = \frac{m}{{qB}}\sqrt {\frac{{2qV}}{m}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( {{\text{using}}\,(1)} \right)$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$$ = \frac{1}{B}\sqrt {\frac{{2mV}}{q}} $
For the same values of $V$ and $B$,
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$$r \propto \sqrt {\frac{m}{q}} $
$\therefore \frac{{{r_\alpha }}}{{{r_p}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{m_\alpha }}}{{{m_p}}}\frac{{{q_p}}}{{{q_\alpha }}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{4m}}{m}\frac{e}{{2e}}} = \sqrt 2 $
${r_\alpha } = \sqrt 2 {r_p} = \sqrt 2 (10\,cm) = 10\sqrt 2 \,cm$

question: An electron moving in a uniform magnetic field of induction of intensity  $B,$  has its radius directly proportional to
explanation:
The force due to magnetic field acting on the electron of mass $m$,
$F = evB\quad \quad \quad (1)$
The centripetal force is given by
$F = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r}\quad \quad \quad (2)$
where, $r$ is radius of circular path.
Equating Eqn. (1) with Eqn. (2), we get
$F = qvB = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r}$
$r = \frac{{mv}}{{qB}}$
$ \Rightarrow r \propto v$
Hence, the radius is directly proportional to speed.

question: A proton moving vertically downward enters a magnetic field pointing towards North. In which direction proton will deflect?
explanation: Proton will represent the direction of current. So, the direction of current is vertically downward. According to Fleming's left hand rule, if middle finger represents the direction of current and forefinger represents the direction of magnetic field, then thumb will represent the direction of Lorentz force acting on the proton which deflects the proton in East direction.

question: An electron moves at right angle to a magnetic field of  $1.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,T$  with a speed of  $6 \times {10^7}m{s^{ - 1}}.$ If the specific charge of the electron is  $1.7 \times {10^{11}}\,C\,k{g^{ - 1}},$  the radius of the circular path will be
explanation:
The formula for radius of circular path is
$r = \frac{{mv}}{{eB}} = \frac{v}{{\left( {\frac{e}{m}} \right)B}}\quad \quad \quad (1)$
Here, $\frac{e}{m} = 1.7 \times {10^{11}}\,C\,k{g^{ - 1}}$
$r = \frac{{6 \times {{10}^7}}}{{1.7 \times {{10}^{11}} \times 1.5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}$
$ = 2.35 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m$
$ = 2.35\,\,cm$

question: A plane metallic sheet is placed with its face parallel to lines of magnetic induction $B$ of a uniform field. A particle of mass $m$ and charge $q$ is projected with a velocity $v$ from a distance $d$ from the plane normal to the lines of induction. Then, the maximum velocity of projection for which the particle does not hit the plate is
explanation:
The charged particle bends in a circle of radius $r$.
$\therefore B q v \sin \dfrac{\pi}{2}=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r} \quad\left(\because \sin \dfrac{\pi}{2}=1\right)$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,r = \frac{{mv}}{{Bq}}$
The particle does not hit the plate, if $r \leq d$
or $\quad \dfrac{m v}{B q} \leq d$
or $v \le \frac{{Bqd}}{m}$
$\therefore \quad v_{\max }=\dfrac{B q d}{m}$

question: In a mass spectrometer used for measuring the masses of ions, the ions are initially accelerated by an electric potential $V$ and then made to describe semicircular paths of radius $R$ using a magnetic field $B$. If $V$ and $B$ are kept constant, then the ratio$\left. {\left( {\frac{{{\rm{charge}}\,\,{\rm{on}}\,\,{\rm{the}}\,\,{\rm{ion}}}}{{{\rm{mass}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{the}}\,\,{\rm{ion}}}}} \right.} \right)$will be proportional to
explanation:
Magnetic Lorentz force will provide required centripetal force.
Hence, $\dfrac{m v^{2}}{R}=q v B$
$\dfrac{q B}{m v}=\dfrac{1}{R}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{q}{m}=\dfrac{1}{R}\left(\dfrac{v}{B}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(\dfrac{q}{m}\right) \propto \dfrac{1}{R}$

question: A particle of specific charge ${\dfrac{q}{m}=\pi {Ckg}^{-1}}$ is projected from the origin towards positive ${x}$ - axis with the velocity ${10 {~ms}^{-1}}$ in a uniform magnetic field ${\vec{B}=-2 \hat{k} T}$. explanation:

Particle will be at point ${P}$ after time
${t=\dfrac{1}{12} s=\dfrac{1}{12}=\dfrac{T}{12}}$
It is deviated by angle ${\theta=\dfrac{2 \pi}{12}=30^{\circ}}$
${\therefore}$ Velocity of particle at point ${P}$
${\vec{v}=10 \cos 30 \hat{i}+10 \sin 30 \hat{j}}$
${=10\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \hat{i}+\dfrac{1}{2} \hat{j}\right)}$
${\vec{v}=5(\sqrt{3} \hat{i}+\hat{j})}$

question:
An electron is moving along the positive $x$-axis. If the uniform magnetic field is applied parallel to the negative $z$-axis, then
A. The electron will experience magnetic force along positive $y$-axis.
B. The electron will experience magnetic force along negative $y$-axis.
C. The electron will not experience any force in magnetic field.
D. The electron will continue to move along the positive-axis.
E. The electron will move along circular path in magnetic field.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
explanation:
As, the electron is moving in $+x$-axis and uniform magnetic field is in negative $z$-axis. So, electron will experience magnetic force along negative $y$-axis.
Force on any moving charge is given by
$F=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$
As, $\vec{F}=-e(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$
Electron will move in a circular path in magnetic field.
So, correct option is (1).

Reason: The effect of magnetic field on a charged particle, is to change its path from linear to parabolic.
explanation:
The radius of the circular path is directly proportional to the momentum is correct.
$r=\dfrac{m v}{q B}=\dfrac{p}{q B}$
$\Rightarrow r \propto p$
The reason is false as path is circular (not parabolic). So correct option is (3).

$F=q v B$
$E_{k}=\dfrac{q^{2} B^{2} r^{2}}{2 m}$

question:

explanation:
In non-relativistic regime$\left(M v^{2} / R\right)=Q v B \Rightarrow v_{\max }=\dfrac{Q B R}{M}$
Where $R$ is radius of Dees of cyclotron. In a cyclotron, the time for an ion to complete one revolution is independent of its velocity due to an alternating electric field with some frequency $f_{\text {supply }}$.
$T=\dfrac{2 \pi M}{Q B}$
However, as ions approach the speed of light, relativistic effects limit further acceleration. So correct option is (3).

question: A proton is projected with a velocity, ${10^7}\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ at right angles to a uniform magnetic field of induction $10\,mT$. The time (in seconds) taken by the proton to traverse $90^{\circ}$ arc is: (Mass of proton $ = 1.65 \times {10^{ - 27}}\,kg$ and charge of proton $ = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}\,kg)$
explanation:
The time $t$ to traverse a $90^{\circ}$ arc in a magnetic field is a quarter of the period $T$ of circular motion, $t=T / 4$. The period $T$ is given by $T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{q B}$, where $m$ is the mass of the proton, $q$ is the charge of the proton, and $B$ is the magnetic field. With
$m=1.65 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}, q=1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ and
$B=10 m T$, we can calculate $t$. Let's perform the calculation.
The time taken by the proton to traverse a $90^{\circ}$
$90^{\circ}$ arc in the magnetic field is $1.62 \times 10^{-6}$ which corresponds to option (2). The calculation is based on the period of the circular motion and the charge-to-mass ratio of the proton.

question: A proton moving in perpendicular magnetic field possess energy ' $E$ '. The strength of magnetic field is increased four times. But the proton is constrained to move in the path of same radius. The kinetic energy of proton will increase
explanation:
Kinetic energy of proton, $E_{K}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
In magnetic field,
$E_{K}=\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) m\left(\dfrac{q^{2} B^{2} R^{2}}{m^{2}}\right)$
As charge $q$, mass $M$ and radius $R$ are constant in both cases.
$E_{K} \propto B^{2}$
As $B$ increased 4 times, $\left(E_{K}\right)$ will increase by 16 times.

question: $\alpha$-particle is revolving in a circular path with radius $r$ with speed $v$, then find the value of magnetic dipole moment.
explanation:
Charge on $\alpha$-particle, $q=2 e$
Time period of $\alpha$ - particle,
$T=\dfrac{2 \pi r}{v}$
$\therefore$ Current associated by $\alpha-$ particle,
$I=\dfrac{q}{T}=\dfrac{2 e}{\dfrac{2 \pi r}{v}} \Rightarrow I=\dfrac{e v}{\pi r}$
Magnetic dipole moment,
$\mu=I \cdot A=\dfrac{e v}{\pi r} \cdot \pi r^{2}$
$\therefore \mu=e v r$
So, correct option is (2)

question: If two protons are moving with speed $v = 4.5 \times {10^5}\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ parallel to each other, then find the value of ratio of electrostatic and magentic force between them.
explanation:
Given, speed of proton,
$v = 4.5 \times {10^5}\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Electrostatic force between two protons,
${F_e} = k\frac{{{e^2}}}{{{r^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Magnetic field produced due to moving proton of speed $v$,
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }} \cdot \frac{{ev}}{{{r^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
$\therefore$ Magnetic force on the proton,
$F_{m}=B e v$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{e v}{r^{2}} \cdot e v$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{[From Eq}}{\rm{. (2)]}}$
${F_m} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }} \cdot \frac{{{e^2}{v^2}}}{{{r^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (3), we get
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{F_{e}}{F_{m}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{k e^{2}}{r^{2}}}{\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{e^{2} v^{2}}{r^{2}}}=\dfrac{k \cdot 4 \pi}{v^{2} \cdot \mu_{0}} \\& \dfrac{F_{e}}{F_{m}}=\dfrac{9 \times 10^{9} \times 4 \times 31.4}{\left(4.5 \times 10^{5}\right)^{2} \times 4 \pi \times 10^{-7}} \\& =4.4 \times 10^{5}\end{aligned}$

question:
Assertion: Electron moving perpendicular to B will perform circular motion

Reason: Force by magnetic field is perpendicular to velocity.
explanation:
That an electron moving perpendicular to $\mathrm{a}$ magnetic field will perform circular motion. This is correct. When a charged particle, such as anelectron, moves perpendicular to a magnetic field, it experiences a magnetic force directed perpendicular to both the magnetic field and the particle's velocity. When the particle moves perpendicular to the magnetic field $(e = 90$ degrees$), \sin (90$ degrees $)=1$, and the magnetic force is indeed perpendicular to the velocity.
The Reason correctly explains why the Assertion is true. Therefore, the correct answer is (1).

question:
Assertion: A proton and an alpha particle having the same kinetic energy are moving in circular paths in a uniform magnetic field. The radii of their circular paths will be equal.

Reason: Any two charged particles having equal kinetic energies and entering a region of uniform magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$ in a direction perpendicular to
$\mathbf{B}$, will describe circular trajectories of equal radii
explanation:
A proton and an alpha particle, both having the same kinetic energy, are moving in circular paths in a uniform magnetic field, and the radii of their circular paths will be equal. This is correct. When particles with the same chargeto-mass ratio and the same kinetic energy enter a uniform magnetic field perpendicularly, they will indeed follow circular paths with equal radii.
Any two charged particles having equal kinetic energies and entering a region of a uniform magnetic field $B$ in a direction perpendicular to $B$ will describe circular trajectories of equal radii. This statement is not entirely accurate.
So, while the Assertion is correct, the Reason is not entirely correct, making the correct answer (3)

question: A uniform magnetic field, $B=B_{0} \hat{j}$ exists in space. A particle of mass $m$ and charge $q$, is projected towards $x$-axis with speed $v$, from a point $(a, 0,0)$. The maximum value of $v$ for which the particle does not hit the $yz$-plane is
explanation:
Here, the particle is projected in a direction perpendicular to the uniform magnetic field, hence, it will describe a circular path. The particle will not hit the $yz$-plane, if the radius of the circular path is smaller than $a$. For maximum value of $v$, the radius of circular path is just equal to $a$.
Hence, $\quad \dfrac{m v}{B q}=a \quad$ or $\quad v=\dfrac{B q a}{m}$

$\therefore$ Time period of proton $>$ Time period of electron

Their time periods will be same

question: A deuteron of kinetic energy 50$keV$ is describing a circular orbit of radius 0.5$m$, in a plane perpendicular to magnetic field $B$. The kinetic energy of a proton that describes circular orbit of radius 0.5$m$ in the same plane with the same magnetic field is:
explanation:
In this case, magnetic force provides necessary centripetal force
i.e., $q v B=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}$
Radius of path, $r=\dfrac{m v}{B q}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2 m E}}{q B}$
$mE = $ Constant
$ \Rightarrow {E_1} = \frac{{{m_D}E}}{{{m_P}}} = \frac{{(2{m_P})}}{{{m_p}}} \times 50keV\,\,(\because {m_D} = 2{m_P})$
$ = 100keV$

question: A proton is moving in a uniform magnetic field $B$ in a circular path of radius $a$ in a direction perpendicular to $z$-axis along which field $B$ exists. Calculate the angular momentum, of the proton about center:
explanation:
Under uniform magnetic field, force $qvB$ acts on proton and provides the necessary centripetal force $m v^{2} / a$.
$\therefore \frac{{m{v^2}}}{a} = evB \Rightarrow v = \frac{{aeB}}{m}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Now, angular momentum,
$\begin{aligned}& J=m v r \\& \Rightarrow J=m\left(\dfrac{a e B}{m}\right) a=a^{2} e B\end{aligned}$

question: An electron moves straight inside a charged parallel plate capacitor of uniform surface charge density $\sigma$. The space between the plates is filled with constant magnetic field of induction $B$, as shown in the figure. Neglecting gravity, the time of straight line motion of the electron in the capacitor is
explanation:
The net electric field
$E = {E_1} + {E_2} \Rightarrow E = \frac{\sigma }{{2{\varepsilon _0}}} + \frac{\sigma }{{2{\varepsilon _0}}} = \frac{\sigma }{{{\varepsilon _0}}}$
The net force acting on the electron is zero because it moves with constant velocity ${F_{net{\rm{ }}}} = {F_e} + {F_m} = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \left| {{F_e}} \right| = \left| {{F_m}} \right|$ or $eE = evB$
Or
$v = \frac{E}{B} = \frac{\sigma }{{{\varepsilon _0}\,B}}$
$\therefore$ The time of motion inside the capacitor
$ = t = \frac{l}{{\,V}} = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}l\,B}}{\sigma }$

question: In a cyclotron, if a deuteron can gain an energy of 40$MeV$ then a proton can gain an energy of
explanation:
$F=q v B=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}=$ Centripetal force
$E=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{B^{2} q^{2} r^{2}}{m} \text { Maximum energy, }$
Hence, ratio of energy of deuteron and proton is
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{E_{d}}{E_{p}}=\left(\dfrac{q_{d}}{q_{p}}\right)^{2}\left(\dfrac{m_{p}}{m_{d}}\right) \\& \dfrac{40}{E_{p}}=\left(\dfrac{q}{q}\right)^{2}\left(\dfrac{m}{2 m}\right) \Rightarrow E_{p}=80 \mathrm{MeV}\end{aligned}$

question: Cyclotron is adjusted to give proton beam, magnetic induction is $0.15wb{m^{ - 2}}$ and the extreme radius is 1.5$m$. The energy of emergent proton in $MeV$ will be
explanation:
Radius of circular path traced by moving charged particle in uniform magnetic field
$r=\dfrac{m v}{B q}$
$\therefore V_{\max }=\dfrac{B q r_{\max }}{m}$
The maximum $KE$ that it can get in cyclotron
$\begin{gathered}=k_{\max }=\dfrac{1}{2} m\left(V_{\max }\right)^{2} \\k_{\text {max }}=\dfrac{1}{2} m\left(\dfrac{B q r_{\max }}{m}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{B^{2} q^{2} r_{\max }^{2}}{2 m}=2.4 \mathrm{MeV}\end{gathered}$

explanation:

explanation:

question: Let $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}$ denote the electric and magnetic fields in a certain region of space. A proton moving with a velocity along a straight line enters the region and is found to pass through it undeflected. Indicate which of the following statement is not consistent with the observations:
explanation:
(1) If $E eq 0$ but parallel to motion of charged particle the particle undergo straight line motion (but not with constant speed)
(2) $E=0, B=0$ definitely $F=0$. So straight line motion is continuous
(3) $\mathrm{E}$ is parallel to $V$ and $B$ is perpedicular to $V$ definitely the particle undergoes deviation with its motion.
(4) $E eq 0 B eq 0$ both are parallel then force due to $B$ is zero so it continues straight line motion with variable velocity. So option (3) is correct.

question: A parallel plate capacitor is moving with a velocity of 25$m/s$ through a uniform magnetic field of 1.5$T$ as shown in figure. If the electric field within the capacitor plates is 175$N/C$ and plate area is$25 \times {10^{ - 7}}\,{m^2}$, then the magnetic force experienced by positive charge plate is
explanation:

$Q = {\varepsilon _o}A \times E = 8.85 \times {10^{ - 12}} \times 25 \times {10^{ - 7}} \times 175$
$ = 3.87 \times {10^{ - 15}}C$

question:
An electron and a proton moving in parallel paths with same velocity enter a semi-infinite region of uniform magnetic filed perpendicular to velocity. Which of these are correct?
I. They will never come out of magnetic field region.
II. They will come out travelling along parallel paths.
III. They will come out same time.
IV. They will come out at different times.
explanation:
Both particles travle in circular motion inside the magnetic field. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950297/preptracker/questions/q_41846_explanation_1764950297011.png $r=\dfrac{m v}{B q} \Rightarrow r \propto m \Rightarrow r_{e} < r_{p}\left(m_{P}>m_{e}\right)$
The two particles come out parallel to each other. and time taken to complete one revolution
$\begin{aligned}& T=\dfrac{2 \pi m}{B q} \Rightarrow T \propto m \\& \Rightarrow T_{e} < T_{p} .\end{aligned}$
So option (3) is correct.

question:
Consider a moving charged particle in a region of magnetic field. Which of the following are correct?
I. If $v$ is parallel to $B$, then path of particle is spiral.
II. If $v$ is perpendicular to $B$, then path of particle is a circle.
III. If $v$ has a component along $B$, then path of particle is helical.
IV. If $v$ is along $B$, then path of the particle is a circle.
explanation: If $v$ is parallel to $B$ then the particle travels along a straight line. If $v \perp B$ then path is a circular motion and if $v$ has a component along $B$, then path will be helical. So option (2) is correct.

question:
The energy of a charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field does not change.
Work done by the magnetic field on a charged particle is zero.
explanation: The force on a charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field always acts in direction perpendicular to the direction of motion of the charge. As work done by magnetic field on the charge is zero, $[W=F S \cos \theta]$, so the energy of the charged particle does not change. So option (3) is correct.

Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question:
Assertion: A charged particle moves perpendicular to a magnetic field. Its kinetic energy remains constant, but momentum changes.

Reason: Force acts on the moving charged particles in the magnetic field.
explanation:
The charged particle would move along a circle.
Magnitude of velocity remains constant, but its direction goes on changing. That is why
$K E=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ remains constant but $p$ goes on changing.

question: A proton beam enters a magnetic field of ${10^{ - 4}}\,Wb\,{m^{ - 2}}$ normally. If the specific charge of the proton is ${10^{11}}Ck{g^{ - 1}}$ and its velocity is ${10^9}\,m\,{s^{ - 1}}$, then the radius of the circle described will be
explanation:
When the proton beam enters the magnetic field B normally, it describes a circular path of radius $r$ given by
$r=\dfrac{m v}{e B}=\dfrac{v}{\dfrac{e}{m} B}$
when $\frac{e}{m}$is the specific charge of the proton and $v$ is its velocity.
Here,
$v = {10^9}\,m\,{s^{ - 1}},\frac{e}{m} = {10^{11}}Ck{g^{ - 1}}$
$B = {10^{ - 4}}\,Wb\,{m^{ - 2}}$
$\therefore r = \frac{{{{10}^9}\,m{s^{ - 1}}}}{{\left( {{{10}^{11}}Ck{g^{ - 1}}} \right)\left( {{{10}^{ - 4}}Wb{m^{ - 2}}} \right)}} = 100\,m$

question: An $\alpha$-particle and a proton moving with the same kinetic energy enter a region of uniform magnetic field at right angles to the field. The ratio of the radii of the paths $\alpha$-particle to that of the proton is
explanation:
Radius of the circular path of a charged particle in the magnetic field is
$r = \frac{{mv}}{{qB}}$
Kinetic energy of the charged particle,
$K = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$
or $v=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 K}{m}} \therefore r=\dfrac{m}{q B} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 K}{m}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}$
$r \propto \dfrac{\sqrt{m}}{q}$ For the same value of $K$ and $B$.
$\therefore \frac{{{r_\alpha }}}{{{r_p}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{m_\alpha }}}{{{m_p}}}} \times \frac{{{q_p}}}{{{q_\alpha }}} = \sqrt {\frac{{4\,m}}{{\,m}}} \times \frac{e}{{2e}} = 1$

question: An $\alpha$ particle of energy 10$eV$ is moving in a circular path in uniform magnetic field. The energy of proton moving in the same path and same magnetic field will be [ mass of $\alpha$ particle $=4$ times mass of proton]
explanation:
The radius of circular path of charged particle in uniform magnetic field,
$R = \frac{{mv}}{{qB}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
The kinetic energy of charged particle
$K = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$
$ \Rightarrow v = \sqrt {\frac{{2K}}{m}} \,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Substituting eq.(2) in eq.(1), we get
$R=\dfrac{m \sqrt{\dfrac{2 K}{m}}}{q B}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}$
For proton
${R_p} = \frac{{\sqrt {2{m_p}{K_p}} }}{{{q_p}B}}\,\,\,(3)$
Where mass of proton is $m_{p}$, kinetic energy of proton is $K_{p}$ and charge on proton is $q_{p}$
Similarly for $\alpha$ particle
${R_\alpha } = \frac{{\sqrt {2{m_\alpha }{K_\alpha }} }}{{{q_\alpha }B}}\,\,(4)$
where mass of $\alpha$ particle is $m_{\alpha}$, charge on $\alpha$ particle is $q_{\alpha}$ and kinetic energy of $\alpha$ particle is $K_{\alpha}$
Here, $m_{\alpha}=4 m_{p}$ and $q_{\alpha}=2 q_{p}$,
Substituting value in eq.(4), we get $R_{\alpha}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2\left(4 m_{p}\right) K_{\alpha}}}{B\left(2 q_{p}\right)}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2 m_{p} K_{\alpha}}}{B q_{p}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{R_{p}}{R_{\alpha}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{K_{p}}{K_{\alpha}}}$
$A s, R_{p}=R_{\alpha}$
$K_{\alpha}=K_{p}=10 \mathrm{eV}$

question: A neutron, a proton, an electron and an aparticle enter a region of uniform magnetic field with the same velocities. The magnetic field is perpendicular and directed into the plane of the paper. The tracks of the particles are labelled in the figure. The elecron follows the track.
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957090/preptracker/questions/q_41697_explanation_1764957090250.png A neutron is electrically neutral particle. It will pass undeflected, therefore it follows track $C$. Electron is a negatively charged particle. According to Fleming's left hand rule, the magnetic force on the electron will be towards right. Therefore, it follows track $D$.
Protonand alphaparticle are positively charged particles. In accordance with Fleming's left hand rule magnetic force on these particles will be towards left. Among the tracks $A$ and $B$, one is of proton and other of $\alpha$-particle.
As $r = \frac{{mv}}{{Bq}}\therefore \frac{{{r_\alpha }}}{{{r_p}}} = \frac{{{m_\alpha }}}{{{m_p}}} \times \frac{{{q_p}}}{{{q_\alpha }}} = \left( {\frac{4}{1}} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right) = 2$
Therefore. $\alpha$-particle follows track B and proton follows track $A$.

question: An alternating electric field of frequency $v$, is applied across the dees (radius $=R$ ) of a cyclotron that is being used to accelerate protons (mass $=m$). The operating magnetic field ($B$) used in the cyclotron and the kinetic energy ($K$) of the proton beam, produced by it, are given by
explanation:
Frequency, $f=\dfrac{e B}{2 \pi m}$
$K E=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ and radius $R=\dfrac{m v}{e B}$
Here, velocity, $v=\dfrac{\pi R}{\dfrac{T}{2}}=\dfrac{2 \pi R}{T}=2 \pi R f$
$\therefore \quad$ Radius, $R=\dfrac{m(2 \pi R f)}{e B}$
Magnetic field, $B=\dfrac{2 \pi m f}{e}$
Kinetic energy, $K=\dfrac{1}{2} m(2 \pi R f)^{2}$
$=2 m \pi^{2} f^{2} R^{2}$

question: A charged particle is moving in a uniform magnetic field in a circular path of radius $R$. when the energy of the particle becomes three times the original, the new radius will be
explanation:
When a charged particle moves in a uniform magnetic field, the radius of circular path,
$R = \frac{{mv}}{{Bq}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
As, momentum $p = mv\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
and kinetic energy
$K=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m}$
$ \Rightarrow p = \sqrt {2mK} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
From eq.(1), (2) and (3), we get
$R=\dfrac{p}{B q}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{B q} \operatorname{or} R \propto \sqrt{K}$
$\therefore \dfrac{R^{\prime}}{R}=\dfrac{\sqrt{K^{\prime}}}{\sqrt{K}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3 K}}{\sqrt{K}}=\sqrt{3}\left(\because K^{\prime}=3 K\right)$
or $R^{\prime}=\sqrt{3} R$

question: A proton and helium nucleus are shot into a magnetic field at right angles to the field with same kinetic energy. Then the ratio of their radii is
explanation:

Radius of the circular path of a charged particle in a uniform magnetic field is
$r=\dfrac{m v}{q B}=\dfrac{m}{q B} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 K}{m}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2 K m}}{q B}}$
Since $K$ and $B$ are same for both the particles,

( $\therefore \alpha$ is a helium nucleus)
$ = \sqrt {\frac{m}{{4\,m}}} \times \frac{{2e}}{e} = 1$
$r_{p}: r_{\alpha}=1: 1$

question: Two particles $X$ and $Y$ having equal charges after being accelerated through same potential difference enter a region of uniform magnetic field and describe a circular paths of radii $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ respectively. The ratio of the mass of $x$ to that of $y$ is
explanation:
According to the question, force acting on the particle inside magnetic field is given by
$F_{B}=q v B \sin \theta$
[ where $\theta=$ angle between $v$ and $B$]
This force $F_{B}$ [provides necessary centripetal force for circular motion of the charged particle
So, $\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}=q v B \sin \theta$
Now, for particles $x$ and $y$ and for $\theta=90^{\circ}$
$\frac{{{m_x}v_x^2}}{r} = q{v_x}B\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$\,\frac{{{m_y}v_y^2}}{{{r_2}}} = q{v_y}B\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From eq.(1) and (2), we get
$\dfrac{m_{x} v_{x}}{m_{y} v_{y}}=\dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}$
As, $\dfrac{v_{x}}{v_{y}}=1$
So, $\dfrac{m_{x}}{m_{y}}=\dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}$

question: A charge $q$ is accelerated through a potential difference $V$. It is then passed normally through a uniform magnetic field, where it moves in a circle of radius $r$. The potential difference required to move it in a circle of radius 2$r$ is
explanation:
If $v$ is the velocity acquired by the charge when accelerated through the potential difference $V$, then
$\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = qV{\rm{ }}or{\rm{ }}v = \sqrt {\frac{{2qV}}{m}} $
When it passes normally through a uniform magnetic field $B$, it moves in the circle of radius r.
$\therefore \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} = qvB$
or $r = \frac{{mv}}{{qB}} = \frac{m}{{qB}}\sqrt {\frac{{2qV}}{m}} = \frac{1}{B}\sqrt {\frac{{2mV}}{q}} $
or $V = \frac{{{B^2}q{r^2}}}{{2\,m}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Let $V^{\prime}$ be the required potential difference in order to move it in the circle of 2$r$.
From eqn (1),
$V^{\prime}=\dfrac{\mathrm{b}^{2} q(2 r)^{2}}{2 m}=4\left(\dfrac{b^{2} q r^{2}}{2 m}\right)=4 V$

question: A beam of cathode rays accelerated through a potential difference of $V$ is subjected to cross electric ($E$) and magnetic fields ($B$). The fields are adjusted such that the beam is not deflected. The specific charge of the cathode rays is given by
explanation:

then $\quad F_{m}=F_{e}$
or $\quad B e v=E e$
or $v = \frac{E}{B}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
As the electron moves from cathode to anode, its potential energy at the cathode appears as its kinetic energy at the anode. If $V$ is the potential difference between the anode and cathode, then potential energy of the electron at cathode $=e V$. Also, kinetic energy of the electron at anode $=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$. According to law of conservation of energy,
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=e V$
or $v = \sqrt {\frac{{2eV}}{m}} \,\,\,\,(2)$
From Eqs.(1) and (2), we have
$\sqrt{\dfrac{2 e V}{m}}=\dfrac{E}{B} \quad \text { or } \quad \dfrac{e}{m}=\dfrac{E^{2}}{2 V B^{2}}$

question: A particle having charge $q$ enters a region of uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$ (directed inwards) and is deflected a distance $x$ after travelling a distance $y$. The magnitude of the momentum of the particle is:
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957088/preptracker/questions/q_41386_explanation_1764957088232.png $\begin{aligned}& R \sin \theta=y \\& R(1-\cos \theta)=x \\& R \cos \theta=R-x\end{aligned}$
Squaring and adding (1) and (2)
$\begin{aligned}& R^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta+R^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta=y^{2}+(R-x)^{2} \\& R^{2}=y^{2}+R^{2}+x^{2}-2 R x \\& 2 R x=x^{2}+y^{2} \Rightarrow R=\dfrac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{2 x} \\& \dfrac{m v}{B q}=R=\dfrac{x}{2}+\dfrac{y^{2}}{2 x} \\& (m v)=B q\left(\dfrac{x}{2}+\dfrac{y^{2}}{2 x}\right) \\& P=\dfrac{B q}{2}\left(x+\dfrac{y^{2}}{x}\right)\end{aligned}$

Magnetic force ${F_m} = qvB$ and directed radially inward.
$N - mg\sin \theta + qvB = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{R}$
$ \Rightarrow N = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{R} + mg\sin \theta - qvB$
At $\theta = \frac{\pi }{2}$
${N_{\max }} = \frac{{2mgR}}{R} + mg - qB\sqrt {2gR} $
$ = 3mg - qB\sqrt {2gR} $

question: A charged particle of specific charge (charge/ mass) $\alpha$ is released from origin at time $t = 0$ with velocity $\overrightarrow v  = {v_0}(\hat i + \hat j)$ in uniform magnetic field $\overrightarrow B  = {B_0}\hat i$. Coordinates of the particle at time $t=\dfrac{\pi}{B_{0} \alpha}$ are:
explanation:
$\alpha=\dfrac{q}{m}$, path of the particle will be a helix of time period, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956216/preptracker/questions/q_41378_explanation_1764956216137.png $T=\dfrac{2 \pi m}{B_{0} q}=\dfrac{2 \pi}{B_{0} \alpha}$
The given time $t=\dfrac{\pi}{B_{0} \alpha}=\dfrac{T}{2}$
$\therefore$ Coordinate of particle at time $t=\dfrac{T}{2}$
Would be $\left(v_{x} \dfrac{T}{2}, 0,-2 r\right)$
Here, $r=\dfrac{m v_{r}}{B_{0} q}=\dfrac{v_{0}}{B_{0} \alpha}$
$\therefore$ The coordinate are $\left(\dfrac{v_{0} \pi}{B_{0} \alpha}, 0, \dfrac{-2 v_{0}}{B_{0} \alpha}\right)$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Motion of a Charged Particle in Transverse Magnetic Field

question:
A particle of charge per unit mass $\alpha$ is released from origin with velocity $\overrightarrow v = {v_0}\hat i$ in a magnetic field
$\overrightarrow B = - {B_0}\hat k$ for $x \leq \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \dfrac{v_{0}}{B_{0} \alpha}$
and $\overrightarrow B = 0$ for $x>\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \dfrac{v_{0}}{B_{0} \alpha}$
The $x$-coordinate of the particle at time $t=\left(>\dfrac{\pi}{3 B_{0} \alpha}\right)$
explanation:
Radius of circular path of charged particle in uniform magnetic field
$\begin{aligned}& r=\dfrac{m v_{r}}{B_{0} q}=\dfrac{v_{0}}{B_{0} \alpha} \\& \dfrac{x}{r}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\sin \theta \\& \therefore \quad \theta=60^{\circ} \\& t_{O A}=\dfrac{T}{6}=\dfrac{\pi}{3 B_{0} \alpha}\end{aligned}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950213/preptracker/questions/q_41374_explanation_1764950212708.png $\begin{aligned}& t>\dfrac{\pi}{3 B_{0} \alpha} \text { will be } \\& x=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \dfrac{v_{0}}{B_{0} \alpha}+v_{0}\left(t-\dfrac{\pi}{3 B_{0} \alpha}\right) \cos 60^{\circ}\end{aligned}$

$=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \dfrac{v_{0}}{B_{0} \alpha}+\dfrac{v_{0}}{2}\left(t-\dfrac{\pi}{3 B_{0} \alpha}\right)$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Motion of a Charged Particle in Limited Portion of Magnetic Field

question: For a positively charged particle moving in a $x-$ $y$ plane initially along the $\mathrm{x}$-axis, there is a sudden change in its path due to the presence of electric field and/or magnetic fields beyond $P$. The curved path is shown in the $x-y$ plane and is found to be non-circular. Which one of the following combinations is possible?
explanation:
Electric field can deviate the path of the particle in the shown direction only when it is along negative $y$-direction. In the given options ${\vec E}$ is either zero or along $x$-direction. Hence it is the magnetic field which is really responsible for its curved path. Options (1) and (3) can't be accepted. When the velocity vector makes an angle other than $0^{\circ}, 180^{\circ}$ and $90^{\circ}$ with the magnetic field the path is a helix. Option (2) is wrong because in that case component of net force on the particle also comes in $k$ direction which is not acceptable as the particle is moving in $x-y$ plane.
In option (4) the particle can move in $x-y$ plane.

options.text: $\overrightarrow E  = 0;\overrightarrow B  = b\widehat i + c\widehat k$
$\overrightarrow E  = a\widehat i;\overrightarrow B  = c\widehat k + b\widehat j$
$\overrightarrow E  = 0;\overrightarrow B  = c\widehat j + b\widehat k$
$\overrightarrow E  = a\widehat i;\overrightarrow B  = c\widehat k + a\widehat i$

subject: Physics

subtopic: Motion of a Charged Particle in both Electric & Magnetic Fields
parent_topic: Motion of a Charged Particle in a Field

question: A particle with charge $+q$ and mass $m$, moving under the influence of a uniform electric field $E \hat{i}$ and a uniform magnetic field $B \hat{k}$, follows a trajectory from $\mathrm{P}$ to $\mathrm{Q}$ as shown in figure. The velocities at $P$ and $Q$ are $v \hat{i}$ and $-2 v \hat{j}$ respectively then the in correct statement among the the following is/are
explanation:
In going from $P$ to $Q$, increase in kinetic energy
$\dfrac{1}{2} m(2 v)^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m\left(3 v^{2}\right)=$ work done by electric field.
or $\quad \dfrac{3 m v^{2}}{2}=E q \times 2 a$
or $\quad E=\dfrac{3}{4}\left(\dfrac{m v^{2}}{q a}\right)$
The rate of work done by $E$ at
$P=$ force due to $\mathrm{E} \times$ velocity.
$=(q E) v=q v\left[\dfrac{3}{4}\left(\dfrac{m v^{2}}{q a}\right)\right]=\dfrac{3}{4}\left(\dfrac{m v^{3}}{a}\right)$
At $q$, $\bar{E}$ is perpendicular to $\bar{v}$ and, and no work is done by either field

options.text: The rate of work done by the electric field at $P$ is $\dfrac{3}{4}\left(\dfrac{m v^{3}}{a}\right)$
The rate of work done by the electric field at $P$ is 0
The rate of work done by both the fields at $Q$ is 0

question: An electron, a proton and an alpha particle having the same kinetic energy are moving in circular orbits of radii $r_{e}, r_{p}, r_{\alpha}$ respectively in a uniform magnetic field $B$. The relation between $r_{e}, r_{p}, r_{\alpha}$ is
explanation:
Radius of circular path in magnetic field
is given by $r=\dfrac{\sqrt{2 K m}}{q B}$
where $K=$ Kinetic energy of particle
$m=$ mass of particle $q=$ charge on particle $B=$ magnetic field intensity $r=$ radius of path
For electron
$r_{e}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2 k m_{e}}}{e B}$
For proton
$r_{p}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2 k m_{p}}}{e B}$
For $\alpha$ particle
$=\dfrac{\sqrt{2 k 4 m_{p}}}{2 e B}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2 k m_{p}}}{e B}$
as $m_{e} < m_{p}$ so $r_{e} < r_{p}=r_{\alpha}$

question: Electrons emitted with negligible speed from an electron gun are accelerated through a potential difference $V_{0}$ along the $x$-axis. These electrons emerge from a narrow hole into a uniform magnetic field of strength $B$ directed along $x$ axis. Some electrons emerging at slightly divergent angles as shown in the figure. These paraxial electrons are refocused on the $x$-axis at a distance equal to
explanation:
The electron will have velocity components both along and perpendicular to $\bar{B}$. They follow helical path. Let $\theta$ is the small divergent angle $v \cos \theta$ is along the field and $v \sin \theta$ is perpendicular to the field.
$\begin{aligned}& T=\dfrac{2 \pi m}{q B} \\& \text { Pitch }=v \cos \theta T \\& =v T(\cos \approx 1) \\& K E=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=e v \text { or } m v=\sqrt{2 e m V}\end{aligned}$
The electron will be refocussed after travelling a distance $=$ pitch of helix pitch
$=\dfrac{2 \pi m v}{q B}=\sqrt{\dfrac{4 \pi^{2} \times 2 e m V}{e^{2} B^{2}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{8 \pi^{2} m V}{e B^{2}}}$

question: A particle of charge $q$ and mass $m$ is projected with a velocity $v_{0}$ towards a circular region having uniform magnetic field $B$ perpendicular and into the plane of paper from point $P$ as shown in figure. $R$ is the radius and $O$ is the centre of the circular region. If the line $OP$ makes an angle $\theta$ with the direction of $v_{0}$ then the value of $v_{0}$ so that particle passes through $O$ is:
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950199/preptracker/questions/q_41329_explanation_1764950199019.png Let $Q$ be the centre of circular path followed by charged particle and ' $r$ ' be the radius of circular path as shown in figure
From triangle $OPQ$
$\begin{aligned}& O P=R \\& O P=(r \sin \theta) 2 \text { from triangle $OQP $} \\& {\left[\because \dfrac{r}{\sin (90-\theta)}=\dfrac{R}{\sin 2 \theta}\right]} \\& 2 r \sin \theta=R \\& 2 \dfrac{m v_{o}}{B q} \sin \theta=R \\& v_{o}=\dfrac{B q R}{2 m \sin \theta}\end{aligned}$

question: A positive charge ' $q$ ' of mass ' $m$ ' is moving along the $+x$ axis. We wish to apply a uniform magnetic field $B$ for time $\Delta t$ so that the charge reverses its direction crossing the $y$-axis at a disatnce $d$. Then :-
explanation:
To reverse it has to finish half circle. So it takes half of time period.
$\begin{gathered}T=\dfrac{2 \pi m}{B q} \\\therefore \quad t=\dfrac{T}{2}=\dfrac{\pi m}{B q}\end{gathered}$
and radius of circular path $R=\dfrac{m v}{B q} \Rightarrow \dfrac{d}{2}=\dfrac{m v}{B q}$
$\dfrac{d}{2}=\dfrac{m v}{B q} \Rightarrow \dfrac{m}{B q}=\dfrac{d}{2 v}$
from (1) and (2)
$t=\pi\left(\dfrac{m}{B q}\right)=\dfrac{\pi d}{2 v}$

question: A proton (mass $m$) ) accelerated by a potential difference $V$ flies through a uniform transverse magnetic field $B$. The field occupies a region of space by width $'d'$ if ' $\alpha$ ' be the angle of deviation of proton from initial direction of motion (see figure), the value of $\sin \alpha$ will be :
explanation:
Energy of proton $=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=q V$
$v=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 q V}{m}}$
magnetic force $=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{R}$
$R=\dfrac{m v}{q B}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950191/preptracker/questions/q_41286_explanation_1764950191316.png $\sin \alpha=\dfrac{d}{R}=\dfrac{d q B}{m v}=\dfrac{d q B}{m} \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{2 q V}}$
$\sin \alpha=B d \sqrt{\dfrac{q}{2 m V}}$

question: A galvanometer of resistance $50 \Omega$ is connected to a battery of 3 $V$ along with a resistance of $2950 \Omega$ in series shows full-scale deflection of 30 divisions. The additional series resistance required to reduce the deflection to 20 divisions is
explanation:
Let current $pr$ division be $I$
For full scale deflection,
$30I = \frac{3}{{50 + 2950}} = \frac{3}{{3000}};I = \frac{1}{3} \times {10^{ - 4}}\,A$
When additional series resistance $R$ is connected,
$20I = \frac{3}{{50 + 2950 + R}}$
$ \Rightarrow 20 \times \frac{1}{3} \times {10^{ - 4}} = \frac{3}{{3000 + R}} \Rightarrow R = 1500\Omega $

question: A galvanometer of resistance $50 \Omega$ gives a full scale deflection for a current $5 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,A$. The resistance that should be connected in series with the galvanometer to read 3 $V$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957937/preptracker/questions/q_111313_explanation_1764957937463.png Here,
Resistance of galvanometer, $G = 50\,\Omega $
Current for full scale deflection,${I_g} = 5 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,A$
Let $R$ be resistance connected in series with the galvanometer to read 3 $V$ . Then
$V = {I_g}(G + R){\rm{ }}$
${\rm{or}}\,\,R = \frac{V}{{{I_g}}} - G$
$R = \frac{{3\,V}}{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}A}} - 50\Omega $
$ = 600\Omega - 50\Omega = 5950\Omega $

question: Match the components and effects related to the Moving Coil Galvanometer (MCG) in Column I with their appropriate descriptions in Column II. Choose the correct option from the codes given below:

question: A galvanometer coil has a resistance of $10 \Omega$ and the meter shows full scale deflection for a current of  $2{\text{ }}mA.$   What resistance needs to be connected to convert the meter into a voltmeter of range 0 to  $18{\text{ }}V$  ?
explanation:
Given,
Resistance of moving coil galvanometer,
$G=10 \Omega$
Current, $I=2 {~mA}=2 \times 10^{-3} {~A}$
Let $R$ resistance is required to convert it to voltmeter,
Voltage rating be $V_{1}$ to $V_{2}$ i. e. 0 to $18{\text{ }}V,$ respectively.
By using Ohm's law,
$V=I(R+G)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{V}{I}=R+G$
$\Rightarrow \quad R=\dfrac{V}{I}-G=\dfrac{18 \times 10^{3}}{2}-10$
$=9000-10=8990 \Omega$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Voltmeter

question: A moving coil galvanometer of resistance $100 \Omega$ is used as an ammeter using a resistance $0.1 \Omega$. The maximum deflection current in the galvanometer is $100 \mu {~A}$. Find the minimum current in the circuit, so that ammeter shows maximum deflection.
explanation:
We are given with following ammeter https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952764/preptracker/questions/q_111208_explanation_1764952763704.png
Now, $V_{A B}=I_{g} G=\left(I-I_{g}\right) S$
$\left(100 \times 10^{-6}\right) 100=\left(I-100 \times 10^{-6}\right) 0.1$
$I-100 \times 10^{-6}=10^{-1} \Rightarrow I=0.1+100 \times 10^{-6}$
$=0.1+0.0001=0.1001 {~A}=100.1 {~mA}$
So, circuit current is $100.1{\text{ }}mA.$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Ammeter

question: A moving coil galvanometer has a rectangular wire coil of enclosed area $0.001 {~m}^{2}$ and 500  turns. The coil operates in a radial magnetic field of $0.2 T$ and carries a current of $6 \pi \times 10^{-8} A$. If the torsional spring constant is $6 \times 10^{-7} {~N}-{m} / {rad}$, then the angular deflection of the coil in radians is
explanation:
Angular deflection of a moving coil galvanometer is given by
$\theta=\left(\dfrac{N B A}{k}\right) i$
Here $N=500, B=0.2 T, A=0.001 m^{2}$
$i=6 \pi \times 10^{-8} A$ and $k=6 \times 10^{-7} N-m / {rad}$
$=\dfrac{500 \times 0.2 \times 0.001}{6 \times 10^{-7}} \times 6 \pi \times 10^{-8}$
$=\dfrac{5 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-1} \times 6 \pi \times 10^{-8}}{6 \times 10^{-7}}$
$\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{100}$
So, the correct option is (1)

subtopic: Moving coil Galvanometer

question: A moving coil galvanometer with coil resistance $G = 30\,\Omega $, shows full scale deflection when the current through it is $20\,mA$. The galvanometer is converted to an ammeter of range $3\,A$ by using a shunt resistance $S$, then resistance $S$ is
explanation:
$i_{g} G=\left(i-i_{g}\right) S$
$S=\dfrac{i_{g} G}{\left(i-i_{g}\right)}$
$=\dfrac{\left(20 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}\right)(30 \Omega)}{\left(3-20 \times 10^{-3}\right)} \Omega$
$=\dfrac{1}{5} \Omega=0.2 \Omega$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952746/preptracker/questions/q_111175_explanation_1764952746255.png

question: A galvanometer having coil resistance $10\,\Omega $ shows a full scale deflection for a current of  $3\,mA.$  For it to measure a current of $8 A$, the  value of the shunt should be
explanation:
As we know, for an ammeter
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950745/preptracker/questions/q_44002_explanation_1764950744952.png
$I_{g}=\left(\dfrac{S}{S+G}\right) I$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{I_g}}}{I} = \frac{S}{{S + G}} \Rightarrow \frac{{3 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{8} = \frac{S}{{S + 10}}$
$ \Rightarrow 8\,S = 3 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,S + 3 \times {10^{ - 2}}$
$ \Rightarrow \left( {8\,S - 3 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}\,S} \right) = 3 \times {10^2}$
$ \Rightarrow S = 3.75 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,\Omega $

question: The deflection in moving coil galvanometer falls from 25 divisions to 5 division when a shunt of resistance $24\,\Omega $ is applied. The resistance of galvanometer coil will be
explanation:
As we have,
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956515/preptracker/questions/q_43998_explanation_1764956515350.png
$S=\dfrac{I_{g} \cdot R_{g}}{\left(I-I_{g}\right)}$
$S$ is the shunt resistance.
$I_{g}$ is the current that passes through the galvanometer.
$I$ is the total current $R_{g}$ is the actual resistance of galvanometer
Here, $I=25 ; I_{g}=5$
$S = 24\,\Omega $
$ \Rightarrow 24\,\Omega = \frac{{5 \times {R_g}}}{{(25 - 5)}} \Rightarrow {R_g} = \frac{{24 \times 20}}{5} = 96\,\Omega $

question: A current of  $200\,\mu \,A,$  deflects the coil of a moving coil galvanometer through $60^{\circ}$. The current to cause deflection through $\dfrac{\pi}{10}$ radian is
explanation:
${I_1} = 200\,\mu A,\,\,{\theta _1} = 60^\circ ,{I_2} = ?\,,$
${I_1} = 200\,\mu A,\,\,{\theta _1} = 60^\circ ,$
$\theta_{2}=\dfrac{\pi}{10}$ radian
The current in moving coil galvanometer is directly proportional to the deflection, $I \propto \theta$
$\frac{{{I_2}}}{{{I_1}}} = \frac{{{\theta _2}}}{{{\theta _1}}} \Rightarrow \frac{{{I_2}}}{{200 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}$
$ = \frac{{\pi /10}}{{\pi /3}};{I_2} = 60\,\mu \,A$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Moving coil Galvanometer

question: In an ammeter, $5 \%$ of the main current passes through the galvanometer. If resistance of the galvanometer is $G$, the resistance of ammeter will be
explanation:
Ammeter consists of parallel combination of shunt resistance with galvanometer.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950620/preptracker/questions/q_43990_explanation_1764950620013.png
Here, shunt resistance and galvanometer both are in parallel, so voltage across them will be equal. $V_{S}=V_{G}$
$I_{S} . S=I_{g} G$
$\rightarrow S=\dfrac{I_{g}}{I_{S}} G$
Given, $\Rightarrow \dfrac{95}{100} I S=\dfrac{5}{100} I G$
$\therefore S=\dfrac{5}{95} G=\dfrac{1}{19} G$
Resistance of ammeter
$R_{A}=\dfrac{S \times G}{S+G}=\dfrac{\dfrac{G}{19} \times G}{\dfrac{G}{19}+G}=\dfrac{G}{20}$
$\therefore \quad R_{A}=\dfrac{G}{20}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Ammeter

question: A galvanometer has a resistance of $50\,\Omega $ and it allows maximum current of  $5\,mA.$  It can be converted into voltmeter to measure upto $100\,V$ by connecting it in series to a resistor of resistance
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956512/preptracker/questions/q_43986_explanation_1764956512344.png
Given, ${R_G} = 50\,\Omega ,I = 5\,mA,\,V = 100\,V$
Let the resistance is $R$.
$\left(R+R_{G}\right) I=V$
$(R+50) \times 5 \times 10^{-3}=100$
$R = 20000 - 50 = 19950\,\Omega $
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Voltmeter

question: A galvanometer has coil of resistane $200\,\Omega $ with a full scale deflection at  $20\,\mu \,A.$ The value of resistance to be added to use it as an ammeter of range $(0-20) m A$ is
explanation:
$G = 200{\mkern 1mu} \,\Omega ,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \,\,{i_g} = 20\,\mu A$
Galvanometer can be converted into ammeter by putting a shunt resistance $S$ in parallel.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950738/preptracker/questions/q_43982_explanation_1764950738130.png
$i_{g} G=\left(i-i_{g}\right) S$
$i.\,e.$ $i=i_{g}\left(\dfrac{G}{S}+1\right)$
$\therefore 20 \times 10^{-3}=20 \times 10^{-6}\left(\dfrac{200}{S}+1\right)$
$ \Rightarrow S = 0.20\,\Omega $
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Ammeter

question: A galvanometer of resistance $100\,\Omega $ when connected in series with $400\,\Omega $ measures avoltage of upto $10\,V.$  The value of resistance required to convert the galvanometer into ammeter to read upto $10\,A$  is   $x \times {10^{ - 2}}\,\Omega .$ The value of $x$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956511/preptracker/questions/q_43978_explanation_1764956510803.png
Let $I_{g}$ be the current through the galvanometer.
$\therefore \quad {I_g} = \frac{V}{{400 + G}} = \frac{{10}}{{400 + 100}} = \frac{1}{{50}}\,A$
Now, for converting galvanometer into an ammeter a shunt resistance $(S)$ is connected parallel to galvanometer. $I_{g} \times G=\left(I-I_{g}\right) \times S$
where $S$ is the shunt resistance.
Substituting given values, we have $I = 10\,A$
$\dfrac{1}{50} \times 100=\left(10-\dfrac{1}{50}\right) \times S$
$ \Rightarrow S = 20 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,\Omega $
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Ammeter

question: An ammeter reads upto $1\,A$. Its internal resistance is $0.81 \Omega$. To increase the range to $10\,A$, the value of the required shunt is
explanation:
Here, $G=0.81\, \Omega, I_{g}=1 \mathrm{~A}, I=10 \mathrm{~A}$
Shunt resistance is given by, $S=\dfrac{G I_{g}}{I-I_{g}}$.
$S=\dfrac{0.81 \times 1}{10-1}=\dfrac{0.81}{9}=0.09\, \Omega$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Ammeter

question: A galvanometer of resistance, $G$ is shunted by a resistance $S$ ohm. To keep the main current in the circuit unchanged, the resistance to be put in series with the galvanometer is
explanation:
Refer to figures $(a)$ and $(b).$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950589/preptracker/questions/q_43468_explanation_1764950589554.png To keep the main current $I$ in the circuit unchanged, a resistance $R$ is connected in series with a shunted galvanometer (figure (b)).
It will be so if
$G = \frac{{GS}}{{G + S}} + R$
or $R = G - \frac{{GS}}{{G + S}} = \frac{{{G^2} + GS - GS}}{{G + S}} = \frac{{{G^2}}}{{G + S}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Ammeter

question: A galvanometer has current range of $15\,mA$ and voltage range $750\,mV$. To convert this galvanometer into an ammeter of range $25\,A$, the required shunt is
explanation:
The current range and voltage range of a galvanometer imply the values of current and voltage which produce full scale deflection in the galvanometer.
Thus,
Current for full scale deflection,
$\,\,\,\,\,\,$${I_g} = 15\,mA = 15 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,A$
Voltage for full scale deflection,
$\,\,\,\,\,\,$${V_g} = 750\,mV = 750 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,V$
The resistance of the galvanometer is
$\,\,\,\,\,\,$$G = \frac{{{V_g}}}{{{I_g}}} = \frac{{750 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}\,V}}{{15 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}\,A}} = 50\,\Omega $
Let $S$ be required shunt to convert the given galvanometer into an ammeter of range $25\,A$.
$S = \frac{{{I_g}G}}{{I - {I_g}}} = \frac{{\left( {15 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}\,A} \right)(50\,\Omega )}}{{25\,A - \left( {15 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)A}} = 0.03\,\Omega $
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Ammeter

question: galvanometer having a resistance of $8 \Omega$ is shunted by a wire of resistance $2 \Omega$. If the total current is  $1\,\,A,$  the part of it passing through the shunt will be
explanation:
Let total current through the parallel combination be $I$, the current through the galvanometer be $I_{g}$ and the current through the shunt be $I-I_{g}$.
The shunted galvanometer is shown in the figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950569/preptracker/questions/q_43398_explanation_1764950569039.png The potential difference,
$V_{a b}=\left(V_{a}-V_{b}\right)$ is the same for both paths, so $I_{g} G=\left(I-I_{g}\right) S$
$\Rightarrow \quad I_{g}(G+S)=I S$
$\Rightarrow$$\dfrac{I_{g}}{I}=\dfrac{S}{S+G}$
The fraction of current passing through shunt
$ = \frac{{I - {I_g}}}{I} = 1 - \frac{{{I_g}}}{I}$
$ = 1 - \frac{S}{{S + G}} = \frac{G}{{S + G}}$
$ = \frac{8}{{2 + 8}} = \frac{8}{{10}} = 0.8\,\,A$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Ammeter

question: A voltmeter of range $2\,\,V$ and resistance $300 \Omega$ cannot be converted into ammeter of range
explanation:
As, ${I_g} = \frac{2}{{300}}\,A$
$ = \frac{2}{{300}} \times 1000\,\,mA = \frac{{20}}{3}\,\,mA$
$\therefore \quad {I_g} = 6.67\,mA$
As, range of ammeter cannot be decreased but can be increased only, therefore the instrument cannot be converted to measure the range $1\,\,mA.$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Voltmeter

question: There are three voltmeters of the same range but of resistance $10000 \Omega, 8000 \Omega$ and $4000 \Omega$, respectively. The best voltmeter among these is the one whose resistance is
explanation: Best voltmeter is one with resistance $10000 \Omega$. As voltmeter with high resistance draw minimum current (or less current) from circuit and gives reading close to the actual current.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Voltmeter

question: A galvanometer coil has a resistance of $10 \Omega$ and the ammeter shows full scale deflection for a current of  $1\,\,mA.$  The shunt resistance required to convert the galvanometer into an ammeter of range  $0 - 100\,\,mA$  is about
explanation:
Resistance of galvanometer coil, $R=10 \Omega$ and current $ = 1\,\,mA$
Range of current is $0 - 100\,\,mA.$
So, the shunt resistance is
$S = \frac{{{I_g}R}}{{I - {I_g}}}$
$ = \frac{{1 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 10}}{{100 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} - 1 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}$
$ = \frac{{10}}{{99}} = 0.1\,\Omega $
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Ammeter

question: A galvanometer acting as a voltmeter should have
explanation: Galvanometer act as a voltmeter when a very high resistance is connected in series with galvanometer.

options.text: low resistance in series with its coil
low resistance in parallel with its coil
high resistance in series with its coil
high resistance in parallel with its coil

subject: Physics

question: In a galvanometer, $5 \%$ of the total current in the circuit passes through it. If the resistance of the galvanometer is $G$, the shunt resistance $S$ connected to the galvanometer is
explanation:
Current in galvanometer, $i_{g}=\dfrac{5}{100} i$
$\therefore$ Shunt resistance,
$S=\dfrac{i_{g} G}{i-i_{g}}=\dfrac{5 i / 100 G}{i-\left(\dfrac{5 i}{100}\right)}=\dfrac{G}{19}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Ammeter

question: A galvanometer coil has a resistance of $15 \Omega$ and gives full scale deflection for a current of  $4\,\,mA.$  To convert it to an ammeter of range 0 to $6\,\,A,$
explanation:
To convert a galvanometer into an ammeter, a shunt resistance in parallel is connected to galvanometer. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956442/preptracker/questions/q_43374_explanation_1764956442011.png Since, galvanometer $G$ and shunt $S$ are in parallel, hence $i_{g} G=\left(i-i_{g}\right) S$.
$ \Rightarrow \quad S = \frac{{{i_g}G}}{{i - {i_g}}} = \frac{{4 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 15}}{{6 - \left( {4 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)}}$
$ = \frac{{60 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{5.99}} = 10\,\,m\,\Omega $
Hence, $10\,\,m\,\Omega $ resistance is to be connected in parallel to the galvanometer.

options.text: $10\,m\,\Omega $ resistance is to be connected in parallel to the galvanometer
$10\,m\,\Omega $  resistance is to be connected in series with the galvanometer
$5 \Omega$ resistance is to be connected in parallel

subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Ammeter

question: A galvanometer of $50\,\,\Omega $ resistance has 25 divisions. A current of  $4 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,A$  gives a deflection of one division. To convert this galvanometer into a voltmeter having a range of  $25\,\,V,$  it should be connected with a resistance of
explanation:
Given, resistance of galvanometer,
$R_{g}=50 \Omega$
Number of divisions$=25$
${I_g} = 4 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times 25 = {10^{ - 2}}\,A$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950567/preptracker/questions/q_43370_explanation_1764950566711.png $R$ is the resistance connected in series of galvanometer to convert into voltmeter
$\therefore V = {I_g}\left( {{R_g} + R} \right)\,25$
$ = {10^{ - 2}}(50 + R)$
$ \Rightarrow R = \frac{{25}}{{{{10}^{ - 2}}}} - 50 = 2500 - 50$
$ \Rightarrow R = 2450\,\Omega $ in series.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Voltmeter

question: The sensitivity of a Galvanometer of resistance ${171 \Omega}$ becomes 20 times when we used shunt of ${x \Omega}$. Find ${x}$.
explanation:
${S=\dfrac{i_{g}}{i-i_{g}} G=\left(\dfrac{1}{i / i_{g}-1}\right) G}$
${S=\left(\dfrac{1}{20-1}\right) 171=9 \Omega}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Moving coil Galvanometer

question: A galvanometer of resistance ${G}$ is connected in a circuit. Now, a resistance ${R}$ is connected in series of galvanometer. To keep the main current in the circuit unchanged, the resistance to be put in parallel with the series combination of ${G}$ and ${R}$ is
explanation:
${G^{\prime}=G}$
${\dfrac{(G+R) S}{G+R+S}=G}$
${S=\dfrac{G^{2}}{R}+G}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Moving coil Galvanometer

question: The number of turns of the coil of a moving coil galvanometer is increased in order to increase current sensitivity by $50 \%$. The percentage change in voltage sensitivity of the galvanometer will be
explanation:
Sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer
$=\dfrac{N A B}{k}$
Deflection in current is increased but current sensitivity is independent of voltage sensitivity. Hence, percentage change in voltage sensitivity of the galvanometer is zero.
So, correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Moving coil Galvanometer

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
If the number of turns in the coil of a moving coil galvanometer is doubled then the current sensitivity becomes double
Increasing current sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer by only increasing the number of turns in the coil will also increase its voltage sensitivity in the same ratio.
explanation:
Current sensitivity, $I_{S}=\dfrac{Q}{I}=\dfrac{N B A}{C}$
Voltage sensitivity, $V_{S}=\dfrac{Q}{V}=\dfrac{N B A}{C R}$
When $N$ is doubled, current sensitivity also doubled. So, statement A is true.
But as $N$ is doubled resistance $R$ will also change and hence voltage sensitivity cannot be changed as $\dfrac{N}{R}$ ratio is constant, so statement B is false.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Moving coil Galvanometer

question: The current sensitivity of moving coil galvanometer is increased by $25 \%$. This increase is achieved only by changing in the number of turns of coils and area of cross section of the wire while keeping the resistance of galvanometer coil constant. The percentage change in the voltage sensitivity will be
explanation:
For moving coil galvanometer, current and voltage sensitivity are given by
$I_{S}=\dfrac{N B A}{C}$ and $V_{S}=\dfrac{N B A}{C G} \Rightarrow V_{S}=\dfrac{I_{S}}{G}$
[Where $C$ is restoring torque per unit twist of suspension string] If $G$ (galvanometer resistance) is constant, then $V_{S} \propto I_{S}$ So, percentage change in $V_{S}$ is also $25 \%$.
So, correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Moving coil Galvanometer

question: Certain galvanometers have a fixed core made of non-magnetic metallic material. The function of this metallic material is
explanation:
The core of non-magnetic metallic material when oscillates, the eddy currents are set up in the opposite direction of motion of coil in the magnetic field and coil is brought to rest by this property.
So correct option is (4).

options.text: to make the magnetic field radial
to oscillate the coil in magnetic field for longer period of time
to produce large deflecting torque on the coil
to bring the coil to rest quickly.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Moving coil Galvanometer

question: For a moving coil galvanometer, the deflection in the coil is $0.05\,rad$ when a current of $10\,mA$ is passed through it. If the torsional constant of suspension wire is $4.0 \times {10^{ - 5}}Nm\,ra{d^{ - 1}}$, the magnetic field is $0.01\,T$ and the number of turns in the coils is 200 , the area of each turn (in $c{m^2}$) is
explanation:
$\theta = 0.05\,rad$, Current, $i = 10\,mA$;
Torsional constant, $k = 4 \times {10^{ - 5}}Nm/rad$
$B = 0.01{\rm{ }}T,{\rm{ }}N = 200$
Let area is $A$.
At equilibrium
$\tau=k \theta$
$N i A B=k \theta$
$A=\dfrac{k \theta}{N i B}=\dfrac{4 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.05}{200 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.01} m^{2}$
$A = 1\,c{m^2}$
So, correction is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Moving coil Galvanometer

question:
For designing a voltmeter of range $50\,V$ and an ammeter of range $10\,mA$ using a galvanometer which has a coil of resistance $54 \Omega$ showing a full scale deflection for $1\,mA$ as in figure. (A) For voltmeter $R \approx 50k\Omega $
(B) For ammeter $r \approx 0.2\Omega $
(C) For ammeter $r = 6\Omega $
(D) For voltmeter $R \approx 5k\Omega $
(E) For voltmeter $R \approx 500\Omega $
Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
explanation:
Given, $G = 54\Omega ,{I_g} = 1\,mA = 1 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,A$
$V = 50\,V$ and $I = 10\,mA = 10 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,A$
$R$ is the resistance connected in series with the galvanometer to get a voltmeter of range $50\,\,V.$ From the circuit diagram
$V=I_{g}(G+R)$ or
$50=1 \times 10^{-3}(54+R)$
$ \Rightarrow R = 49,946\Omega \simeq 50k\Omega $
Now, for converting galvanometer into an ammeter, a shunt resistance is connected $I_{g} \times G=\left(I-I_{g}\right) \times r$,
where $r$ is the shunt resistance.
$\therefore 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 54=\left(10^{-2}-10^{-3}\right) \times r$
$\Rightarrow r=\dfrac{54}{9}=6 \Omega$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Voltmeter

question: The resistance of an ammeter is $13\,\Omega $ and its scale is graduated for a current upto $100\,amps.$ After an additional shunt has been connected to this ammeter it becomes possible to measure currents upto 750 amperes by this meter. The value of shunt-resistance is
explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956382/preptracker/questions/q_43063_explanation_1764956381778.jpg  $\begin{aligned}& V_{A B}=V_{C D} \\\Rightarrow & I_{G} R_{G}=\left(I-I_{G}\right) S \\\Rightarrow & 100 \times 13=(750-100) S \\\Rightarrow & S=\dfrac{1300}{650}=2 \Omega\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Ammeter

question: A current of $200\,\mu A$ deflects the coil of a moving coil galvanometer through $60^\circ .$ The current to cause deflection through $\dfrac{\pi}{10}$ radian is :
explanation:
Deflection in galvanometer is proportional to current flowing in the loop
As $I \propto \theta$
$\therefore \dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\dfrac{\theta_{1}}{\theta_{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{200 \mu A}{I_{1}}=\dfrac{\pi / 3}{\pi / 10}=\dfrac{10}{3}$
$ \Rightarrow {I_2} = 60\,\mu A$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Moving coil Galvanometer

question: Three voltmeters, all having different internal resistances are joined as shown in figure. When some potential difference is applied across $A$ and $B$, their readings are ${V_1},{\rm{ }}{V_2}$ and ${V_3}$. Choose the correct options.
explanation:
Using ohm's law across points $A$ and $B$
$V=i R$
$V_{A B}=I_{1}\left(R_{1}+R_{2}\right)$
$V_{A B}=I_{2} R_{3}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957106/preptracker/questions/q_43046_explanation_1764957106132.png ${I_1}\left( {{R_1} + {R_2}} \right) = {I_2}{R_3}$
$ \Rightarrow {V_1} + {V_2} = {V_3}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Voltmeter

question:

Reason: Resistance of a voltmeter is very large.
explanation: Voltmeter has a very high resistance, and connecting it in parallel allows it to measure the voltage across the component or portion of the circuit without significantly affecting the current flow through the circuit. So correct option is (1).

Reason: A galvanometer can be used as an ammeter only.
explanation: A galvanometer can indeed be used both as an ammeter and as a voltmeter. It can be used as an ammeter by connecting a low resistance in parallel with it, and as a voltmeter by connecting a high resistance in series with it. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Assertion: The range of given voltmeter can be both increased and decreased.

Reason: By adjusting the value of resistance in series with galvanometer the range of voltmeter can be adjusted.
explanation:
A voltmeter's range is determined by a series resistor $(R)$ in conjunction with a galvanometer. Increasing $R$ extends the voltmeter range for higher voltages, Reducing $R$ decreases sensitivity for lower voltage measurements.Sensitivity is reciprocal of full scale value of $I_{f s d}$ .
A sensitivity of $1 \mathrm{k} \Omega / \mathrm{V}$ means $I_{\text {sd }}=1 \mathrm{~mA}$
So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Out of galvanometer, ammeter and voltmeter, resistance of ammeter is lowest and resistance of voltmeter is highest.

Reason: An ammeter is connected in series and a voltmeter is connected in parallel, in a circuit.
explanation: Ammeter shall show reading almost equal to actual current in circut. Ammeters have low resistance to minimize the impact on current measurement in series, while voltmeters have high resistance in parallel to minimize the current drawn during voltage measurement, accurately explaining their designs. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: The coil is wound over the metallic frame in moving coil galvanometer.

Reason: The metallic frame help in making steady deflection without any oscillation.
explanation: Due to the metallic frame, the deflection is only due to current in a coil and magnetic field not due to vibration of springs. The metallic frame prevents oscillations, by damping. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question: A galvanometer of resistance $3663 \Omega$ gives full scale deflection for a certain $I_{\mathrm{g}}$. Calculate the value of the resistance of the shunt which, when joined to the galvanometer coil will result in 1/ 34 of the total current passing through the galvanometer. Also the total resistance of the galvanometer and shunt will be
explanation:
' $I$ ' is the total current then
$I_{g}=I / 34$
Now,
$S=\dfrac{I_{g} G}{I-I_{g}}=\dfrac{I_{g} \times 3663}{34 I_{g}-I_{g}}=\dfrac{3663 I_{g}}{33 I_{g}}=111 \Omega$
Combined resistance of shunt and galvanometer.
$R_{P}=\dfrac{G S}{G+S}=\dfrac{3663 \times 111}{3663+111}=\dfrac{406593}{3774}=107.7 \Omega$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Moving coil Galvanometer

question: The coil of galvanometer consists of 100 turns and effective area of 1 square $cm$. The restoring couple is ${10^{ - 8}}\,N - m{\rm{/}}rad$. The magnetic field between the pole pieces is $5\,T$. The current sensitivity of this galvanometer will be
explanation:
Current sensitivity $\dfrac{\theta}{I}=\dfrac{N B A}{C}$
$ = \frac{{100 \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 8}}}} = 5\,rad{\rm{/}}\mu \,amp$
subject: Physics

question: A galvanometer has a resistance of $100\,\Omega $. A potential difference of $100\,mV$ between its terminals gives a full scale deflection. The shunt resistance required to convert it into an ammeter reading upto $5\,A$ is
explanation:
The current required for full scale deflection is
$i_{g}=\dfrac{\text { PD across galvanometer }}{\text { Resistance of galvanometer }}$
$ \Rightarrow {i_g} = \frac{{100 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{100}} = {10^{ - 3}}\,A$
The shunt resistance required is
$S=\left(\dfrac{i_{g}}{i-i_{g}}\right) G=\left(\dfrac{10^{-3}}{5-10^{-3}}\right) \times 100$
$=0.02 \Omega$
So, correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Ammeter

question: A moving coil galvanometer is converted into an ammeter of range 0 to $5\,mA$. The galvanometer resistance is $90 \Omega$ and the shunt resistance has a value of $10 \Omega$. If there are 50 divisions in the galvanometer turned ammeter on either sides of zero, its current sensitivity is
explanation:
$G$ and $S$ are parallel $\Rightarrow i_{g} G=\left(i-i_{g}\right) S$.https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956348/preptracker/questions/q_42727_explanation_1764956347701.png The shunt resistance used is:
$S = \frac{G}{{\left[ {\left( {\frac{i}{{{i_g}}}} \right) - 1} \right]}}$
$ \Rightarrow 10 = \frac{{90}}{{\left[ {\left( {\frac{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{{i_g}}}} \right) - 1} \right]}}$
$ \Rightarrow 5 \times {10^{ - 3}} = 10{\mkern 1mu} {i_g}$
$ \Rightarrow {i_g} = 0.5 \times {10^{ - 3}} = 50 \times {10^{ - 5}}A$
Sensitivity$ = \frac{\theta }{{{i_g}}} = \frac{{50}}{{50 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}} = {10^{ - 5}}\frac{{div}}{A}$
Correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Ammeter

question: The deflection in a moving coil galvanometer falls from 50 divisions to 10 divisions when a shunt of 12 $ohm$ is applied. What is the resistance of the galvanometer?
explanation:
Let $i_{1}$ and $i_{2}$ be the currents through galvanometer intially and finally. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956336/preptracker/questions/q_42443_explanation_1764956336296.png From fig-(b)
${i_2}G = {i_1} - {i_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
As$i \propto \theta $ (Deflection in galvanometer)
$\frac{{{i_1}}}{{{i_2}}} = \frac{{50\,\,divisions}}{{10\,\,divisions}}$
${i_1} = 5{i_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From Eq's (1) & (2)
$G = 48\Omega $

question: What is the value of shunt which passes $10 \%$ of the main current through a galvanometer of $99 \Omega$ ?
explanation:
The shunt resistance is given by
$S=\dfrac{I_{G} G}{\left(I-I_{G}\right)}$
$G = 99\Omega \,$ and ${I_G} = (10/100)I = 0.1I$
$S = \frac{{0.1I \times 99}}{{(I - 0.1I)}} = \frac{{0.1}}{{0.9}} \times 99 = 11\Omega $

question: The shunt resistance required to allow $4 \%$ of the main current through the galvanometer of resistance $48 \Omega$ is
explanation:
Here, resistance of galvanometer, $G=48 \Omega$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956303/preptracker/questions/q_42117_explanation_1764956303097.png $I_{g}=\dfrac{4}{100} I=0.04 I$
Let $S$ be required shunt.
$S=\dfrac{I_{g} G}{\left(I-I_{g}\right)}=\dfrac{0.04 I \times 48}{(I-0.04 I)}=\dfrac{0.04 \times 48}{0.96}=2 \Omega$

question: A voltmeter has a resistance of $g$ ohm and range of $V$ volt. The value of resistance used in series to convert it into voltmeter of range $n$ volt is
explanation:
The initial range of voltmeter is
$V = {i_0}\,g\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Where ${i_0}$ is the maximum current that passes through voltmeter
$n = {i_0}(\,s + g)\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) & (2)
$S = g(n - 1)$

question: A voltmeter having resistance of $50 \times {10^3}ohm$ is used to measure the voltage in a circuit. To increase the range of measurement 3 times the additional series resistance required is
explanation:
Given that ${R_v} = 50 \times {10^3}ohm$
The range of a voltmeter is
$\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{v}}$
As the range is to be made three times
$3 \mathrm{~V}=\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{g}}\left(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{v}}+\mathrm{S}\right)$
From both the equations we get
$\mathrm{S}=10^{5} \Omega$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Voltmeter

question: A moving coil galvanometer has 100 turns and each turns has an area of $2\,c{m^2}$. The magnetic field produced by the magnet is $0.01\,T$. The deflection in the galvanometer coil is $0.05\,rad$ when a current of 10$mA$ is passed through it. The torsional constant of the spiral spring is
explanation:
Torque experienced by current carrying loop due to magnetic field is $=B i A N$
$C \theta=B i A n$
We have,$i=\dfrac{k}{N A B} \theta$
$\Rightarrow k=\dfrac{N A B i}{\theta}$
or
$k=\dfrac{100 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.01 \times 10 \times 10^{-3}}{0.05}$
$\therefore \quad k = 4.0 \times {10^{ - 5}}Nmra{d^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Moving coil Galvanometer

question: The area of the coil in a moving coil galvanometer is $15\,c{m^2}$ and has 20 turns. The magnetic induction is $0.2 T$ and the couple per unit twist of the suspended wire is ${10^{ - 6}}Nm$ per degree. If the deflection is $45^{\circ}$, the current passing through it is
explanation:
Torque experienced by current carrying loop due to magnetic field is $=B i A N$
$\begin{aligned}& C \theta=B A I n \\& I=\dfrac{C \theta}{B A n} \\& =75 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Moving coil Galvanometer

question: To increase the current sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer by $50 \%$, its resistance becomes twice its initial resistance. By what factor does the voltage sensitivity change?
explanation:
Let $C S$ and $V S$ be the original current sensitivity and voltage sensitivity of $MCG$
Changed current sensitivity
$C S^{1}=C S+\dfrac{50}{100} C S=\dfrac{3}{2} C S$
Since $\quad V S=\dfrac{C S}{R}$
changed voltage sensitivity, i.e.,
$\begin{aligned}& V S^{1}=\dfrac{C S^{1}}{2 R}=\dfrac{(3 / 2) C S}{2 R} \\& =\dfrac{3}{4}\left(\dfrac{C S}{R}\right)=0.75 V S\end{aligned}$
Thus, voltage sensitivity decreases by $25 \%$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Moving coil Galvanometer

question:

Galvanometer gives a full-scale deflection for a current of the order of micro ampere.

Also for measuring current the galvanometer has to be connected in series, and as it has a large resistance, this will change the value of the current in the circuit. To overcome these difficulties one attaches a small resistance called shunt resistance in parallel with galvometer coil. So option (3) is correct.

question: Current sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer is $5div/mA$ and its voltage sensitivity (angular deflection per unit voltage applied) is $20div/V$. The resistance of the galvanometer is
explanation:
5 div. $=1 m A \Rightarrow 1 d i v .=\dfrac{1}{5} m A$
Similarly 1 div. $=\dfrac{1}{20}$ volts
$R=\dfrac{V}{i}=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{20}}{\dfrac{1}{5} \times 10^{-3}}=\dfrac{1}{4} \times 10^{-3}=250 \Omega$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Moving coil Galvanometer

question: A millivoltmeter of 25$mV$ range is to be converted into an ammeter of 25$A$ range. The value (in ohm) of necessary shunt will be
explanation:
The full scale deflection current
$i_{g}=\dfrac{25 m V}{G} A$
where, $G$ is the resistance of the meter.
https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2023_07_07_68f63c56f9c3da24b09cg-08.jpg?height=182&width=289&top_left_y=914&top_left_x=515
As $V$ and $S$ are parallel $i_{g} G=\left(i-i_{g}\right) S$
The value of shunt required for converting it into ammeter of range $25 \mathrm{~A}$ is $S=\dfrac{i_{g} G}{i-i_{g}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad S=i_{g} \dfrac{G}{i}\left(\right.$ As $\left.i>>i_{g}\right)$
So that,$S \approx \frac{{25mV}}{G} \cdot \frac{G}{i} = \frac{{25mV}}{{25}} = 0.001\Omega $
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Ammeter

question: A galvanometer has a coil of resistance $100 \Omega$ and gives a full scale deflection for 30$mA$ current. If it is to work as a voltmeter of 30$V$ range, the resistance required to be added will be
explanation:
Required resistance to convert a galvanometer into voltmeter of $30 \mathrm{~V}$ is given by
$ \to {\rm{ }}G - MM$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956230/preptracker/questions/q_41599_explanation_1764956230271.png ${\rm{ }}V = {i_g}(G + R)$
$R = \frac{V}{{{i_g}}} - G = \frac{{30}}{{30 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} - 100 = 900\Omega $
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Voltmeter

question: The resistance of an ammeter is $13 \Omega$ and its scale is graduated for a current upto 100$A$. After an additional shunt has been connected to this ammeter it becomes possible to measure current upto 750$A$ by this meter. The value of shunt resistance is
explanation:
Let $i_{A}$ be the current flowing through ammeter and $i$ be the total current. So, $i-i_{A}$ will flow through shunt resistance.Potential difference across ammeter and shunt resistance is same.
i.e., $\quad i_{A} \times R=\left(i-i_{A}\right) \times S$
$ \Rightarrow \quad S = \frac{{{i_A}R}}{{i - {i_A}}}\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Given, $i_{A}=100 A, i=750 A, \& R=13 \Omega$
Hence, $\quad S=\dfrac{100 \times 13}{750-100}=2 \Omega$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Ammeter

question: In an ammeter $0.2 \%$ of main current passes through the galvanometer. If resistance of galvanometer is $G$, the resistance of ammeter will be
explanation:
For ammeter $0.002 I \times G=0.998 I \times r_{s}$
$r_{s}=\dfrac{0.002}{0.998} G \Rightarrow r_{s}=\dfrac{1}{499} \times G$ Equivalent resistance of ammeter,
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{R}=\dfrac{1}{G}+\dfrac{1}{r_{s}} \\& \therefore \quad \dfrac{1}{R}=\dfrac{1}{G}+\dfrac{1}{G / 499} \Rightarrow R=\dfrac{G}{500}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conversion of Galvanometer into Ammeter

question: In an experiment to determine the figure of merit of a galvanometer by half deflection method, a student constructed the following circuit. He plugged a resistance of $5200 \Omega$ in $R$. When $K_{1}$ is closed and $K_{2}$ is open, the deflection observed in the galvanometer is 26 div. When $K_{2}$ is also closed and a resistance of $90 \Omega$ is placed in $S$, the deflection becomes 13 div. The resistance of galvanometer is nearly
explanation:
When $K_{1}$ is closed and $K_{2}$ is open
${I_1} = 26K = \frac{V}{{5200 + G}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
When $K_{2}$ is also closed the equivalent resistance of $G$ and $S$ is given by $\dfrac{G \times S}{G+S}$
$\therefore$ Main current, $I=\dfrac{V}{\left[5200+\dfrac{G(90)}{G+90}\right]}$
$G$ current, $I_{2}=13 K=\dfrac{I \times 90}{90+G}$
$ \Rightarrow 13K = \frac{{90 \times V}}{{5200(90 + G) + G90}}{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From (1) and (2) $\Rightarrow \dfrac{G[5290]+5200 \times 90}{90[5200+G]}=2$
Solving, $G=91.58 \Omega \approx 91.6 \Omega$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Oersted’s Discovery
