question: A circular coil carrying a current of $2.5\,A$ is free to rotate about an axis in its plane perpendicular to an external magnetic field. When the coil is made to oscillate, the time period of oscillation is $T$. If the current through the coil is $10\,A$, the time period of oscillation is
explanation:
The time period of oscillation of a current carrying coil in external magnetic field is
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{M B}} \Rightarrow T \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{M}}$
But $=N I A \Rightarrow M \alpha T$
$\therefore T \alpha \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{I}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{M_{1}}{M_{2}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2.5}{10}}=\dfrac{1}{2}$
$\therefore T_{2}=\dfrac{T_{1}}{2}=\dfrac{T}{2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Torque on a Current Carrying Loop in Uniform Magnetic Field

question: The torque acting on a magnetic dipole placed in uniform magnetic field is zero, when the angle between the dipole axis and the magnetic field is
explanation:
$\tau=M B \sin \theta$
Given $\tau=0 \Rightarrow \sin \theta=0 \Rightarrow \theta=0^{\circ}$.
Correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Torque on a Current Carrying Loop in Uniform Magnetic Field

question: A rectangular coil of 400 turns and ${10^{ - 2}}\,{m^2}$ area, carrying a current of $0.5\,A$ is placed in a uniform magnetic field of $1\,T$ such that the  plane of the coil makes an angle of $60^\circ $ with the direction of the magnetic field. The initial moment of force acting on the coil in $Nm$ is
explanation:
Magnetic moment
$\vec{\tau}_{B}=\vec{M} \times \vec{B}$
$\Rightarrow \tau_{B}=M B \sin \alpha=N i A B \sin \alpha$
Here, $\alpha=90^{\circ}-60^{\circ}=30^{\circ}$
$=400 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 1 \times \sin 30^{\circ}$
$ = 400 \times 0.5 \times {10^{ - 2}} \times 1 \times \frac{1}{2} = 1Nm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Torque on a Current Carrying Loop in Uniform Magnetic Field

question: Match the terms related to current loops and magnetic dipoles in Column I with their corresponding descriptions in Column II. Choose the correct option from the codes given below:
explanation:
A: A Rectangular Current Loop, when placed in a magnetic field, behaves like a magnetic dipole, which is an elementary magnetic element producing a magnetic field at large distances (Q).
B: The Magnetic Moment ($M$) of a current loop is defined $M=N I A(\mathrm{R})$.
C : The Torque ($\tau$) on a current loop placed in a uniform magnetic field is given by the cross product of the magnetic moment ($M$) and the magnetic field ($B$), which simplifies to $\tau=M \times B \times \sin \theta$ when the magnetic field makes an angle $\theta$ with the normal to the loop (S).
D: The Magnetic Field ($B$) produces on a current carrying loop (P). Correct option is (4)

options.text: A - S,        	B - Q,	C - P,	D - R
A - Q, 	B - S,	C - R, 	D - P

question: A circular coil of area $2 {~cm}^{2}$ has 1000 turns. If the current through the coil is $1 A$, then its magnetic moment is
explanation:
Given, Number of turns, $N=1000$
Area of coil, $A=2 {~cm}^{2}=2 \times 10^{-4} {~m}^{2}$
Current, $i=1 {~A}$
Magnetic moment is given by the formula
$M=N i A$
$=1000 \times 1 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}=0.2 {Am}^{2}$
So, the correct option is (2)
Hence, magnetic moment is $0.2 {~A}-{m}^{2}$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Dipole Moment of a Circular Current Carrying Loop

question: A current carrying loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field $B$ in different orientations I, II, III and IV as shown in the figure. The correct order of decreasing potential energy is ( $\hat{n}=$ unit vector normal to the plane of the loop)
explanation:
Given
Current is circulating in loop $=i$
Magnetic field $=B$
Potential energy of a current carrying loop when it placed in a magnetic field is given by
$U=-\mu B \cos \theta[\mu$ is magnetic moment,
$\mu= \pm i A]$
$ = - IAB\cos \theta \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
For loop (1), $\theta=180^{\circ}$
${U_1} = - iAB( - 1) = + iAB\quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
For loop (2) $\theta=90^{\circ}$
${U_2} = 0\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (3)$
For loop (3), $\theta < 90^{\circ}$
${U_3} = - iAB\cos \theta \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (4)$
For loop (4), $\theta=(180>\theta>90)=+{ve}$ in
second quadrant
${U_4} = + iAB\cos \theta \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (5)$
Hence correct order is
$U_{1}>U_{4}>U_{2}>U_{3}$
Option (3) is the correct option.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy of Magnetic Dipole

question: A current $i$ is flowing through a wire of length ' $L$ '. If it is made into a circular loop of one turn, then its magnetic moment is
explanation:
Given,
Current in wire $=i$
Wire length $=L$
When the wire of length $L$ is bent in circular
shape of radius $r, \quad r=\dfrac{L}{2 \pi}$
Area of circular loop $=\pi r^{2}=\pi\left(\dfrac{L}{2 \pi}\right)^{2}$
Magnetic moment is given by
$M=i A=i \times \pi\left(\dfrac{L}{2 \pi}\right)^{2}$
$M=\dfrac{L^{2} i}{4 \pi}$
Hence, option (1) is correct option.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Dipole Moment of a Circular Current Carrying Loop

question: A thin magnetic needle is placed in a magnetic field of $200 G$ with its axis at $30^{\circ}$ to the direction of the field. Find the magnetic moment of the needle, if it experiences a torque of  $0.012{\text{ }}Nm$  in this field.
explanation:
Given, torque,
$\tau=0.012 N-m=1.2 \times 10^{-2} N-m$
Magnetic field,
$B=200 G=2 \times 10^{-2} T\left[\because 1 G=10^{-4} T\right]$
$\theta=30^{\circ}$
We know that,
$\tau=M B \sin \theta$
$\Rightarrow M=\dfrac{\tau}{B \sin \theta}=\dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \times 10^{-2} \sin 30^{\circ}}$
$=\dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \times 10^{-2} \times(1 / 2)}=1.2 {~A}-{m}^{2}$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Torque on a Current Carrying Loop in Uniform Magnetic Field

question: A circular coil of 10 turns and radius  $10{\text{ }}cm$  is placed in a uniform magnetic field of  $0.1{\text{ }}T$  normal to the plane of the coil. If the current in the coil is $5 A$, then the magnitude of the torque on the coil is
explanation:
Magnitude of torque on the coil is given as
$\tau=|\vec{m} \times \vec{B}|$
$\Rightarrow \tau=N i A B \sin \theta[\because m=N i A]$
where, $\theta$ is the angle between axis of loop (normal to the loop) and direction of magnetic field.
Here, $\theta=0$ So, $\tau=0$
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics

question: A 2 amp current is flowing through two different small circular copper coils having radii ratio $1: 2$. The ratio of their respective magnetic moments will be
explanation:
Magnetic moment of current carrying circular loop $=I A$
$M = IA \Rightarrow M \propto A\quad \quad [I - {\text{Same}}]$
$\dfrac{M_{1}}{M_{2}}=\dfrac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}=\dfrac{\pi r_{1}^{2}}{\pi r_{2}^{2}}=\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{4}$

question: A uniform magnetic field of $2 \times 10^{-3} T$ acts along positive $Y$-direction. A rectangular loop of sides $20\,cm$ and $10\,cm$ with current of  $5\,A$  is in $Y-Z$ plane. The current is in anti clockwise sense with reference to negative $X$ -axis. Magnitude and direction of the torque is
explanation:
Given: $\vec B = 2 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,T$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950731/preptracker/questions/q_43966_explanation_1764950731276.png
Sides of loop $ = 20\,cm$ and $10\,cm$
Area of loop, $A = 20 \times 10 = 200\,c{m^2}$
$ = 2 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,{m^2}$
Current, $I = 5\,A$
As current is in anticlockwise direction $i.\,e.$ negative
${\text{So}},{\text{ }}\vec \tau = - I(\vec A \times \vec B)$
$ = - 5\left( {2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}\hat i} \right) \times \left( {2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)\,\,\hat j$
$ = - 2 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,Nm$
$i.\,e.$ torque acts along negative $z$ direction.
$\therefore \vec \tau = 2 \times {10^{ - 4}}( - \hat k)Nm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Torque on a Current Carrying Loop in Uniform Magnetic Field

question: Calculate the magnetic moment (in ${{Am}^{2}}$ ) of a thin wire with a current ${I=8 {~A}}$, wound tightly on a half a tore (see figure). The diameter of the cross section of the tore is equal to ${d=5 {~cm}}$, and the number of turns is ${N=500}$.
explanation:
The magnetic moment of circular current is given by $A I, I$ being the circulating current and $A$ is the area of cross-section; the direction is perpendicular to the plane of current. Now, for an element of toroid of length $r d \theta$, its magnetic moment is along the direction of arrow as shown, of magnitude (perpendicular to its cross-section) $=$ current $\times$ area $\times$ number of turns in the length $r d \theta$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950639/preptracker/questions/q_43775_explanation_1764950636903.png
$dM = I\frac{{\pi {d^2}}}{4}\left( {\frac{N}{{\pi r}}r{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} d\theta } \right) = \frac{N}{4}{d^2}I{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} d\theta $
Resolving $d M$ into components along $x$ and $y$, we get $d M \sin \theta$ and $d M \cos \theta$; components along y from neighbouring elements cancel out to zero, and components along $x$ are added. So $M = \int d M\sin \theta $
$ = \int_0^\pi {\frac{N}{4}} \cdot {d^2}I\sin \theta d\theta = \frac{{N{d^2}I}}{2}$
putting the given values we get $M=5 {Am}^{2}$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Dipole Moment of a Circular Current Carrying Loop

question: Figure shows a coil bent with all edges of length 2 $m$ and carrying a current of 2 $A$ . There exists in space a uniform magnetic field of 2 $T$ in positive ${y}$-direction. Find the torque on the loop.
explanation:
We assume equal and opposite currents in wires $P Q$ and $R S$, then we split the given loop in three independent square loops and find magnetic moment vector of the loop which is only due to the square coil $P Q R S$ as top and bottom square coils magnetic moments are equal and opposite so will cancel each other. Thus magnetic moment of the given loop is given as $\vec{M}=I a^{2} \hat{j}=2 \times(2)^{2} \hat{j}=8 \hat{j}$
The magnetic induction vector in space is given as $\vec{B}=2 \hat{j}$
The torque on the given loop is given as $\vec{\tau}=\vec{M} \times \vec{B}=0$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Torque on a Current Carrying Loop in Uniform Magnetic Field

question: As shown in the figure, three sided frame is pivoted at ${P}$ and ${Q}$ hangs vertically. Its sides are of same length and have a linear density of ${\sqrt{3} {~kg} / {m}}$. A current of ${10 \sqrt{3} {~A}}$ is sent through the frame, which is in a uniform magnetic field of $2\,T$ directed upwards as shown in the figure. Then, angle through which the frame will be deflected in equilibrium is ${\dfrac{\pi}{k}}$.   Find  the value of ${k}$ is    (Given, ${g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$)
explanation:
Torque of magnetic force about $PQ,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
${\tau _m} = (ILB)L\cos \theta = IL2B\cos \theta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Torque of gravitational force about ${P Q}$,
${\tau _g} = [(\lambda L)gL\sin \theta + 2(\lambda L)g(1/2)L\sin \theta ]$
$ = 2\lambda {L^2}g\sin \theta $
${\tau _m} = {\tau _g}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \tan \theta = \frac{{IB}}{{2\lambda g}} = \frac{{10\sqrt 3 \times 2}}{{2 \times \sqrt 3 \times 10}} = 1$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \theta = 45^\circ $
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} k = 4$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Torque on a Current Carrying Loop in Uniform Magnetic Field

question: A current carrying small circular loop is placed at a distance $2\,cm$ from an infinitely long current conductor as shown in the figure. Wire and loop lie in same plane. The loop has 100 turns, its effective radius is $2\,cm$. The torque acting on the loop in the given situation is $x{\mu _0}.$   Find  the value of $x$
explanation:
Magnetic field at a distance ${(r=2 {~cm})}$
${B_{0}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 \pi r}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \times 2}{2 \pi \times 2 \times 10^{-2}}=\dfrac{50 \mu_{0}}{\pi} {T}}$
Magnetic moment of the current carrying loop $ = M = i\,AN$
$ = 2 \times \pi \times {\left( {2 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}} \right)^2} \times 100$
$ = 2 \times \pi \times 4 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times 100$
$ = 8 \times {10^{ - 2}}\pi $
${\Rightarrow}$ Torque acting on the loop
$ = |\tau | = |M \times B| = |M||B|\sin \theta $
$ = M{B_0}\sin 90^\circ = M{B_0}$
Or $\tau = 8 \times {10^{ - 2}}\pi \times \frac{{50{\mu _0}}}{\pi } = 400 \times {10^{ - 2}}{\mu _0} = 4\,{\mu _0}$
Thus, value of ${x=4}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Torque on a Current Carrying Loop in Non Uniform Magnetic Field

question: A current $I$ flows in a rectangulary shaped wire whose center lies at $\left( {{x_0},0,0} \right)$ and whose vertices are located at the points $A\left( {{x_0} + d, - a, - b} \right)$, $B\left( {{x_0} - d,a, - b} \right),$$C\left( {{x_0} - d,a,b} \right),$and $D\left( {{x_0} + d, - a,b} \right)$ respectively. Assume that $a,b,d <  < {x_0}$. What is the magnitude of magnetic dipole vector of the rectangular wire frame? (Given :  $b = 10\,m,$  $d = 12\,m,$ $a = 5\,m,$ $I = 0.03\,A\,)$
explanation:
Magnetic moment of a current carrying loop,
$\vec m = I\overrightarrow S $ [where $\overrightarrow{{S}}$ : area vector]
Area of loop,
$\overrightarrow{{S}}=\overrightarrow{{AB}} \times \overrightarrow{{BC}}$
Here, $\overrightarrow{A B}=(-2 d \hat{i})+2 a \hat{j}, \overrightarrow{B C}=2 b \,\hat{k}$
$\therefore \quad \overrightarrow S = \left[ \begin{gathered} \left( {{B_x} - {A_x}} \right)\hat i + \left( {{B_y} - {A_y}} \right)\hat j + \hfill \\ \left( {{B_z} - {A_z}} \right)\hat k \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]$
$\times\left[\left(C_{x}-B_{x}\right) \hat{i}+\left(C_{y}-B_{y}\right) \hat{j}+\left(C_{z}-B_{z}\right) \hat{k}\right]$
$\left.=\left[x_{0}-d-x_{0}-d\right] \hat{i}+(a+a) \hat{j}+(-b+b) \hat{k}\right]$
$\times\left[\left(x_{0}-d-x_{0}+d\right) \hat{i}+(a-a) \hat{j}+(b+b) \hat{k}\right]$
$\therefore \quad \overrightarrow{{s}}=(-2 d \hat{i}+2 {a} \hat{{j}}) \times(2 {~b} \hat{{k}})=4 {bd} \hat{{j}}+4 a b \hat{i}$
$|\overrightarrow m | = I|\overrightarrow S |$
$ = 4\,Ib\sqrt {{a^2} + {d^2}} $
$ = 4 \times (0.03)\,(10)\sqrt {{{12}^2} + {5^2}} $
$ = 4 \times 0.3 \times 13 = 15.6\,J{T^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Dipole Moment of a Circular Current Carrying Loop

question: The magnetic moments associated with two closely wound circular coils $A$ and $B$ of radius ${r_A} = 10\,cm$ and ${r_B} = 20\,cm$ respectively are equal if (Where $N_{A}, I_{A}$ and $N_{B}, I_{B}$ are number of turn and current of $A$ and $B$ respectively)
explanation:
Magnetic moment of the current carrying loop is
$M=N I A$
$M \propto R^{2}$
$\left(\because A=\pi R^{2}\right)$
Here, $\dfrac{R_{A}}{R_{B}}=\dfrac{1}{2}$.
Therefore, $\dfrac{M_{A}}{M_{B}}=\dfrac{R_{A}^{2}}{R_{B}^{2}}=\dfrac{1}{4}=1$

explanation: $M = niA,\,\,A = \pi {r^2}$ Doubling the radius $(r)$ , magnetic moment $M$ of a current loop 	increases 	its magnetic moment fourfold, as the 	magnetic dipole moment is directly proportional to the product of the current $(i)$ and the loop's area $(A)$. So correct option is (3).

question:
Assertion: A small coil carrying current, in equilibrium, is perpendicular to the direction of the uniform magnetic field.

Reason: Torque is maximum when plane of coil and direction of the magnetic fields is parallel to each other.
explanation:
When $\vec{M}$ and $\vec{B}$ are in same direction. Ifcoil is slightly rotated torque $\vec{\tau}=\vec{M} \times \vec{B}$ will restore it to original position. It is stable equilibrium when $\vec{M}$ and $\vec{B}$ make certain angle, $\tau$ will be $\tau=M B \sin \theta$.
If $\theta=90^{\circ}, \Rightarrow \tau_{\max }=M B$
So correct option is (2).

question:
Assertion: When radius of circular loop carrying current is doubled its magnetic moment becomes four times.

Reason: Magnetic moment is inversely proportional to on area of the loop.
explanation:
When the radius $(r)$ of a circular loop is doubled, its magnetic moment $(M)$ becomes four times the original value. Since $M=N I A$
$N=1$ (for one loop) and $A=\pi r^{2}$
For given $N$ and $I$, we have $M \propto A$.
So correct option is (3).

question:
The magnetic field at the centre of a current carrying circular coil of area '$A$' is '$B$'. The magnetic moment of the coil is
[ $\mu_{0}=$ permeability of free space.]
explanation:
Let ' $r$ ' be the radius of the circular loop.
$\therefore A=\pi r^{2} \quad$ or $\quad r=\sqrt{\dfrac{A}{\pi}}$
Magnetic field at the centre of the loop is,
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \sqrt{\dfrac{A}{\pi}}} \Rightarrow I=\dfrac{2 B}{\mu_{0}} \sqrt{\dfrac{A}{\pi}}$
The magnetic moment of the loop is,
$M=I A=\dfrac{2 B A}{\mu_{0}} \sqrt{\dfrac{A}{\pi}}=\dfrac{2 B A^{3 / 2}}{\mu_{0} \pi^{1 / 2}}$

question:
Assertion: Torque on the coil is the maximum when coil is suspended in a radial magnetic field.

Reason: The torque tends to rotate the coil on its own axis.
explanation:
The torque on a coil is maximum when the coil is suspended in a radial magnetic field. This is correct. When a current-carrying coil is placed in a magnetic field, the torque it experiences is given by $\tau=N \cdot B \cdot i \cdot A \cdot \sin \theta$, where $\mathrm{N}$ is the number of turns in the coil, $B$ is the magnetic field strength, $i$ is the current, $A$ is the area of the coil, and è is the angle between the magnetic field and the plane of the coil. The torque is maximized when $\theta=90^{\circ}$, meaning the plane of the coil is parallel to the radial magnetic field.
The torque tends to rotate the coil on its own axis. This is generally true, but it doesn't directly explain why the torque is maximum in a radial magnetic field. While torque does tend to rotate the coil, this doesn't serve as a direct explanation for why the torque is maximum in a radial magnetic field.
Therefore, both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason does not provide the correct explanation for the Assertion, making the correct answer (2).

question: A coil in the shape of an equilateral triangle of side $0.02\,m$ is suspended from its vertex such that it is hanging in a vertical plane between the pole pieces of permanent magnet producing a uniform field of $5 \times 10^{-2} T$. If a current of $0.1\,A$ is passed through the coil, what is the couple acting?
explanation:
Torque, $\tau=i A B \sin \theta$,
$i=0.1 A, \theta=90^{\circ}$
$A=\dfrac{1}{2} \times$ base $\times$ height
or $A=\dfrac{1}{2} a \times \dfrac{a \sqrt{3}}{2}$
$=\dfrac{\sqrt{3} a^{2}}{4}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3} \times(0.02)^{2}}{4}$
$=\sqrt{3} \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{2} ; \theta=90^{\circ}$
$\tau=0.1 \times \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10^{-2} \sin 90^{\circ}$
$ = 5\sqrt 3 \times {10^{ - 7}}\,N\,{\rm{ - }}\,m$
So, correct option is (1)

question:
For a current-carrying wire loop of \[N\]-turns, placed in region of a uniform magnetic field \(B\), match Column I and Column II
A Torque on loop
B Torque on loop when $\overrightarrow M $
C Magnetic moment of loop
D Torque on loop when $\overrightarrow M $ is perpendicular to $\overrightarrow B $
P $MB$
Q $NIA$
R Zero
S $\overrightarrow M \times \overrightarrow B $
explanation:
$A$. Torque on loop, $\tau=\vec{M} \times \vec{B}$ where, $\vec{M}$ is magnetic moment of a current-carrying loop and $B$ is magnetic field $B$. Torque on loop when $M$ is either parallel or anti-parallel to $B$ is zero.
$C$. Magnetic moment of loop $M=N I A$, where $I$ is current and $A$ is area of cross-section of wire.
$D$. Torque on loop when $\vec{M}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{B}$ is $\vec{\tau}=\vec{M} \times \vec{B}=M B \sin 90^{\circ}=M B$. So option (3) is correct.

options.text: A - P, B - Q, C - E, D - S
A - Q, B - P, C - S, D - R
A - S, B - R, C - Q, D - P
A - P, B - R, C - S, D - Q

question: A wire of length $l$ is bent in the form of circular coil of some turns. A current, i flows through the coil. The coil is placed in a uniform magnetic filed, B. The maximum torque on the coil can be
explanation:
Let, $r$ be the radius of the coil and $n$ be the number of turns formed. Then
$l = 2\pi rn \Rightarrow r = \frac{1}{{2\pi n}}\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Maximum torque, $\tau_{\max }=B n i A=B n i \pi r^{2}$
$=B n i \pi \times \dfrac{l^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} n^{2}}=\dfrac{i B l^{2}}{4 \pi n}$
Torque will be maximum if $n = 1$
$\therefore \tau_{\max }=\dfrac{B i l^{2}}{4 \pi}$
subject: Physics

question: Two wires of same length are shaped into a square and a circle. If they carry same current, ratio of the effective magnetic moment will be
explanation:
Suppose length of each wire is $l$.
$A_{\text {square }}=\left(\dfrac{l}{4}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{l^{2}}{16}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950338/preptracker/questions/q_42037_explanation_1764950338307.png https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950339/preptracker/questions/q_42037_explanation_1764950339169.png $A_{\text {cirde }}=\pi r^{2}=\pi\left(\dfrac{l}{2 \pi}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{l^{2}}{4 \pi}$
$\because$ Magnetic moment $M = iA$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{M_{\text {square }}}{M_{\text {circle }}}=\dfrac{A_{\text {square }}}{A_{\text {circle }}}=\dfrac{l^{2} / 16}{l^{2} / 4 \pi}=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$

question: In the figure shown, a coil of single turn is wound on a sphere of radius $r$ and mass $m$. The plane of the coil is parallel to the inclined plane and lies in the equatorial plane of the sphere. If the sphere is in rotational equlibrium, the value of $B$ is (current in the coil is $i$ )
explanation:
The gravitational torque must be counter balanced by the magnetic about $O$, for equilibrium of the sphere.
The gravitational torque $ = {\tau _{gr}} = mg \times r\sin \theta $
$ \Rightarrow {\tau _{gr}} = mgr\sin \theta $
The magnetic torque,${\tau _m} = M \times B$
Magnetic torque on the coil does not depends on point of measurement.
where the magnetic moment of the coil $M = \left( {i\pi {r^2}} \right)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950337/preptracker/questions/q_42033_explanation_1764950336870.png $ \Rightarrow {\tau _m} = \pi i{r^2}\,B\sin \theta $
$ \Rightarrow \pi i{r^2}\,B\sin \theta = mgr\sin \theta $
or $B = mg/\pi ir$

question: A coil area $100\,c{m^2}$ having 500 turns carries a current of 1$mA$. It is suspended in a uniform magnetic field of induction ${10^{ - 3}}\,Wb/{m^2}$. Its plane makes an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the lines of induction. The torque acting on the coil is
explanation:
Given, $i = 1\,mA = {10^{ - 3}}\,A;$
$\tau = ?A = 100\,c{m^2} = 100 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,{m^2}$
Couple acting on the coil is given by
$\tau=B i A N \sin \phi$
Where $\phi$ is angle made by normal to the plane of coil with B. $\phi=30^{\circ}$
$\therefore C = {10^{ - 3}} \times {10^{ - 3}} \times 100 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times 500 \times \sin 30$
$ = 250 \times {10^{ - 8}}Nm$

question: A straight conductor of mass $m$ and carrying a current $'i'$ is hinged at one end and placed in a plane perpendicular to the magnetic field of intensity $B$ as shown in the figure. At any moment if the conductor is left free, then the angular acceleration of the conductor will be (Assume gravity free region)
explanation:
The force acting on the elementary portion of the current carrying conductor is given as,
$dF = i(dr)B\sin {90^o} \Rightarrow dF = iB\,dr$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950321/preptracker/questions/q_41930_explanation_1764950320973.png The torque applied by $dr$ about $Od\tau = rdF$
$\Rightarrow$ The total torque about
$O = \tau = \int r (iBdr)$
$\Rightarrow \tau=i B \int_{0}^{l} r d r=\dfrac{i B l^{2}}{2}=I \alpha$
The angular acceleration,
$ \Rightarrow \alpha = \left( {\frac{{iB{{\text{l}}^2}}}{2}} \right)/\left( {\frac{{m{{\text{l}}^2}}}{3}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha = \frac{{3iB}}{{2m}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on Torque and Magnetic Dipole

question: Three point charges each of mass $m$ and charge $q$, are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side ' $a$ '. $G$ is the centroid. The system is rotated with a constant angular frequency $\omega$ about an axis passing through the centroid and perpendicular to the plane of triangle. The ratio of magnetic dipole moment to angular momentum of the system will be :
explanation:
Magnetic dipole moment,
$\mu = IA = \frac{{3q\omega }}{{2\pi }} \times \pi \times {\left( {\frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right)^2} = \frac{{q\omega {a^2}}}{2}$
Angular momentum,
$L = 3m{\left( {\frac{{\text{a}}}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right)^2}\omega = m{{\text{a}}^2}\omega $
$\therefore \frac{\mu }{L} = \frac{{q\omega {{\text{a}}^2}}}{{2m\omega {{\text{a}}^2}}} = \frac{q}{{2m}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Dipole Moment of a Rotating Charge

question: The rectangular coil having 100 turns is present in a uniform magnetic field of $\dfrac{0.05}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{\mathrm{j}}$ as shown in the figure. The torque acting on the loop is
explanation:
The magnetic dipole moment of the current carrying coil is given by
$\bar M = NiA\hat n$
$ = 100 \times 0.5(0.08) \times 0.044\hat i$
$ = 16 \times {10^{ - 2}}(\hat i)A{m^2}$
The torque acting on the coil is
$\tau = M \times B = MB(\hat i \times \hat j)$
$ = 16 \times {10^{ - 2}} \times \frac{{0.05}}{{\sqrt 2 }}\hat k$
$ = 5.66 \times {10^{ - 5}}(Nm)\hat k$
subject: Physics

question: A circular coil of 16 turns and radius 10$cm$ carrying a current of 0.75$A$ rests with its plane normal to an external field of magnitude $5.0 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,T$. The coil is free to turn about an axis in its plane perpendicular to the field direction. When the coil is turned slightly and released, it oscillates about its stable equilibrium with a frequency of $2.0\,{s^{ - 1}}$. The moment of inertia of the coil about its axis of rotation is (in $kg - {m^2}$)
explanation:
The frequency of oscillation of freely suspended magnet in magnetic field is
$\begin{aligned}& v=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{M B}{I}} \\& M=N i A=N i\left(\pi r^{2}\right)\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad I=\dfrac{M B}{4 \pi^{2} n^{2}}=\dfrac{N i \pi r^{2} B}{4 \pi^{2} n^{2}}$
$I=\dfrac{N i r^{2}}{4 \pi n}=1.19 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~kg} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{2}$

question: In a hydrogen atom, an electron of charge e revolves in a orbit of radius $r$ with speed $v$. Then, magnetic moment associated with electron is
explanation:
Magnetic moment of revolving electron,
$\mu_{e}=i \times A$
Where, $i=$ current and
$A=$ area
$\therefore \mu_{e}=\dfrac{e}{T} \times \pi r^{2}$
(As current, $i=\dfrac{\text { charge }}{\text { time }}=\dfrac{e}{T}$ )
Where, $\mathrm{T}$ = time period of revolvtion
and $\quad r=$ radius of orbit
$\mu_{e}=\dfrac{e}{2 \pi r} \times v \times \pi r^{2}$
$\because $ Time period ($T$)$\frac{{{\text{distance}}}}{{{\text{speed}}}}$$ = \frac{{2\pi r}}{v}$
$\Rightarrow \mu_{e}=\dfrac{e v r}{2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Dipole Moment of a Rotating Charge

explanation:

$\tau=M \times B$
where,$M=$ magnetic moment $=niA$
$\therefore \tau=n i(A \times B)$
where, $n=$ number of turns
$i=$ current flowing in coil
and $A=$ area of coil

question:
A gyromagnetic ratio of the electron revolving in a circular orbit of hydrogen atom is $8.8 \times {10^{10}}Ck{g^{ - 1}}$. What is the mass of the electron? Given charge of the electron
$ = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}C$.
explanation:
Gyromagnetic ratio of an electron
$ = \frac{e}{{2\,{m_e}}} = 8.8 \times {10^{10}}Ck{g^{ - 1}}$
$\therefore$ Mass of the electron,
${m_e} = \frac{e}{{2 \times 8.8 \times {{10}^{10}}Ck{g^{ - 1}}}} = \frac{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}C}}{{2 \times 8.8 \times {{10}^{10}}Ck{g^{ - 1}}}}$
$ = {0.0909^\prime }{10^{ - 29}}\,kg = \frac{1}{{11}} \times {10^{ - 29}}\,kg$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Dipole Moment of a Rotating Charge

question: An electron ($e$) is revolving in a circular orbit of radius $r$ in hydrogen atom. The angular momentum of the electron is ($M=$ magnetic dipole moment associated with it and $m=$ mass of electron)
explanation:
The angular momentum of electron is given as
$L=m v r=m(r \omega) \cdot r=m \omega r^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \omega r^{2}=\dfrac{L}{m}$
Magnetic dipole moment,
$\begin{aligned}& M=i A=\dfrac{e}{T} \cdot \pi r^{2}=\dfrac{e}{(2 \pi / \omega)} \pi r^{2}=\dfrac{e}{2} \omega r^{2} \\& \Rightarrow M=\dfrac{e}{2} \dfrac{L}{m} \quad \text { [ from eq.(1)] } \\& \Rightarrow L=\dfrac{2 m M}{e}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Dipole Moment of a Rotating Charge

question: The magnetic dipole moment of a current loop is independent of
explanation:

From the relation, we can conclude that magnetic dipole moment of a current loop is independent of magnetic field in which it is lying.

question: A coil carrying current $I$ has radius $r$ and number of turns $n$. It is rewound so that radius of new coil is $\dfrac{r}{4}$ and it carries current $I$. The ratio of magnetic moment of new coil to that of original coil is
explanation:
Magnetic moment of the original coil,
$M_{0}=n_{1} I A=n I\left(\pi r^{2}\right)$
Magnetic moment of the new coil,
$M_{n}=n_{2} I A=n_{2} I\left(\pi \dfrac{r^{2}}{16}\right)$
Equating length of wires we have,
$\begin{aligned}& n_{1} \times 2 \pi r_{1}=n_{2} \times 2 \pi r_{2} \\& \Rightarrow n \times 2 \pi r=n_{2} \times 2 \pi \dfrac{r}{4} \\& \Rightarrow n_{2}=4 n\end{aligned}$
Now from eq.(1) and (2), we get
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{M_{0}}{M_{n}}=\dfrac{n}{n_{2}} \times 16=\dfrac{1}{4} \times 16=4 \quad \because\left[n_{2}=4 n\right] \\& \dfrac{M_{n}}{M_{0}}=\dfrac{1}{4}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Dipole Moment of a Circular Current Carrying Loop

question: A circular coil and a square coil is prepared from two identical metal wires and a current is passed through it. Ratio of magnetic dipole moment associated with circular coil to that of square coil is
explanation:
Let $l$ be the length of metal wire.
When the wire is bent into a circular coil of radius $\mathrm{r}$, then
$r=\dfrac{l}{2 \pi}$
Therefore, Area ,
$A=\pi\left(\dfrac{l}{2 \pi}\right)^{2}=\pi \dfrac{l^{2}}{4 \pi^{2}}$
Therefore, magnetic dipole moment associated with circular coil
$\mu_{c}=i A=i \pi\left(\dfrac{l^{2}}{4 \pi^{2}}\right)$
$\mu_{c}=\left(\dfrac{i l^{2}}{4 \pi}\right)$
When metal wire is bent into a square coil then side of square
$a=\dfrac{l}{4}$
Therefore, Area
$A=a^{2}=\dfrac{l^{2}}{16}$
Therefore, magnetic dipole moment associated with square coil,
$\mu_{s}=i A=i \dfrac{l^{2}}{16}$
$\therefore \dfrac{\mu_{c}}{\mu_{s}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{i l^{2}}{4 \pi}}{\dfrac{i l^{2}}{16}}=\dfrac{16}{4 \pi}=\dfrac{4}{\pi}$
Hence, the ratio of magnetic dipole moment of circular coil and square coil is $4: \pi$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Dipole Moment of a Circular Current Carrying Loop

question: A circular coil of radius 10$cm$ and 100 turns carries a current 1$A$. What is the magnetic moment of the coil?
explanation:
Here,
Radius, $\mathrm{r}=10 \mathrm{~cm}=10 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$
Number of turns, $\mathrm{N}=100$ Current, $\mathrm{I}=1 \mathrm{~A}$
Area of the coil, $\mathrm{A}=\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}$
Magnetic moment of the coil,
$\begin{aligned}& M=N I A=N I \pi r^{2} \\& =100 \times 1 \times 3.142 \times\left(10 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2} \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~m}^{2} \\& =3.142 \mathrm{Am}^{2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Dipole Moment of a Circular Current Carrying Loop

question: A dielectric disc of radius $R$ carries a charge uniformly on its surface. The surface charge density is $\sigma$. The disc rotates about an axis perpendicular to its plane passing through the centre with angular velocity $\omega$. Find the torque on the disc if it is placed in a uniform magnetic field $b$ directed perpendicular to the rotation axis.
explanation:
Consider a circular ring of radius $r$ and of thickness $dr$ on the disc. Charge within this ring.
or
$dq = (\sigma )(2\pi rdr)$
$i = \sigma \omega rdr$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950214/preptracker/questions/q_41390_explanation_1764950214091.png Magnetic moment of this annual ring,
$M = iA = (\sigma \omega rdr)\left( {\pi {r^2}} \right)$ (along the axis of rotation).
Torque on this ring,
$d\tau = MB\sin 90^\circ = \sigma \pi \omega {r^3}dr$
$\therefore$ Total torque on the disc is,
$\tau = \int_0^R d \tau = (\sigma \pi \omega B)\int_0^R {{r^3}} dr = \frac{{\sigma \omega \pi B{R^4}}}{4}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Torque on a Current Carrying Loop in Uniform Magnetic Field

question: Current is flowing in a wire frame kept as shown in the figure where $EDCF$ is in $y-z$ plane and $A F C B$ is in $x-z$ plane. Current in the wire frame is $i$ and length of each side is $a$. The magnetic moment of loop is
explanation:
The given wire frame can be seen as composed of two rectangular wire frames where current $CF$ of loop $EDCF$ cancels current $FC$ in $AFCB$.
Magnetic moment $\overrightarrow M = \overrightarrow {{M_1}} + \overrightarrow {{M_2}} $
$\overrightarrow {{M_1}} $ is magnetic moment of $AFCB$
$\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{M_{1}}=-i a^{2} \hat{j}$
$\overrightarrow {{M_2}} $ is magnetic moment of $FEDC$
$\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{M_{2}}=-i a^{2} \hat{i}$ So, $\vec{M}=-i a^{2}(\hat{i}+\hat{j})$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950200/preptracker/questions/q_41345_explanation_1764950200385.png
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Dipole Moment of a Circular Current Carrying Loop

question: The dipole moment of a circular loop carrying a current $I$, is $m$ and the magnetic field at the centre of the loop is $B_{1}$. Where the dipole moment is doubled by keeping the current constant, the magnetic field at the centre of the loop is $B_{2}$. The ratio $\dfrac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}$ is:
explanation:
Dipole moment of circular loop is $m_{1}=I \cdot A=I . . \pi R^{2}\{\mathrm{R}=$ radius of the loop $\}$
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}$
when moment becomes double
$ \Rightarrow R$ becomes $\sqrt{2} R$ (keeping current constant)
$\begin{aligned}& m_{2}=I \cdot \pi(\sqrt{2} R)^{2}=2 \cdot I \pi R^{2}=2 m_{1} \\& B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2(\sqrt{2} R)}=\dfrac{B_{1}}{\sqrt{2}} \\& \dfrac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=\sqrt{2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Dipole Moment of a Circular Current Carrying Loop
